topics.title: Introduction of Non-Conventional Oil
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT752 - NON-CONVENTIONAL PETROLEUM RESOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction of Non-Conventional Oil

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Conventional Oil** refers to crude oil resources that cannot be extracted, refined, or produced using traditional primary recovery methods (natural flow or basic pumping) and standard surface processing. These hydrocarbons are characterized by their geological entrapment in low-permeability formations, their high viscosity, or their solid/semi-solid state in the reservoir, requiring advanced extraction technologies such as thermal stimulation, mining, hydraulic fracturing, or specialized upgrading.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PECHT752)**, Non-Conventional Oil includes a distinct family of hydrocarbon accumulations that differ from conventional crude oil in **three fundamental dimensions**: (1) **Mobility** (viscosity/API gravity), (2) **Reservoir Geology** (permeability/porosity), and (3) **Extraction Methodology** (thermal, mechanical, or chemical).

### 1.1.1 The KTU 2024 Classification Framework

The KTU 2024 syllabus broadly categorizes Non-Conventional Oil into the following sub-classes:

| Sub-Class | State in Reservoir | API Gravity (°API) | Viscosity (cP) | Primary Location |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Heavy Oil** | Liquid | 10 – 22.3 | 100 – 10,000 | Venezuela, Canada, California |
| **Extra-Heavy Oil** | Liquid | < 10 | > 10,000 | Orinoco Belt (Venezuela) |
| **Oil Sands / Bitumen** | Semi-solid | < 10 | > 50,000 | Alberta (Canada) |
| **Oil Shale** | Solid (kerogen) | N/A (solid) | N/A (solid) | Colorado (USA), Estonia |
| **Tight Oil (Shale Oil)** | Liquid in nano-pores | 30 – 50 | 0.5 – 5 | Bakken, Eagle Ford (USA) |
| **Coal-to-Liquid (CTL)** | Solid (coal) | N/A | N/A | South Africa, China |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Stuck Honey" Analogy:** Imagine a bottle of regular honey (conventional oil) versus a jar of crystallized, hardened honey stuck to the bottom of a glass jar (non-conventional oil). With conventional oil, you simply tilt the bottle and it pours out naturally — this is **primary recovery**. With the crystallized honey, you must heat the jar (thermal recovery), use a chemical solvent (in-situ upgrading), or physically scrape and dig it out (mining). Non-conventional oil behaves exactly like that crystallized honey — **Nature has made it stubbornly immobile**, demanding human ingenuity to liberate every drop.

Another useful analogy is the **"Sponge vs. Chalk" comparison**:
- **Conventional Oil Reservoir** = A wet, porous sponge sitting in a bowl. The oil flows out easily when squeezed.
- **Tight Oil Shale** = A piece of chalk. The oil is locked inside microscopic pores that don't communicate. You must **fracture the chalk** (hydraulic fracturing) to release the oil.
- **Oil Shale** = A rock that doesn't even contain liquid oil, only **kerogen** (a precursor solid). You must "cook" the rock to convert kerogen into liquid oil — analogous to converting raw dough into bread.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of "Non-Conventionality"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A hydrocarbon deposit is classified as "non-conventional" when it fails at least one of the three tests below. The KTU 2024 framework uses these tests as the primary diagnostic tool:

1. **Mobility Test:** Can the hydrocarbon flow at reservoir conditions without stimulation?
   - If **NO** → Non-conventional (e.g., bitumen, heavy oil, oil shale kerogen)
2. **Permeability Test:** Can the hydrocarbon migrate through the rock matrix to a wellbore?
   - If **NO** → Non-conventional (e.g., tight oil, shale oil)
3. **Phase Test:** Does the hydrocarbon exist in the reservoir as a producible liquid?
   - If **NO** (solid kerogen) → Non-conventional (e.g., oil shale)

## 1.4 Global Significance & Strategic Importance

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Topic Matters for KTU Examiners:** Conventional oil reserves are declining at approximately **4–6% per year** globally. Non-conventional oil reserves are estimated at **6–9 trillion barrels** of oil equivalent — roughly **three times** the remaining conventional reserves (~1.7 trillion barrels). The International Energy Agency (IEA) estimates non-conventional oil could supply **15–20% of global liquid fuel demand** by 2050.

### Key Global Statistics (KTU 2024 Reference Data)

- **Total Global Oil Resources:** ~9–13 trillion barrels (all categories combined)
- **Recoverable Non-Conventional Oil:** ~1.5–3.5 trillion barrels
- **Largest Oil Sand Deposits:** Alberta, Canada (~167 billion barrels proven reserves)
- **Largest Oil Shale Deposits:** Green River Formation, USA (~1.5 trillion barrels of shale oil in place)
- **Largest Heavy Oil Province:** Orinoco Belt, Venezuela (~235 billion barrels extra-heavy oil)

## 1.5 Conventional vs. Non-Conventional Oil: The Definitional Contrast

| Parameter | Conventional Oil | Non-Conventional Oil |
|---|---|---|
| **API Gravity** | > 22.3° (light/medium) | < 22.3° (heavy/bitumen) or solid |
| **Viscosity at reservoir** | < 100 cP | > 100 cP (often > 10,000 cP) |
| **Permeability** | > 1 mD (often 50–1000 mD) | < 0.1 mD (often < 0.001 mD) |
| **Primary Recovery** | Natural drive, pumps | Thermal, mining, fracturing |
| **Recovery Factor** | 30–50% | 5–20% (often < 10% in primary) |
| **Hydrogen-to-Carbon Ratio** | High (1.8–2.0) | Low (1.3–1.6) |
| **Sulfur Content** | 0.1–2 wt% | 2–6 wt% (often > 5%) |
| **Metals (Ni, V)** | Trace (< 50 ppm) | High (200–1000+ ppm) |
| **Refining Complexity** | Standard atmospheric/vacuum distillation | Requires upgrading (coking, hydrocracking) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation of Non-Conventionality

The fundamental physics governing why a hydrocarbon is "non-conventional" can be distilled into **three governing equations** that the KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to internalize. These equations form the analytical backbone of the entire PECHT752 course.

### 2.1.1 Equation 1: Darcy's Law and the Mobility Constraint

Darcy's Law governs fluid flow through porous media:

$$Q = \frac{k \cdot A}{\mu} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

Where the variables and their typical ranges for non-conventional systems are:

- $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate (m³/day)
- $k$ = Absolute permeability of rock (mD or md) — for tight oil, this can be **0.0001 to 0.1 mD**
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of flow (m²)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of the oil (cP) — for bitumen, this can exceed **1,000,000 cP**
- $\Delta P$ = Pressure differential (psi or kPa)
- $L$ = Length of flow path (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Mobility Ratio (M):** For non-conventional systems, the mobility ratio is the most diagnostic parameter:
>
> $$M = \frac{k_{ro}}{\mu_o} \cdot \frac{\mu_w}{k_{rw}}$$
>
> When $M \gg 1$ (very low oil mobility), the oil is essentially immobile under normal conditions. This is the mathematical signature of non-conventionality.

### 2.1.2 Equation 2: API Gravity and Viscosity Correlation

The relationship between API gravity and kinematic viscosity is foundational to classifying non-conventional oils:

$$°API = \frac{141.5}{\rho_{oil}} - 131.5$$

Where $\rho_{oil}$ is the specific gravity of oil at **15.6 °C (60 °F)** relative to water at the same temperature.

A widely used correlation for dead oil viscosity (Beggs & Robinson):

$$\mu_{od} = 10^{10^{(10^{0.43 + 0.79 \log_{10}(°API) - 0.0015 \cdot T})}) - 1$$

Where:
- $\mu_{od}$ = Dead oil viscosity (cP)
- $°API$ = API gravity
- $T$ = Temperature (°F)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Threshold:** When °API drops below 10 and viscosity exceeds **10,000 cP**, the oil transitions from "heavy oil" to "extra-heavy oil/bitumen" — a key KTU 2024 classification boundary.

### 2.1.3 Equation 3: Thermal Recovery and Viscosity Reduction

The Arrhenius-type relationship governing thermal viscosity reduction is the operational principle behind Steam-Assisted Gravity Drainage (SAGD), Cyclic Steam Stimulation (CSS), and Steam Flooding:

$$\mu(T) = \mu_0 \cdot \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

Where:
- $\mu(T)$ = Viscosity at temperature $T$ (cP)
- $\mu_0$ = Reference viscosity at temperature $T_0$ (cP)
- $E_a$ = Activation energy of flow (typically **10,000 – 25,000 cal/mol** for heavy oils)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **1.987 cal/(mol·K)**
- $T, T_0$ = Absolute temperatures (K)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every high-yield equation for the KTU 2024 Module 1 examination. Memorize the physical meaning, units, and boundary conditions of each.**

| # | Formula | Application / Physical Meaning | Key Variables & Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \frac{kA}{\mu} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L}$ | Darcy's Law — governs oil flow rate | $k$ (mD), $\mu$ (cP), $\Delta P$ (psi) | Valid for laminar flow; fails in nano-pores of shale |
| 2 | $M = \frac{k_{ro}}{\mu_o} \div \frac{k_{rw}}{\mu_w}$ | Mobility ratio for displacement efficiency | All dimensionless | $M \gg 1$ indicates non-conventionality |
| 3 | $°API = \frac{141.5}{\rho_{oil}} - 131.5$ | API gravity classification | $\rho_{oil}$ dimensionless (60 °F/60 °F) | Light: > 31.1, Medium: 22.3–31.1, Heavy: < 22.3 |
| 4 | $\mu(T) = \mu_0 \cdot e^{E_a/R \cdot (1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | Thermal viscosity reduction (Arrhenius) | $\mu$ in cP, $T$ in K, $E_a$ in cal/mol | Valid for 10–300 °C range |
| 5 | $RF = \frac{N_p}{N}$ | Recovery Factor (overall) | $N_p$ produced, $N$ in place | Heavy oil RF ≈ 5–20% primary; up to 60% thermal |
| 6 | $S_o + S_w + S_g = 1$ | Saturation constraint (volume balance) | All saturations dimensionless | $0 \leq S \leq 1$ for each phase |
| 7 | $\phi = \frac{V_{pores}}{V_{bulk}}$ | Porosity of reservoir rock | Dimensionless | Oil shale: 4–15%, Sandstone: 15–30% |
| 8 | $k = k_0 \cdot \phi^3 \cdot S_{w}^{-n}$ | Permeability scaling (Kozeny-Carman type) | $n \approx 2$ for sandstones | Tight oil: $k < 0.1$ mD |
| 9 | $\rho_{oil} = \frac{141.5}{°API + 131.5}$ | Reverse API conversion | kg/m³ or specific gravity | Used to convert °API to density |
| 10 | $E_{thermal} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Energy required for steam injection | $E$ in kJ, $c_p \approx 4.2$ kJ/kg·K | Typical CSS: 200–400 m³ steam per m³ oil |

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility of These Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **How KTU 2024 Examiners Frame Real-World Application Questions:**

- **Darcy's Law** is used to design well spacing in heavy oil fields. In the **Orinoco Belt (Venezuela)**, well spacing is 200–400 m due to the very low mobility of extra-heavy oil.
- **API gravity correlations** determine the **selling price per barrel**: every 1° increase in API gravity roughly increases the price by **$0.50–$1.50/barrel** in international markets.
- **The Arrhenius equation** is the design basis for **steam injection projects**. For example, in the **Cold Lake field (Canada)**, injecting steam at 300 °C reduces bitumen viscosity from > 100,000 cP to < 10 cP, enabling gravity drainage.
- **Recovery Factor** governs project economics: an oil sands mining project with 90% recovery factor is economically viable only if the oil price is > **$50/barrel**.

## 2.4 The KTU 2024 Classification Decision Tree

The following decision logic is what examiners use to award full marks on "classification" questions:

1. **Is the hydrocarbon a solid at reservoir conditions?**
   - YES → **Oil Shale** (kerogen) or **Coal** (for CTL)
   - NO → Go to step 2.
2. **Is the viscosity at reservoir conditions > 10,000 cP?**
   - YES → **Bitumen / Extra-Heavy Oil**
   - NO → Go to step 3.
3. **Is the viscosity at reservoir conditions between 100 and 10,000 cP?**
   - YES → **Heavy Oil**
   - NO → Go to step 4.
4. **Is the rock permeability < 0.1 mD?**
   - YES → **Tight Oil / Shale Oil**
   - NO → **Medium / Light Oil** (potentially conventional)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Converting API Gravity to Density and Back

This is a routine 3-mark problem in KTU 2024 examinations. The student must demonstrate bidirectional fluency.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: API to Density

**Given:** A heavy oil sample from the Orinoco Belt has an API gravity of **8.5 °API**. Calculate its specific gravity and density in **kg/m³**.

**Step 1 — Recall the API gravity definition:**

The American Petroleum Institute (API) defined API gravity as a hyperbolic function of density to make crude oils lighter than water have positive °API values:

$$°API = \frac{141.5}{\rho_{oil}} - 131.5$$

Where $\rho_{oil}$ is the specific gravity relative to water at 60 °F (15.6 °C).

**Step 2 — Algebraic rearrangement to solve for $\rho_{oil}$:**

Add 131.5 to both sides:

$$°API + 131.5 = \frac{141.5}{\rho_{oil}}$$

Invert both sides:

$$\rho_{oil} = \frac{141.5}{°API + 131.5}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the given value (°API = 8.5):**

$$\rho_{oil} = \frac{141.5}{8.5 + 131.5} = \frac{141.5}{140.0}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$\rho_{oil} = 1.0107 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to absolute density in SI units (kg/m³):**

The density of water at 15.6 °C is $\rho_w = 999.0$ kg/m³ (often taken as 1000 kg/m³ in KTU textbooks):

$$\rho_{oil,abs} = \rho_{oil} \cdot \rho_w = 1.0107 \times 999.0 = 1009.7 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Since the oil is denser than water, it should sink. Indeed, 1009.7 kg/m³ > 999.0 kg/m³, confirming our result is physically correct. Bitumen with °API < 10 is denser than water and would sink to the bottom of a tank — a key reason why oil sands bitumen cannot be separated by simple gravity settling without dilution with lighter hydrocarbons (e.g., diluent or naphtha).

### 3.1.2 Reverse Verification

**Given:** Density of 1010 kg/m³. Verify the °API.

**Step 1:** Convert to specific gravity:

$$\rho_{oil} = \frac{1010}{999.0} = 1.01101$$

**Step 2:** Apply the API formula:

$$°API = \frac{141.5}{1.01101} - 131.5$$

**Step 3:** Compute:

$$°API = 139.96 - 131.5 = 8.46 \text{ °API}$$

**Step 4 — Round-off check:** The result (8.46) matches our input (8.5) within rounding tolerance. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Thermal Viscosity Reduction for SAGD Design

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark question):**
A Canadian oil sands operator plans a Steam-Assisted Gravity Drainage (SAGD) project. The bitumen at reservoir temperature (T₀ = 10 °C = 283.15 K) has viscosity μ₀ = **2,000,000 cP**. The activation energy of flow for this bitumen is Eₐ = **18,000 cal/mol**. If steam is injected to raise the reservoir temperature to T = 220 °C = 493.15 K, calculate:
(a) The reduced viscosity at the operating temperature.
(b) The percentage reduction in viscosity.
(c) Comment on the operational significance for SAGD feasibility.

### 3.2.1 Part (a): Calculating Reduced Viscosity

**Step 1 — Recall the Arrhenius equation:**

$$\mu(T) = \mu_0 \cdot \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values:**

$$\mu(T) = 2{,}000{,}000 \cdot \exp\left[\frac{18{,}000}{1.987}\left(\frac{1}{493.15} - \frac{1}{283.15}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Compute the term $\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}$:**

$$\frac{1}{493.15} = 0.002028 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{283.15} = 0.003532 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{493.15} - \frac{1}{283.15} = 0.002028 - 0.003532 = -0.001504 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the exponential argument:**

$$\frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right) = \frac{18{,}000}{1.987} \cdot (-0.001504)$$

$$\frac{18{,}000}{1.987} = 9058.9 \text{ K}$$

$$9058.9 \times (-0.001504) = -13.625$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the exponential:**

$$\exp(-13.625) = 1.211 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 6 — Final multiplication:**

$$\mu(T) = 2{,}000{,}000 \times 1.211 \times 10^{-6} = 2.42 \text{ cP}$$

### 3.2.2 Part (b): Percentage Reduction in Viscosity

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{\mu_0 - \mu(T)}{\mu_0} \times 100\%$$

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000 - 2.42}{2{,}000{,}000} \times 100\% = 99.9999\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Observation:** A viscosity reduction from 2,000,000 cP (essentially a solid) to 2.42 cP (lighter than most conventional oils) is a **six-order-of-magnitude** reduction. This is precisely why SAGD is the most successful in-situ recovery method for Canadian oil sands — heat transforms immobile bitumen into mobile oil.

### 3.2.3 Part (c): Operational Significance for SAGD

- At 220 °C, the bitumen viscosity (2.42 cP) is **comparable to that of conventional light crude oil**, enabling it to flow by gravity drainage to the horizontal production well.
- This validates the **economic threshold** for SAGD: steam must be injected at temperatures above **200 °C** to achieve mobilities exceeding **1 cP**.
- The **steam-to-oil ratio (SOR)** for this project would typically be 2.5–4.0, with energy returned on energy invested (EROEI) of about 4–6.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Darcy Flow Rate for a Heavy Oil Well

**Problem Statement (typical KTU Part B question):**
A horizontal well in a heavy oil reservoir has the following properties:
- Permeability: $k = 500$ mD
- Cross-sectional area of flow: $A = 50$ m²
- Viscosity of oil at reservoir: $\mu = 500$ cP
- Pressure drawdown: $\Delta P = 1500$ psi
- Length of flow path: $L = 800$ m

Calculate the volumetric flow rate in **m³/day** and assess whether the well can produce at an economic rate of **50 m³/day** without thermal stimulation.

### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Unit Conversions (Crucial for KTU marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always convert all quantities to consistent units before substituting. The "Imperial" Darcy equation uses field units:

$$Q \text{ (bbl/day)} = \frac{k \text{ (mD)} \cdot A \text{ (ft}^2) \cdot \Delta P \text{ (psi)}}{1.127 \cdot \mu \text{ (cP)} \cdot L \text{ (ft)}}$$

**Given conversions:**
- $A = 50$ m² $\times$ 10.764 = 538.2 ft²
- $L = 800$ m $\times$ 3.281 = 2624.8 ft
- $\Delta P = 1500$ psi
- $k = 500$ mD
- $\mu = 500$ cP

### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Apply Field-Unit Darcy Equation

$$Q = \frac{500 \times 538.2 \times 1500}{1.127 \times 500 \times 2624.8}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 500 \times 538.2 \times 1500 = 403{,}650{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator:**

$$\text{Denominator} = 1.127 \times 500 \times 2624.8 = 1{,}478{,}978.4$$

**Step 5 — Division:**

$$Q = \frac{403{,}650{,}000}{1{,}478{,}978.4} = 272.95 \text{ bbl/day}$$

### 3.3.3 Step 6 — Convert to m³/day

Using 1 bbl = 0.159 m³:

$$Q = 272.95 \times 0.159 = 43.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

### 3.3.4 Step 7 — Economic Assessment

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The calculated flow rate (43.4 m³/day) is **below** the economic threshold of 50 m³/day. **The well cannot produce economically without thermal stimulation.** This justifies the application of **Steam Flooding** or **Cyclic Steam Stimulation (CSS)** to reduce viscosity and increase flow rate.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Problem-Solving Decision Map

| Problem Type | First Step | Key Equation | Common Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| Convert °API ↔ Density | Identify target unit, invert the formula | $°API = 141.5/\rho - 131.5$ | Forgetting to use specific gravity (not kg/m³) directly |
| Viscosity reduction with temperature | Compute $(1/T - 1/T_0)$ correctly | $\mu(T) = \mu_0 \cdot e^{E_a/R \cdot (1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | Sign error in the exponent (T > T₀ should give negative exponent) |
| Flow rate with Darcy's Law | Unit conversion to field units | $Q = kA\Delta P / (1.127 \mu L)$ | Mixing SI and field units |
| Mobility ratio check | Identify relative permeabilities | $M = (k_{ro}/\mu_o)/(k_{rw}/\mu_w)$ | Forgetting to invert the water term |
| Recovery factor analysis | Distinguish primary vs. EOR | $RF = N_p/N$ | Confusing STOIIP with recoverable reserves |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Non-Conventional Oil Resources

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete taxonomy of non-conventional oil as per the KTU 2024 syllabus. This is a **block-level functional architecture** designed to be board-examination friendly.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Non-Conventional Oil Resources] --> B[Liquid in Reservoir]
    A --> C[Solid in Reservoir]
    A --> D[Bound to Source Rock]

    B --> E[Heavy Oil]
    B --> F[Extra-Heavy Oil]
    B --> G[Bitumen]

    C --> H[Oil Shale Kerogen]

    D --> I[Tight Oil Shale Oil]
    D --> J[Coal to Liquid CTL]

    E --> E1[10 to 22.3 deg API]
    E --> E2[100 to 10000 cP]
    E --> E3[Cold Production CHOPS]

    F --> F1[Below 10 deg API]
    F --> F2[10000 to 50000 cP]
    F --> F3[Steam Flooding]

    G --> G1[Below 10 deg API]
    G --> G2[Above 50000 cP]
    G --> G3[Mining or SAGD]

    H --> H1[Solid Kerogen]
    H --> H2[Retorting or In-situ Pyrolysis]
    H --> H3[Above 350 deg C]

    I --> I1[Liquid in Nano-pores]
    I --> I2[Below 0.1 mD]
    I --> I3[Horizontal Drilling plus Hydraulic Fracturing]

    J --> J1[Synfuel from Coal]
    J --> J2[Fischer Tropsch Process]
    J --> J3[Sasol South Africa]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Recovery Method Selection Logic

The following diagram maps the decision logic for selecting the appropriate recovery method, mirroring what KTU 2024 examiners expect in 14-mark design questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Non-Conventional Oil Deposit Identified] --> Q1{Depth of Reservoir?}

    Q1 -->|Shallow less than 75 m| M1[Surface Mining]
    Q1 -->|Deep greater than 75 m| Q2{Viscosity at Reservoir?}

    M1 --> M1A[Bucket Wheel Excavation]
    M1 --> M1B[Hot Water Extraction]
    M1 --> M1C[90 percent Recovery Factor]

    Q2 -->|Greater than 50000 cP| M2[In-Situ Thermal]
    Q2 -->|100 to 50000 cP| Q3{Permeability?}

    M2 --> M2A[SAGD Steam Assisted Gravity Drainage]
    M2 --> M2B[CSS Cyclic Steam Stimulation]
    M2 --> M2C[ESAGD Expanding Solvent]

    Q3 -->|Greater than 1 Darcy| M3[Cold Production CHOPS]
    Q3 -->|Below 0.1 mD| M4[Unconventional EOR]

    M3 --> M3A[Sand Production with Oil]
    M3 --> M3B[Wormholes in Reservoir]

    M4 --> M4A[Horizontal Multistage Fracturing]
    M4 --> M4B[CO2 Huff and Puff]
    M4 --> M4C[Nano-pore Network Activation]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Non-Conventional Oil Value Chain

The following diagram illustrates the complete upstream-to-downstream value chain, helping students understand where each non-conventional resource enters the energy economy.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Reservoir In-Place Resource] --> R2[Extraction Phase]
    R2 --> R3[Primary Processing]
    R3 --> R4[Transportation]
    R4 --> R5[Upgrading or Refining]
    R5 --> R6[Marketable Synthetic Crude]

    R2 -. Steam Injection .-> R2
    R2 -. Hydraulic Fracturing .-> R2
    R2 -. Surface Mining .-> R2

    R3 -. De-sanding .-> R3
    R3 -. Water Removal .-> R3
    R3 -. Diluent Addition .-> R3

    R5 -. Coking .-> R5
    R5 -. Hydrocracking .-> R5
    R5 -. Hydrotreating .-> R5

    R6 --> M1[Refineries]
    R6 --> M2[Petrochemicals]
    R6 --> M3[Export Pipelines]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Representation: Comparative Reservoir Architecture

The following block diagram contrasts the geological architecture of conventional vs. non-conventional reservoirs, which is a high-yield topic for KTU 2024 short-answer questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CONV[Conventional Reservoir]
        C1[Impermeable Cap Rock] --> C2[Porous Sandstone]
        C2 --> C3[Oil Leg with API above 30]
        C3 --> C4[Water Leg Aquifer]
        C2 -. Natural Permeability .-> C2
    end

    subgraph NONCONV[Non-Conventional Reservoir]
        N1[Shale Source Rock acting as Reservoir] --> N2[Liquid Hydrocarbons in Nano-pores]
        N2 --> N3[Bound to Kerogen or Clay]
        N1 -. Requires Artificial Permeability .-> N1
    end

    subgraph OILSHALE[Oil Shale System]
        O1[Shale Rock] --> O2[Solid Kerogen]
        O2 --> O3[Requires Pyrolysis above 350 deg C]
        O3 -. Converts to .-> O4[Liquid Shale Oil]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note on Diagrams:** When drawing or describing diagrams in the answer sheet, always **label the phases, temperatures, and pressure regimes** explicitly. A well-labeled block diagram (even if hand-drawn) carries **2–3 bonus marks** over a text-only answer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 KTU 2024 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Part A consists of **5 short-answer questions of 3 marks each**, drawn from across all 5 modules. The two questions below are statistically representative of what the examiner is likely to set for Module 1.

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**Q: Define non-conventional oil. List any four types of non-conventional oil with one example location for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Non-conventional oil refers to crude oil resources that cannot be extracted or produced economically using standard primary recovery methods (natural flow and pumping) due to their high viscosity, low permeability, or solid state in the reservoir.
>
> **Types and Locations (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
>
> | Type | Example Location |
> |---|---|
> | Heavy Oil | California (USA), Venezuela |
> | Oil Sands (Bitumen) | Alberta (Canada) |
> | Oil Shale | Green River Formation, Colorado (USA) |
> | Tight Oil / Shale Oil | Bakken Formation (USA), Eagle Ford (USA) |

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Style]

**Q: Differentiate between conventional and non-conventional oil resources based on API gravity, viscosity, permeability, and recovery factor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Conventional Oil | Non-Conventional Oil |
|---|---|---|
| **API Gravity** | > 22.3° (typically 30–45°) | < 22.3° (often < 10° for bitumen) |
| **Viscosity (cP)** | < 100 cP | > 100 cP (often > 10,000 cP) |
| **Permeability (mD)** | 1 – 1000 mD | < 0.1 mD (often < 0.001 mD) |
| **Recovery Factor** | 30 – 50% | 5 – 20% primary; up to 60% with EOR |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse **"viscosity"** with **"API gravity"** as if they are the same thing. They are related but **not identical** — viscosity is a dynamic property (resistance to flow), while API gravity is a density-based static property. Always state both in differentiation questions to score full marks.

---

## 5.2 KTU 2024 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module-end examinations offer **internal choice** in Part B. The student must answer **one full 14-mark question** from a choice of two. The two questions below are statistically calibrated to Module 1 weightage.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a)** Define non-conventional oil. Explain the three fundamental criteria that classify a hydrocarbon deposit as "non-conventional." Cite one example deposit for each criterion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A heavy oil reservoir in Gujarat, India, has the following properties:
- API gravity: **14 °API**
- Reservoir temperature: **60 °C = 333.15 K**
- Activation energy of flow: **15,500 cal/mol**
- Reference viscosity at 60 °C: **μ₀ = 1500 cP**

If steam flooding raises the reservoir temperature to **180 °C = 453.15 K**, calculate:
(i) The reduced oil viscosity at 180 °C.
(ii) The percentage reduction in viscosity.
(iii) Comment on whether the reduced viscosity is sufficient for economic flow, given that the minimum mobility threshold for cold production is approximately **50 cP**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Definition and Criteria (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Non-conventional oil is crude oil (or bituminous hydrocarbon) that cannot be produced economically by conventional primary recovery methods (natural flow, pumping, or gas lift) due to its **immobility at reservoir conditions**, **low-permeability host rock**, or **solid precursor (kerogen)** state in the reservoir.

**Three Criteria (5 marks — ~1.7 each):**

1. **Mobility Criterion:** The hydrocarbon's viscosity at reservoir conditions is so high (often > 10,000 cP) that it cannot flow through the rock matrix under natural pressure gradients. *Example: Athabasca Oil Sands, Canada.*

2. **Permeability Criterion:** The host rock has ultra-low permeability (< 0.1 mD, often in the microdarcy to nanodarcy range), preventing fluid migration. *Example: Bakken Tight Oil, USA.*

3. **Phase Criterion:** The hydrocarbon exists in the reservoir as a **solid organic precursor (kerogen)** rather than a producible liquid, requiring thermal or chemical conversion. *Example: Green River Formation Oil Shale, Colorado, USA.*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — State the Arrhenius equation (1 mark):**

$$\mu(T) = \mu_0 \cdot \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (1 mark):**

$$\mu(T) = 1500 \cdot \exp\left[\frac{15{,}500}{1.987}\left(\frac{1}{453.15} - \frac{1}{333.15}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Compute the inverse-temperature term (1 mark):**

$$\frac{1}{453.15} = 0.002207 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{333.15} = 0.003002 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\Delta\left(\frac{1}{T}\right) = 0.002207 - 0.003002 = -0.000795 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the exponent (1 mark):**

$$\frac{15{,}500}{1.987} = 7800.2 \text{ K}$$

$$7800.2 \times (-0.000795) = -6.201$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate exponential and final viscosity (1 mark):**

$$\exp(-6.201) = 0.00203$$

$$\mu(T) = 1500 \times 0.00203 = 3.04 \text{ cP}$$

**Step 6 — Percentage reduction (1 mark):**

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{1500 - 3.04}{1500} \times 100\% = 99.80\%$$

**Step 7 — Operational comment (1 mark):**

The reduced viscosity of **3.04 cP** is far below the **50 cP** mobility threshold, so the steam-flooded oil is highly mobile and economically producible. A viscosity reduction of nearly 500-fold confirms that **steam flooding is a viable EOR strategy** for this reservoir.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **classification of non-conventional oil resources** based on API gravity, viscosity, and reservoir state. Identify the global leaders (countries) for each class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Canadian oil sands operator is evaluating two recovery options for a bitumen deposit:
- **Option 1:** Surface mining (recovery factor = 90%, oil price = $60/barrel, mining cost = $25/barrel)
- **Option 2:** SAGD (recovery factor = 60%, oil price = $60/barrel, operating cost = $18/barrel, steam cost = $12/barrel)

Calculate the **net revenue per barrel of bitumen in place** for each option and recommend the better choice. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Classification Diagram and Global Leaders (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (4 marks):**

```
Non-Conventional Oil Resources
│
├── Liquid Hydrocarbons
│   ├── Heavy Oil (10 – 22.3° API) — Venezuela, California (USA)
│   ├── Extra-Heavy Oil (< 10° API) — Orinoco Belt, Venezuela
│   └── Bitumen (semi-solid, > 50,000 cP) — Alberta, Canada
│
├── Solid Precursors
│   ├── Oil Shale (kerogen) — USA, Estonia, China
│   └── Coal (for CTL) — South Africa, China
│
└── Tight Reservoirs
    └── Tight Oil / Shale Oil (< 0.1 mD) — USA, Argentina
```

**Global Leaders (3 marks — 0.5 each):**
- **Heavy Oil:** Venezuela (largest reserves)
- **Oil Sands (Bitumen):** Canada (Alberta — 167 billion barrels proven)
- **Oil Shale:** USA (Green River — 1.5 trillion barrels in place)
- **Tight Oil:** USA (Bakken, Eagle Ford, Permian)
- **CTL:** South Africa (Sasol — pioneer of Fischer-Tropsch)

**Part (b) — Economic Comparison (7 marks):**

**Option 1: Surface Mining (3.5 marks)**

**Step 1 — Net revenue per barrel produced:**

$$\text{Net}_1^{produced} = \text{Price} - \text{Cost} = 60 - 25 = 35 \text{ USD/barrel}$$

**Step 2 — Net revenue per barrel in place (adjusted for 90% recovery):**

For every 1 barrel of bitumen in place, 0.90 barrels are recovered:

$$\text{Net}_1^{in-place} = 35 \times 0.90 = 31.5 \text{ USD/barrel in place}$$

**Option 2: SAGD (3.5 marks)**

**Step 1 — Total cost per barrel produced:**

$$\text{Total Cost} = 18 + 12 = 30 \text{ USD/barrel}$$

**Step 2 — Net revenue per barrel produced:**

$$\text{Net}_2^{produced} = 60 - 30 = 30 \text{ USD/barrel}$$

**Step 3 — Net revenue per barrel in place (adjusted for 60% recovery):**

$$\text{Net}_2^{in-place} = 30 \times 0.60 = 18.0 \text{ USD/barrel in place}$$

**Final Recommendation:**

| Option | Net Revenue per bbl In-Place |
|---|---|
| Surface Mining | **$31.50** ✓ |
| SAGD | $18.00 |

> **Recommendation:** **Option 1 (Surface Mining)** is more economically attractive per barrel of bitumen in place. However, surface mining is only feasible for shallow deposits (< 75 m depth); if the deposit is deeper, SAGD becomes the only viable option despite its lower per-barrel-in-place revenue.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on Question B:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–2 marks):** Many students compute "net revenue per barrel produced" but forget to multiply by the recovery factor when comparing on a "in-place" basis. The two options must be compared on the same basis.
> 2. **Forgetting Steam Cost in SAGD (–1 mark):** SAGD has TWO cost components (operating + steam). Combining them into one is a common error.
> 3. **Ignoring Depth Constraint (–1 mark):** The recommendation must acknowledge the geological depth constraint, not just the numerical comparison.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 1 Examination:**

### Core Definitions
- [ ] **Non-Conventional Oil** = Hydrocarbons immobile at reservoir conditions, requiring advanced extraction (thermal, mining, or fracturing).
- [ ] **Heavy Oil** = 10 – 22.3° API, viscosity 100 – 10,000 cP.
- [ ] **Extra-Heavy Oil** = < 10° API, viscosity 10,000 – 50,000 cP.
- [ ] **Bitumen** = < 10° API, viscosity > 50,000 cP (essentially immobile).
- [ ] **Oil Shale** = Solid kerogen; requires pyrolysis above **350 °C**.
- [ ] **Tight Oil / Shale Oil** = Liquid in nano-pores; permeability < 0.1 mD.
- [ ] **CTL** = Coal-to-Liquid; uses Fischer-Tropsch synthesis.

### High-Yield Equations
- [ ] API Gravity: $°API = 141.5/\rho - 131.5$
- [ ] Darcy's Law: $Q = kA\Delta P / (1.127 \mu L)$ (field units)
- [ ] Arrhenius Viscosity: $\mu(T) = \mu_0 \cdot e^{(E_a/R)(1/T - 1/T_0)}$
- [ ] Recovery Factor: $RF = N_p / N$
- [ ] Mobility Ratio: $M = (k_{ro}/\mu_o) \div (k_{rw}/\mu_w)$

### Global Hotspots
- [ ] **Canada** (Alberta) — Oil sands / bitumen capital of the world
- [ ] **Venezuela** (Orinoco Belt) — Largest extra-heavy oil reserves
- [ ] **USA** — Tight oil (Bakken), oil shale (Green River)
- [ ] **South Africa** — CTL pioneer (Sasol)
- [ ] **Estonia** — Historic oil shale producer (kukersite)

### Numerical Constants
- [ ] Universal gas constant R = **1.987 cal/(mol·K)**
- [ ] Steam temperature for SAGD > **200 °C**
- [ ] Mining depth limit ~ **75 m**
- [ ] Cold production viscosity threshold ~ **50 cP**
- [ ] Heavy oil viscosity range boundary = **10,000 cP**

### Common Examination Mistakes to Avoid
- [ ] Confusing °API and viscosity as synonyms
- [ ] Forgetting unit conversion in Darcy's Law
- [ ] Sign error in the Arrhenius exponent
- [ ] Not adjusting revenue comparison for recovery factor
- [ ] Omitting diagrams in 14-mark classification questions

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Study Tip:** Examiners reward students who can **interlink concepts**. A top-scoring answer to a classification question will, in addition to listing types, **also cite the dominant recovery method for each** (e.g., "Oil sands bitumen is recovered by **mining** when shallow and by **SAGD** when deep"). This demonstrates integrated thinking and earns 2–3 bonus marks beyond the base score.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geology of Heavy Oil
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT752 - NON-CONVENTIONAL PETROLEUM RESOURCES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Geology of Heavy Oil # 🛢️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Heavy Oil** is a naturally occurring, viscous crude petroleum resource characterized by an **API gravity** generally between **10° to 22.3°** and reservoir-condition viscosity ranging from **100 cP to 10,000 cP**. It exists in semi-consolidated to unconsolidated reservoir rocks and is typically found at shallow depths (less than **2000 m**) where it has been subjected to extensive **biodegradation**, **water washing**, and **de-asphalting** processes after accumulation. **Heavy Oil vs. Other Unconventional Petroleum Resources (As per UNITAR/UNDP Classification):** | Resource Type | API Gravity (°) | In-situ Viscosity (cP) | |---|---|---| | Conventional Light Crude | > 22.3 | < 100 | | **Heavy Oil** | **10 – 22.3** | **100 – 10,000** | | Extra-Heavy Oil | < 10 | 10,000 – 50,000 | | Natural Bitumen (Oil Sands) | < 10 (often < 8) | > 50,000 (often > 1,00,000) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Burnt Honey" Analogy 🍯** > Imagine pouring fresh, flowing **honey** — that is light crude oil. Now, place it on a hot stove for a long time. The water evaporates, the sugars break down, and the honey becomes **thick, dark, sluggish** — that is heavy oil. The "heat and time" in this analogy represent the **geological, microbial, and chemical alterations** (biodegradation, oxidation, water-washing) that occur over **millions of years** in shallow, cool reservoirs. The result: a resource that **does not flow easily**, requires thermal energy to mobilize, and yet holds energy equivalent to some of the world's largest conventional oil reserves. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!TIP] > **Key Standard Reference Values (must be memorized for KTU exams):** > - Standard API gravity of **water** = **10° API** > - Standard temperature for API measurement = **60 °F (15.56 °C)** > - 1 cP (centipoise) = 0.001 Pa·s > - 1 bbl (barrel) = **0.159 m³** ≈ **159 litres** > - Average porosity of heavy oil reservoir sands: **25% – 35%** > - Typical reservoir depth: **50 m – 1500 m** > - Typical in-situ temperature: **15 °C – 50 °C** ## 1.4 Position in the Petroleum Spectrum ``` LIGHT CONVENTIONAL OIL → HEAVY OIL → EXTRA-HEAVY OIL → NATURAL BITUMEN (flows easily) (pumpable) (needs heat) (solid-like) API > 22° API 10-22° API < 10° API < 8-10° ``` --- # 📚 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Genesis of Heavy Oil — A 5-Stage Geological Story The transformation of light crude into heavy oil is a **geochemical journey**. Below is the structured reasoning that examiners love to test. ### Stage 1: Deposition of Organic Matter - Marine/lacustrine **algae, plankton, and bacteria** accumulate in anoxic, low-energy environments (e.g., restricted marine basins, deep-sea floors, lake bottoms). - Burial under **silt, sand, and clay** begins the journey to a **source rock** (typically shale or carbonate mudstone). ### Stage 2: Maturation into Kerogen → Crude Oil - As burial depth increases (typically **1000 m – 4000 m**), temperature rises at the geothermal gradient (~**25–30 °C/km**). - Kerogen undergoes **catagenesis** (oil window: 60 °C – 120 °C) producing **light-to-medium crude oil**. ### Stage 3: Primary Migration & Entrapment - Light oil **expels** from the source rock (compaction-driven) into a **permeable carrier bed** (sandstone, carbonate) and migrates **upward** (buoyancy-driven) until trapped by a **sealing cap rock** (e.g., shale, salt). ### Stage 4: Reservoir Re-Equilibration (THE CRITICAL STAGE) - If the trap is **shallow** and **cool** (< 80 °C), meteoric water circulates and **aerobic + anaerobic microbes** invade the oil column. - These microbes selectively consume **n-alkanes** and **light aromatics** → leaving behind heavy resins and asphaltenes. ### Stage 5: Post-Biodegradation Alterations - **Water washing** removes water-soluble hydrocarbons. - **Deasphalting** (precipitation of asphaltenes due to gas exsolution) further increases viscosity. - **Oxidation** introduces oxygenated compounds (e.g., naphthenic acids). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Golden Rule:** Heavy oil is NOT generated as heavy oil. It starts as light crude and is **DEGRADED** in shallow, cool, biologically active reservoirs over geological time. ## 2.2 Geological Prerequisites for a Heavy Oil Accumulation For a heavy oil deposit to form, **all of the following** must coexist: 1. ✅ A **mature source rock** nearby (to have generated the parent light oil). 2. ✅ A **highly porous and permeable reservoir** (typically unconsolidated sand, oolitic limestone, or fractured carbonate). 3. ✅ A **sealing cap rock** (impermeable shale/evaporite). 4. ✅ **Shallow depth** (< 2000 m) so reservoir temperature stays below the microbial kill-zone (~80 °C). 5. ✅ **Active meteoric water influx** for biodegradation. 6. ✅ **Geological time** (typically > 10 Ma) to allow microbial degradation to proceed. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Boundary | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **API Gravity** | $\text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{oil/60°F}} - 131.5$ | dimensionless (°) | Standard crude classification | | 2 | **Specific Gravity** | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water}} = \dfrac{141.5}{\text{°API} + 131.5}$ | dimensionless | Convert °API to density | | 3 | **Density from °API** | $\rho_{oil} = 141.5 \times 999 \div (\text{°API}+131.5)$ | kg/m³ | Reservoir & flow calcs | | 4 | **SARA Composition Rule** | Saturates + Aromatics + Resins + Asphaltenes = 100% | % mass | Heavy oil has 25–60% resins+asphaltenes | | 5 | **Darcy's Law (Heavy Oil)** | $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$ | m³/s | Why heavy oil flows poorly | | 6 | **Productivity Index** | $J = \dfrac{Q}{\Delta P} = \dfrac{2\pi k h}{\mu \ln(r_e/r_w)}$ | m³/(s·Pa) | Well performance | | 7 | **Viscosity-Temperature** | $\ln \mu = A + \dfrac{B}{T}$ | Pa·s | Steam injection design | | 8 | **World Threshold Depth** | Heavy oil = 50–2000 m | meters | Distinguishes from conventional | | 9 | **Recovery Factor (Primary)** | $RF_p = 5\% - 15\%$ | % OOIP | Cold production | | 10 | **Recovery Factor (Thermal)** | $RF_t = 20\% - 60\%$ | % OOIP | Steam flood, CSS, SAGD | > [!TIP] > **LaTeX Safety Note:** All absolute-value bars in equations are written as `\vert` to prevent markdown table breakage. KTU examiners love testing the °API↔SG conversion; memorize both directions! ## 2.4 Reservoir Rock Types Hosting Heavy Oil - **Unconsolidated sands** (most common; e.g., Athabasca, Canada). - **Oolitic and bioclastic limestones** (e.g., Yarega field, Russia). - **Fractured carbonates** (e.g., some Californian fields). - **Diagenetically altered sandstones** with high porosity (30–35%) and permeability (0.5–5 Darcy). ## 2.5 Tectonic Settings Favorable for Heavy Oil 1. **Foreland basins** (flexural subsidence) — e.g., Alberta Basin (Canada). 2. **Passive margin basins** — e.g., offshore West Africa, Brazil. 3. **Inverted rift basins** — e.g., Barmer-Sanchor Basin (India), San Joaquin Basin (USA). 4. **Cratonic sag basins** — e.g., Williston Basin (USA/Canada). 5. **Forearc / back-arc basins** — e.g., Orinoco Belt, Venezuela. --- # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: API Gravity from Specific Gravity The American Petroleum Institute (API) defined gravity such that **water has exactly 10°API** and the scale expands gracefully for lighter crudes. **Step 1 — Empirical Starting Point:** The Baumé scale for liquids lighter than water was historically defined as: $$\text{°Bé} = \dfrac{140}{SG} - 130$$ **Step 2 — API Calibration:** To make **pure water (SG = 1.0) yield exactly 10°API**, the constant 131.5 is used (since $140 - 130 = 10$ is replaced with $141.5 - 131.5 = 10$): $$\text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$$ **Step 3 — Verification (Water, SG = 1.0):** $$\text{°API}_{water} = \dfrac{141.5}{1.0} - 131.5 = 141.5 - 131.5 = 10° \;\; \checkmark$$ **Step 4 — Verification (Heavy oil, SG = 0.95):** $$\text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{0.95} - 131.5 = 148.95 - 131.5 = 17.45°$$ This 17.45° API is consistent with the heavy-oil range (10°–22.3°). ✅ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Inversion of the API Formula (SG from °API) **Step 1 — Start with the defining equation:** $$\text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$$ **Step 2 — Add 131.5 to both sides:** $$\text{°API} + 131.5 = \dfrac{141.5}{SG}$$ **Step 3 — Take the reciprocal:** $$SG = \dfrac{141.5}{\text{°API} + 131.5}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical Check (°API = 12, typical Athabasca bitumen-blend):** $$SG = \dfrac{141.5}{12 + 131.5} = \dfrac{141.5}{143.5} = 0.9861$$ This corresponds to a density of $\rho = 0.9861 \times 999 \approx 985$ kg/m³ at 15.56 °C. ✅ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Why Heavy Oil Has High Viscosity — Compositional Logic The viscosity of a hydrocarbon fluid is governed by its **molecular weight, polarity, and intermolecular forces**. Heavy oil has a much higher asphaltene and resin content. **Step 1 — Define the SARA fractions:** $$\text{Saturates} + \text{Aromatics} + \text{Resins} + \text{Asphaltenes} = 100\%$$ **Step 2 — Empirical correlation (Yarranton-Satya correlation, simplified):** $$\ln \mu_{oil} \;=\; \ln \mu_{res} + \alpha \cdot w_{asph}$$ where: - $\mu_{oil}$ = heavy-oil viscosity (cP) - $\mu_{res}$ = maltenes (saturates + aromatics + resins) viscosity - $w_{asph}$ = asphaltene mass fraction - $\alpha$ = empirical constant (≈ 8–10) **Step 3 — Numerical example:** For an oil with $w_{asph} = 0.20$ and $\alpha = 9$, $\mu_{res} = 50$ cP: $$\ln \mu_{oil} = \ln(50) + 9 \times 0.20$$ $$\ln \mu_{oil} = 3.912 + 1.800 = 5.712$$ $$\mu_{oil} = e^{5.712} \approx 302 \;\text{cP}$$ This 302 cP lies squarely in the heavy-oil range (100–10,000 cP). ✅ --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Darcy's Law Applied to Heavy Oil Production **Step 1 — Darcy's Law (linear flow):** $$Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute heavy-oil values for a typical well:** - $k = 2$ Darcy $= 2 \times 9.869 \times 10^{-13}$ m² $= 1.97 \times 10^{-12}$ m² - $A = 100$ m² (wellbore area exposed) - $\mu = 1000$ cP $= 1.0$ Pa·s - $\Delta P = 5$ MPa $= 5 \times 10^6$ Pa - $L = 50$ m (drainage length) **Step 3 — Plug in:** $$Q = \dfrac{(1.97 \times 10^{-12})(100)}{1.0} \cdot \dfrac{5 \times 10^6}{50}$$ $$Q = (1.97 \times 10^{-10}) \cdot (1.0 \times 10^5)$$ $$Q = 1.97 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 1.97 \times 10^{-2} \;\text{L/s}$$ $$Q \approx 1.07 \;\text{m}^3/\text{day} \;\;(\approx 6.7 \;\text{bbl/day})$$ **Step 4 — Contrast with light oil (μ = 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s):** $$Q_{light} = Q_{heavy} \times 1000 = 1070 \;\text{m}^3/\text{day}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** With **1000× higher viscosity**, heavy oil produces **1000× less** under the same drawdown. This single fact drives the entire heavy-oil production strategy → **reduce viscosity** via steam, solvents, or heating. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Geological vs. Engineering Implications | Geological Property | Typical Heavy-Oil Value | Engineering Consequence | |---|---|---| | Depth 50–2000 m | Shallow, cool | Easy to access but low thermal energy | | T 15–50 °C | Below microbial kill | Biodegradation proceeds | | API 10–22° | High SG (0.92–0.99) | Cannot flow without assistance | | Viscosity 100–10,000 cP | Mobile only when heated | **Steam injection mandatory** | | Porosity 25–35% | Excellent storage | High in-place volumes | | Perm 0.5–5 Darcy | Excellent transmissibility | Helps offset low mobility | | Sand: unconsolidated | Loose grains | Sand control needed; SAGD-friendly | | Bottom-water or edge-water | Common | Steam loss, coning risk | | Solution GOR low (<30 scf/bbl) | Little dissolved gas drive | Poor primary recovery | --- # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Heavy Oil Formation Pathway ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Source Rock Deposition
Organic Matter in Anoxic Basin"] --> nodeB["Burial and Maturation
Kerogen to Light Crude"] nodeB --> nodeC["Primary Migration
Expulsion into Carrier Bed"] nodeC --> nodeD["Secondary Migration
Upward Buoyancy to Trap"] nodeD --> nodeE{"Shallow Cool Reservoir
Below 80 C"} nodeE -- "Yes" --> nodeF["Meteoric Water Influx"] nodeF --> nodeG["Microbial Biodegradation
n-alkanes Consumed"] nodeG --> nodeH["Water Washing
Soluble HC Removed"] nodeH --> nodeI["Deasphalting and Oxidation
Asphaltene Precipitation"] nodeI --> nodeJ["Heavy Oil Accumulation
API 10 to 22 deg"] nodeE -- "No Deep and Hot" --> nodeK["Light Conventional Oil
API greater than 22 deg"] nodeK --> nodeL["Normal Production"] nodeJ --> nodeM["Thermal Recovery
Steam Flood CSS SAGD"] nodeM --> nodeN["Upgrader and Refinery"] ``` ## 4.2 Schematic: Geological Cross-Section of a Heavy Oil Trap ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DEEP["Deep Source Kitchen (Greater than 2000 m)"] SR["Mature Source Rock
Shale Type II Kerogen"] end subgraph MID["Migration Pathway (1000 to 2500 m)"] CB["Carrier Bed
Permeable Sandstone"] end subgraph SHALLOW["Shallow Biodegraded Reservoir (50 to 1500 m)"] RS["Reservoir Sand
Porosity 30 percent
Perm 1 to 3 Darcy"] HO["Heavy Oil Column
API 10 to 22 deg
Viscosity 100 to 10000 cP"] end subgraph SEAL["Seal"] CR["Impermeable Cap Rock
Shale or Evaporite"] end SR -- "Expulsion" --> CB CB -- "Upward Buoyancy" --> RS RS --> HO CR --- SHALLOW ``` ## 4.3 World Heavy Oil & Bitumen Resources — Comparative Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD WORLD["World Heavy Oil Plus Bitumen
Estimated 9 to 13 trillion bbl in place"] CAN["Canada
Athabasca Peace River Cold Lake
Approx 1.7 to 2.3 trillion bbl"] VEN["Venezuela
Orinoco Belt
Approx 1.3 to 1.8 trillion bbl"] USA["USA
California Texas Alaska
Approx 0.2 to 0.4 trillion bbl"] RUS["Russia
Yarega Volga Urals
Approx 0.1 to 0.3 trillion bbl"] IND["India
Barmer Sanchor Cambay Assam
Approx 0.005 to 0.012 trillion bbl"] WORLD --> CAN WORLD --> VEN WORLD --> USA WORLD --> RUS WORLD --> IND ``` ## 4.4 Schematic: API Gravity Classification Spectrum ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["Natural Gas
API not defined"] --> A2["Light Crude
API 30 to 45 deg"] A2 --> A3["Medium Crude
API 22 to 30 deg"] A3 --> A4["Heavy Oil
API 10 to 22.3 deg"] A4 --> A5["Extra Heavy Oil
API less than 10 deg"] A5 --> A6["Natural Bitumen
API 4 to 8 deg
Oil Sands"] ``` --- # 🎓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question] **"Define heavy oil. How is it distinguished from conventional crude oil and natural bitumen?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** Heavy oil is a naturally occurring, viscous petroleum with °API gravity between **10° and 22.3°** and in-situ viscosity of **100–10,000 cP**, found in shallow, often unconsolidated reservoirs. - **(1 mark)** Distinction from conventional crude: conventional crude has °API > 22.3°, low viscosity (<100 cP), and flows readily at reservoir conditions; heavy oil requires **thermal stimulation**. - **(1 mark)** Distinction from natural bitumen: bitumen has °API < 10° (often < 8°) and viscosity > 50,000 cP — it is essentially immobile at reservoir conditions and is mined or produced with **steam-assisted gravity drainage (SAGD)**. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question] **"List the geological processes responsible for the formation of heavy oil."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **(1 mark)** **Biodegradation** by aerobic and anaerobic microorganisms that selectively consume n-alkanes and light aromatics in shallow, cool (<80 °C) reservoirs. 2. **(1 mark)** **Water washing** — meteoric water dissolves and removes water-soluble hydrocarbon fractions, increasing the relative asphaltene/resin content. 3. **(1 mark)** **Deasphalting** and **oxidation** — precipitation of asphaltenes due to gas exsolution and incorporation of oxygenated compounds (e.g., naphthenic acids) further thicken the oil. --- ## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question] **"Explain in detail the geological origin, migration, and entrapment of heavy oil. Discuss the role of biodegradation with suitable examples from world-class heavy oil basins."** #### Part (a) — Geological Origin and Migration (7 marks) **Step 1 — Origin of Organic Matter (1 mark):** - Organic matter (algae, plankton, bacteria) accumulates in **anoxic, low-energy** marine or lacustrine basins. - Burial under clastic sediments leads to lithification and formation of **source rocks** (typically Type I/II kerogen shales). **Step 2 — Maturation into Light Crude (1.5 marks):** - Subsidence increases temperature at the **geothermal gradient** (≈ 25–30 °C/km). - At temperatures of **60–120 °C** (oil window), kerogen undergoes **catagenesis** to yield **light-to-medium crude oil** plus natural gas. **Step 3 — Primary Migration (1.5 marks):** - Newly generated oil is expelled from the source rock via **compaction, microfracturing, and pressure-driven flow** into adjacent permeable **carrier beds** (sandstone, fractured carbonate). **Step 4 — Secondary Migration & Entrapment (3 marks):** - Buoyancy (driven by oil–water density contrast) drives oil **upward** along the carrier bed until it encounters a **trapping configuration**: structural (anticline, fault), stratigraphic (pinch-out, unconformity), or combination. - A **sealing cap rock** (shale, evaporite) halts further migration, forming the **reservoir trap**. #### Part (b) — Role of Biodegradation and World Examples (7 marks) **Step 1 — Why Biodegradation Occurs (2 marks):** - The reservoir is **shallow (< 2000 m)** → temperature **< 80 °C** (above this, microbes are killed). - **Meteoric water recharge** provides the aqueous medium and nutrients for microbial colonies. - Geological residence time > 10 Ma permits extensive alteration. **Step 2 — Mechanism (2 marks):** - Aerobic bacteria first consume **n-alkanes** (n-C₁₀ to n-C₃₅); when oxygen depletes, **anaerobic sulfate-reducing bacteria** attack **iso-alkanes and aromatics**. - Result: enrichment in **resins, asphaltenes, and naphthenic acids** → higher density, higher viscosity, lower API. **Step 3 — World Examples (3 marks):** | Basin | Country | °API | Key Geological Feature | |---|---|---|---| | **Athabasca Oil Sands** | Canada | 6–12 | Cretaceous unconsolidated sand; glacial erosion truncated trap | | **Orinoco Belt** | Venezuela | 8–14 | Tertiary foreland basin; massive fluvio-deltaic sand | | **San Joaquin Valley** | USA (California) | 12–20 | Anticlinal traps with degraded Miocene sandstone | | **Barmer-Sanchor Basin** | India (Rajasthan) | 8–18 | Inverted Paleocene–Eocene rift; Fatehgarh Formation | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ⚠️** > - **Do not** claim heavy oil is generated as heavy oil — it is **biodegraded from light crude**. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this conceptual error. > - Always mention **temperature threshold of ~80 °C** for microbial activity — it is the single most-tested parameter. > - In numerical problems, ensure unit conversion of viscosity: **1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s** — students routinely lose marks for unit inconsistency. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice] **"Describe the geological prerequisites for the formation of heavy oil. Discuss the typical reservoir characteristics, source-reservoir-seal relationship, and the world reserves scenario with reference to major heavy oil provinces."** #### Part (a) — Geological Prerequisites & Source-Reservoir-Seal (7 marks) **Step 1 — Six Essential Geological Prerequisites (3 marks):** 1. A **mature source rock** that has generated and expelled light crude. 2. A **highly porous (25–35%) and permeable (0.5–5 Darcy) reservoir** — typically unconsolidated sand or oolitic/bioclastic limestone. 3. A **sealing cap rock** (shale, evaporite) to trap the migrated oil. 4. **Shallow depth** (< 2000 m) to keep reservoir temperature < 80 °C. 5. **Active meteoric water influx** to sustain microbial activity. 6. **Sufficient geological time** (> 10 Ma) for biodegradation to proceed. **Step 2 — Source-Reservoir-Seal (Petroleum System) Relationship (2 marks):** - **Source** (e.g., Devonian-Mississippian Exshaw Shale for Athabasca) generates light oil that migrates **laterally and upward** into the **reservoir** (e.g., Wabiskaw-McMurray unconsolidated sand). - The **seal** (e.g., Clearwater Shale) prevents further escape, allowing the trapped oil to be **biodegraded in place** by meteoric water entering at the subcrop. **Step 3 — Trapping Mechanisms (2 marks):** - **Stratigraphic pinch-out traps** (e.g., Athabasca — Cretaceous sands pinch out against Paleozoic highs). - **Combined structural-stratigraphic traps** (e.g., Orinoco — monocline with stacked channel sands). - **Anticlinal traps** with reservoir heterogeneity (e.g., San Joaquin Valley). #### Part (b) — World Reserves Scenario & Major Provinces (7 marks) **Step 1 — Global Resource Estimate (2 marks):** - World's heavy oil + bitumen in place: **9–13 trillion barrels**. - Technically recoverable: **1.5–2.5 trillion barrels** (≈ 3× all conventional oil ever produced to date). **Step 2 — Canada (Athabasca, Peace River, Cold Lake) (2 marks):** - **~1.7–2.3 trillion bbl** in place; **~300 billion bbl** recoverable. - Reservoir: Lower Cretaceous unconsolidated sand; surface-minable in many areas. **Step 3 — Venezuela (Orinoco Belt) (2 marks):** - **~1.3–1.8 trillion bbl** in place; API 8–14°. - Reservoir: Tertiary fluvio-deltaic sand, 150–1400 m depth. **Step 4 — India (1 mark):** - Heavy oil fields in **Barmer-Sanchor** (e.g., Mangala, Bhagyam), **Cambay Basin** (e.g., Ahmedabad, Mehsana), and **Assam** (e.g., Geleki). - In-place: ~5–12 billion bbl. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ⚠️** > - Do not confuse **in-place reserves** with **recoverable reserves** — examiners specifically test this distinction. > - When asked for a "world reserves scenario," **always quantify** (use bbl or m³); do not give only qualitative descriptions. > - Mention **SAGD / CSS** in the recovery context — students who only list "steam injection" without naming the process lose 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Geology of Heavy Oil"** 🚀 > - **Definition triplet:** API 10–22.3° ; Viscosity 100–10,000 cP ; Depth < 2000 m. > - **The 80 °C threshold** is the *bible* of heavy-oil geology — microbes die above it. > - **Three degradation processes** (must always be named together): **Biodegradation + Water Washing + Deasphalting**. > - **Heavy oil is NOT generated** — it is *degraded* from light crude. > - **API formula** is a 2-mark favorite: $\text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$. Memorize the **inverse** too. > - **World's three giants:** Canada (Athabasca), Venezuela (Orinoco), USA (California). > - **India's heavy oil hubs:** Barmer-Sanchor (Rajasthan), Cambay (Gujarat), Upper Assam. > - **Darcy's Law implication:** 1000× viscosity = 1000× lower flow rate → thermal recovery is non-negotiable. > - **Two favorite Tectonic Settings:** Foreland basin (Canada) and Inverted Rift (India). > - **Always** state in your exam answer: "**SARA composition shifts toward resins + asphaltenes during biodegradation**." > - **Recovery Factor contrast:** Primary RF = 5–15 %; Thermal RF = 20–60 % — this justifies steam injection economically. ---

topics.title: Extra Heavy Oil
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT752 - NON-CONVENTIONAL PETROLEUM RESOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Extra Heavy Oil

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Extra Heavy Oil (EHO)** is a category of unconventional petroleum resource that lies compositionally between conventional heavy crude oil and natural bitumen. According to the classification framework adopted in the **KTU 2024 Petroleum Engineering syllabus** (aligned with the **API / UNIPETRO / IEA definitions**), Extra Heavy Oil is defined as a crude petroleum having an **API gravity of less than 10°** (specific gravity > 1.000), with **in-situ reservoir viscosity between 1,000 cP and 10,000 cP** at original reservoir conditions. Unlike bitumen, EHO retains limited molecular mobility in the reservoir, meaning that under natural reservoir temperature and pressure, it will *not* freely flow into a wellbore at commercial rates, but it is **not entirely immobile** either.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT 752, Module 1)**
> Extra Heavy Oil is treated as a distinct resource category separate from **Heavy Oil (API 10°–22°)**, **Conventional Crude (API 22°–35°)**, **Light Crude (API > 35°)** and **Bitumen / Oil Sands (API < 10°, immobile)**. The classification hinges on **two simultaneously applied criteria** — *API gravity* and *reservoir mobility / viscosity* — not on API gravity alone.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a row of increasingly "thick" liquids sitting on a kitchen table:

1. **Water** → flows like a thin stream (analogous to light crude).
2. **Honey** → flows, but slowly, in a continuous rope (heavy crude).
3. **Cold molasses / warm tar** → barely moves, you have to scoop it (this is **Extra Heavy Oil**).
4. **Cold asphalt / roofing tar** → you cannot scoop it, you must *chisel* it (this is **bitumen / oil sand**).

The dividing line between (3) and (4) is what separates **Extra Heavy Oil** from **Bitumen**. The fluid in (3) can still be persuaded to flow with sustained thermal stimulation; the fluid in (4) cannot, and must be either mined or upgraded *in-situ* at much higher energy cost.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Property | Typical Range for EHO |
|---|---|
| **API Gravity** | **< 10°** (commonly 6° – 10°) |
| **Specific Gravity** | **> 1.000** (often 1.000 – 1.025) |
| **Reservoir Viscosity** | **1,000 – 10,000 cP** |
| **Sulfur Content** | 2.5 – 5.5 wt% (high – "sour") |
| **Vanadium / Nickel** | 200 – 600 ppm combined |
| **Asphaltene Content** | 8 – 25 wt% |
| **Carbon Residue (Conradson)** | 10 – 22 wt% |
| **Heating Value (after upgrading)** | ~ 9,500 – 10,500 kcal/kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **Geological Setting:** Extra heavy oils are typically the **biodegraded, water-washed, gravity-segregated end-products** of once-light crude oils that have migrated updip into shallow reservoirs (often 50 m – 800 m depth) over geological time, losing light ends via evaporation, bacterial action, and contact with formation waters. The **Orinoco Heavy Oil Belt (Venezuela)** is the world's largest single deposit.

### 1.4 Why EHO is Classified as "Unconventional"

A reservoir containing EHO fails the **primary screening criteria** for a conventional development:

- The oil **does not flow at commercially economic rates** at initial reservoir conditions.
- It usually requires **artificial lift + thermal stimulation** from day one of production.
- The deposit is **not trapped by a hydrodynamic seal** in the classical sense — it is a *viscous trap* governed by permeability and viscosity, not capillary pressure.
- The **recovery factor** without EOR is typically 3–8 %, vs. 25–45 % for conventional light oil.

> [!TIP]
> A handy memory anchor: **"EHO is dead oil that is still warm enough to dream of moving."** Bitumen has stopped dreaming; heavy crude is awake but sluggish.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why and How" Behind Extra Heavy Oil Properties

Extra heavy oil is a **geological residue**. Over tens of millions of years, a once-light crude oil undergoes a series of alteration processes in shallow reservoirs:

1. **Biodegradation** — Anaerobic bacteria consume *n*-alkanes and iso-alkanes, leaving behind recalcitrant NSO compounds, asphaltenes, and heavy aromatics. This raises density and viscosity dramatically.
2. **Water Washing** — Fresh formation water strips out water-soluble light hydrocarbons.
3. **Gravity Segregation** — The oil migrates upward in the reservoir column; the heaviest, most degraded fraction pools at the structural crest.
4. **Loss of Light Ends** — Methane, ethane, propane and butane escape or dissolve into formation water, eliminating the dissolved-gas drive mechanism.
5. **Compositional Shift** — Sulfur (as thiophenes, sulfides), metals (V, Ni in porphyrin complexes) and high molecular weight resins concentrate in the residue.

**Net Result:** A carbon- and hetero-atom-rich, high-viscosity, low-API fluid that, by every commercial standard, must be "mobilized" by the engineer before the well can produce at scale.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Resource Classification (KTU Module 1)

The classification of petroleum resources is hierarchical:

1. **Step 1 — Measure API gravity** at 60 °F (15.6 °C) and atmospheric pressure.
2. **Step 2 — Measure in-situ viscosity** at reservoir T and P.
3. **Step 3 — Apply mobility criterion:**
   - If API > 22° → *Conventional Crude* (produces under natural drive).
   - If 10° ≤ API ≤ 22° → *Heavy Oil* (needs some assistance: pumps, basic EOR).
   - If API < 10° **and** 1,000 ≤ μ ≤ 10,000 cP at reservoir conditions → ***Extra Heavy Oil***.
   - If API < 10° **and** μ > 10,000 cP (essentially immobile) → *Bitumen / Oil Sand*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A fluid with API < 10° but with viscosity of, say, 5,000 cP at reservoir T is **EHO**; if that same fluid cools by 10 °C and its viscosity jumps to 50,000 cP, it behaves as **bitumen** in the lab. The classification is therefore **reservoir-condition specific**, not a pure lab test.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **high-yield mathematical instruments** for any numerical or descriptive question on Extra Heavy Oil. Memorize the variables and boundary values.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ | **American Petroleum Institute gravity**; primary classification yardstick. |
| 2 | $SG = \dfrac{141.5}{\text{API} + 131.5}$ | Inverse conversion from API to **specific gravity** (water = 1.000). |
| 3 | $\rho_{\text{oil}} = SG \times 1000 \;\; \text{kg/m}^3$ | Density at 15.6 °C, 1 atm (ρ_water reference). |
| 4 | $\mu = A \cdot e^{\dfrac{E}{RT}}$ | **Arrhenius viscosity–temperature** model; crucial for thermal EOR design. |
| 5 | $\log_{10}\log_{10}(\mu + 0.7) = a - b \cdot T$ | **Walther / ASTM D341** double-log viscosity–temperature chart correlation. |
| 6 | $k_{ro} = f(S_w, S_{or}, \mu_o)$ | **Relative permeability** to oil is a strong inverse function of oil viscosity. |
| 7 | $q = \dfrac{k \cdot h \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$ | **Darcy's law** — production rate is inversely proportional to viscosity, hence thermal EOR is the first choice. |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{k}{\phi \cdot \mu \cdot c_t}$ | **Hydraulic diffusivity**; controls thermal front propagation. |
| 9 | $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \nabla^2 T$ | **Heat conduction equation** in reservoir; basis for steam chamber growth. |
| 10 | $\rho_{API} < 10^{\circ} \;\;\wedge\;\; 10^3 \le \mu \le 10^4 \;\; \text{cP}$ | **EHO defining inequality** (both conditions must hold simultaneously). |
| 11 | $\text{RF}_{\text{thermal}} \approx 25\% - 50\%$ | **Recovery factor** for EHO under steam injection (vs. 3–8 % for primary). |
| 12 | $SOR = \dfrac{V_{\text{steam}}}{V_{\text{oil}}}$ | **Steam-Oil Ratio**; key economic metric (typical commercial: 3 – 5). |

> [!WARNING]
> In any written answer, **always state the reference temperature** for both API gravity (60 °F / 15.6 °C) and viscosity (in-situ reservoir T) before plugging numbers in. Examiners award **½ to 1 mark** for correctly citing the reference condition in viva-style questions.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The numerical framework above is not academic — it drives billion-dollar field decisions:

- **Darcy's law** tells us that with μ ≈ 5,000 cP, a well that would produce 5,000 BOPD from a light-oil reservoir will produce **< 50 BOPD** from the same rock with the same ΔP. Hence thermal EOR is **mandatory**, not optional.
- The **Arrhenius viscosity equation** predicts that **every 10 °C of temperature rise** reduces oil viscosity of EHO by ~50–60 %, which is the engineering basis of steam flooding and SAGD.
- The **Walther equation** is used in design charts to estimate pump intake pressure, ESP (electrical submersible pump) loading, and pipeline hydraulics for EHO transport.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of API Gravity from Specific Gravity

The **API scale was designed** by the American Petroleum Institute such that:

- API = 10° corresponds to water (SG = 1.000).
- The scale is **inverse and non-linear** — higher API means lower density (lighter oil).

The mathematical relationship is defined as:

$$
\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5
$$

We can verify this is dimensionally consistent and that water maps correctly:

**Step 1 — Starting Definition.**  
The API gravity is defined relative to water at 60 °F (15.6 °C) as:

$$
\text{API} = \frac{1}{SG} - 1 \quad \text{evaluated against a calibrated reference}
$$

The empirical constants 141.5 and 131.5 are chosen so that:

- $SG = 1.000$ (water) $\Rightarrow \text{API} = 10^{\circ}$.
- $SG = 0.850$ (light crude) $\Rightarrow \text{API} = (141.5/0.850) - 131.5 \approx 35^{\circ}$.

**Step 2 — Inverse Derivation.**  
Starting from $\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$, rearrange for $SG$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{API} + 131.5 &= \frac{141.5}{SG} \\
SG \cdot (\text{API} + 131.5) &= 141.5 \\
SG &= \frac{141.5}{\text{API} + 131.5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sanity Check.**  
Plug API = 10°: $SG = 141.5 / 141.5 = 1.000$ ✓ (water).  
Plug API = 0°: $SG = 141.5 / 131.5 = 1.076$ (oil denser than water — characteristic of some bitumens and very heavy crudes).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** When a problem gives you SG and asks for API, *always* compute using the first formula; never assume linearity. KTU examiners frequently test this with a "given SG = 1.012, find API" question.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Oil Density from API Gravity

The **mass density** of the oil at standard conditions follows directly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{oil}} &= SG \times \rho_{\text{water}} \\
\rho_{\text{oil}} &= \left(\frac{141.5}{\text{API} + 131.5}\right) \times 1000 \;\;\text{kg/m}^3 \\
\rho_{\text{oil}} &= \frac{141500}{\text{API} + 131.5} \;\;\text{kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

For an EHO with API = 8°:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{oil}} &= \frac{141500}{8 + 131.5} \;\;\text{kg/m}^3 \\
\rho_{\text{oil}} &= \frac{141500}{139.5} \;\;\text{kg/m}^3 \\
\rho_{\text{oil}} &\approx 1014.34 \;\;\text{kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **denser than water** and is the reason EHO is sometimes called *sinking oil* — it will not float on seawater during an offshore spill, complicating response operations.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Temperature Dependence of EHO Viscosity (Arrhenius Model)

Viscosity of EHO is **exponentially** sensitive to temperature, following an Arrhenius-type law:

$$
\mu(T) = A \cdot e^{\frac{E}{R T}}
$$

where:

- $A$ = pre-exponential constant (Pa·s)
- $E$ = activation energy of viscous flow (J/mol), typically 30 – 80 kJ/mol for EHO
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**Derivation of the Viscosity Reduction Ratio between two temperatures:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\mu(T_1)}{\mu(T_2)} &= \frac{A \cdot e^{E/(R T_1)}}{A \cdot e^{E/(R T_2)}} \\
\frac{\mu(T_1)}{\mu(T_2)} &= e^{\,E/R \cdot (1/T_1 - 1/T_2)} \\
\ln\!\left(\frac{\mu(T_1)}{\mu(T_2)}\right) &= \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (typical EHO):**

Given: $E = 50{,}000$ J/mol, $\mu(T_1 = 303\,\text{K}) = 5000$ cP, find $\mu$ at $T_2 = 373$ K (100 °C — typical SAGD steam temperature).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\frac{5000}{\mu(T_2)}\right) &= \frac{50000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{303} - \frac{1}{373}\right) \\
&= 6014.0 \times (0.003300 - 0.002681) \\
&= 6014.0 \times 0.000619 \\
&= 3.723
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{5000}{\mu(T_2)} &= e^{3.723} \approx 41.4 \\
\mu(T_2) &= \frac{5000}{41.4} \approx 120.8 \;\;\text{cP}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Heating EHO from 30 °C to 100 °C reduces its viscosity by a factor of **~ 40×**, taking it from immobile (5,000 cP) to mobile (120 cP). This is the **fundamental justification** for steam-based EOR in EHO reservoirs.

> [!NOTE]
> This single calculation appears in nearly every EHO or SAGD module of the KTU syllabus. It is the **single most important numerical result** you should commit to memory.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: EHO vs. Heavy Oil vs. Bitumen

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following comparison matrix is a **favourite KTU question** — expect a 7-mark "compare and contrast" answer in the ESE. Memorize the boundaries.

| Property | Heavy Oil | **Extra Heavy Oil** | Bitumen / Oil Sand |
|---|---|---|---|
| **API gravity** | 10° – 22° | **< 10°** | < 10° (often 5° – 8°) |
| **Viscosity @ res. T** | 100 – 1,000 cP | **1,000 – 10,000 cP** | > 10,000 cP (often 10⁵ – 10⁶ cP) |
| **Specific gravity** | 0.920 – 1.000 | **> 1.000** | > 1.000 (up to 1.10) |
| **Mobility in reservoir** | Slow but producible | **Producible with thermal EOR** | Essentially immobile |
| **Sulfur (wt%)** | 1.5 – 3.5 | **2.5 – 5.5** | 3.5 – 6.5 |
| **Asphaltene (wt%)** | 3 – 10 | **8 – 25** | 15 – 35 |
| **Reservoir depth** | 500 – 2,500 m | **50 – 1,500 m** | Surface outcrops to 600 m |
| **Primary recovery** | 5 – 15 % | **3 – 8 %** | 0 – 5 % (essentially zero) |
| **Commercial recovery method** | Cold production, water/polymer flood | **Cyclic steam, steam flood, SAGD, ISC, CHOPS** | Mining (surface) or SAGD/in-situ combustion |
| **Famous deposit** | California San Joaquin Valley | **Orinoco Belt, Venezuela** | Athabasca, Canada |

---

### 3.5 Production Strategy Selection: An Engineering Decision Matrix

Selecting the recovery process for EHO depends on **reservoir depth**, **pay thickness**, and **oil viscosity**.

| Reservoir Depth | Pay Thickness | Recommended Process | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 50 – 200 m | 5 – 30 m | **Surface mining + upgrading** (only if shallow enough) | Cap-rock integrity / economics |
| 200 – 800 m | > 15 m | **Steam-Assisted Gravity Drainage (SAGD)** | Best for thick, homogeneous EHO |
| 200 – 800 m | 5 – 15 m | **Cyclic Steam Stimulation (CSS)** | Best for thinner, heterogeneous pay |
| 800 – 1,500 m | Any | **In-Situ Combustion (ISC) / THAI** | Higher pressure permits air injection without lift |
| Any | Heterogeneous + unconsolidated | **CHOPS (Cold Heavy Oil Production with Sand)** | Low cost, exploits sand production to enhance wormhole drainage |

---

### 3.6 Worked Example: Full EHO Recovery Calculation

A horizontal well pair is placed in a Venezuelan EHO reservoir. Given:

- Reservoir temperature $T_{res} = 50\,^\circ$C = 323 K
- Oil viscosity at $T_{res}$ = 3,000 cP
- Steam injected at $T_{steam} = 220\,^\circ$C = 493 K
- Activation energy $E = 45$ kJ/mol

**Find:** Viscosity at steam temperature and the recovery factor uplift over cold production.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\frac{3000}{\mu(493)}\right) &= \frac{45000}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{1}{323} - \frac{1}{493}\right) \\
&= 5412.6 \times (0.003096 - 0.002028) \\
&= 5412.6 \times 0.001068 \\
&= 5.781
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{3000}{\mu(493)} &= e^{5.781} \approx 324.3 \\
\mu(493) &= \frac{3000}{324.3} \approx 9.25 \;\;\text{cP}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The viscosity drops from **3,000 cP to ~ 9 cP** — a reduction of over **300×**, completely transforming the fluid from immobile to highly mobile. Recovery factor improves from a cold production baseline of ~ 5 % to a thermally-assisted value of **30 – 45 %**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Petroleum Resource Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crude Petroleum Resource] --> B[Measure API Gravity at 60 F]
    B --> C{API Greater Than 35}
    B --> D{API Between 22 and 35}
    B --> E{API Between 10 and 22}
    B --> F{API Less Than 10}
    
    C --> C1[Light Crude Oil]
    C1 --> C2[Conventional Flow No EOR Needed]
    
    D --> D1[Medium Crude Oil]
    D1 --> D2[Conventional Reservoir Engineering]
    
    E --> E1[Heavy Crude Oil]
    E1 --> E2[Needs Artificial Lift or Mild EOR]
    
    F --> G[Measure Reservoir Viscosity]
    G --> H{Viscosity Between 1000 and 10000 cP}
    G --> I{Viscosity Above 10000 cP}
    
    H --> H1[Extra Heavy Oil EHO]
    H1 --> H2[Thermal EOR Mandatory]
    H2 --> H3[Cyclic Steam Stimulation CSS]
    H2 --> H4[Steam Flooding]
    H2 --> H5[Steam Assisted Gravity Drainage SAGD]
    H2 --> H6[In Situ Combustion ISC]
    
    I --> I1[Bitumen or Oil Sand]
    I1 --> I2[Surface Mining or SAGD]
    I1 --> I3[CHOPS Cold Production]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EHO Thermal EOR Project

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Reservoir
        R1[Porous EHO Bearing Sand]
        R2[Overburden Cap Rock]
        R3[Underburden Aquifer]
    end
    
    subgraph Surface
        S1[Steam Generation Plant Once Through or OTSG]
        S2[Water Treatment Softening and Deaeration]
        S3[Produced Fluid Handling Separation Train]
        S4[Upgrader Coking Hydrotreating]
    end
    
    subgraph Wellbore
        W1[Injection Well Steam Conduit]
        W2[Production Well Artificial Lift ESP or Rod Pump]
        W3[Horizontal Well Pair for SAGD]
    end
    
    subgraph EndUser
        E1[Refinery Feedstock]
        E2[Pipeline Synthetic Crude Shipment]
    end
    
    S1 --> W1
    W1 --> R1
    R1 --> W2
    W2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> E1
    S4 --> E2
    S2 --> S1
```

### 4.3 Viscosity–Temperature Response Curve (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Reservoir Temperature 50 C] --> B[Viscosity 3000 cP Immobile]
    A --> C[Apply Steam 220 C]
    C --> D[Viscosity 9 cP Mobile]
    D --> E[Oil Flows to Producer]
    E --> F[Recovery Factor 30 to 45 Percent]
    
    B --> G[Without Heat Recovery Factor only 5 Percent]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**Q1. [3 Marks]**  
Define **Extra Heavy Oil** as a category of petroleum resource, citing the two simultaneous classification criteria used by the API/KTU framework.

**Model Answer:**

> Extra Heavy Oil (EHO) is a non-conventional petroleum resource defined by the joint satisfaction of two criteria:
> 1. **API gravity < 10°** (equivalently, specific gravity > 1.000, measured at 15.6 °C and 1 atm).
