> 2. **In-situ reservoir viscosity between 1,000 cP and 10,000 cP** at original reservoir temperature and pressure.
>
> Unlike bitumen, EHO is *not* completely immobile in the reservoir; however, it will not flow to a wellbore at commercial rates without thermal stimulation. It is typically the biodegraded residue of once-light crude oil trapped in shallow, unconsolidated sandstone reservoirs.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for API criterion, 1 mark for viscosity criterion, 1 mark for mobility/density context.)*

---

**Q2. [3 Marks]**  
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, KTU 2024 Scheme Adapted]**  
State **three distinguishing characteristics** that separate Extra Heavy Oil from (a) Heavy Crude Oil and (b) Bitumen.

**Model Answer:**

| Distinguishing Feature | Heavy Oil | **Extra Heavy Oil** | Bitumen |
|---|---|---|---|
| API gravity | 10° – 22° | **< 10°** | < 10° |
| Reservoir viscosity | 100 – 1,000 cP | **1,000 – 10,000 cP** | > 10,000 cP |
| Mobility at reservoir T | Slow but producible | **Needs thermal EOR** | Immobile (mining/SAGD only) |

*(3 marks: 1 mark per distinguishing feature with correct numerical boundary.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Module Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]**  
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1 Adapted]**  
Discuss the **geological origin, migration pathway, and reservoir characteristics** of Extra Heavy Oil deposits, with a specific reference to the **Orinoco Heavy Oil Belt (Faja Petrolífera del Orinoco)** in Venezuela.

**Model Answer:**

Extra Heavy Oil deposits are the **end-products of long-term in-reservoir alteration** of once-light crude oils. The formation pathway involves four coupled geological processes:

1. **Original Accumulation:** A conventional light oil (API 30°–40°) is trapped in a deep, geopressured sandstone reservoir capped by an effective seal.

2. **Uplift and Seal Degradation:** Tectonic uplift or seal failure over geological time allows the oil column to leak **updip** into a shallower, unconsolidated sandstone carrier bed, often directly connected to the surface.

3. **In-reservoir Biodegradation:** At shallow depths (50 – 800 m) and reservoir temperatures of 25 – 60 °C, **anaerobic bacteria** metabolize the *n*-alkanes, iso-alkanes, and light aromatics of the crude. This selectively strips the mobile, low-viscosity fractions and leaves behind **resins, asphaltenes, and heavy NSO compounds**. Water washing simultaneously removes water-soluble light ends.

4. **Gravity Segregation and Final Pooling:** The dense, altered oil pools at the **structural crest** of the shallow trap, often as a thick (30 – 100 m) continuous column with little or no gas cap and weak aquifer support.

**The Orinoco Belt, Venezuela (Reference Example):**

- Location: North bank of the Orinoco River; covers an area of ~ **55,000 km²** (about the size of Switzerland).
- Reserves: ~ **1,300 billion barrels** of original oil in place, the largest single hydrocarbon deposit on Earth.
- API gravity: **6° – 10°** (canonical EHO range).
- Viscosity at reservoir T: 1,000 – 5,000 cP.
- Sulfur content: 2.8 – 4.0 wt% (sour, requires hydrodesulfurization).
- Reservoir: **Oficina Formation**, unconsolidated Oligocene sandstone, 150 – 1,500 m depth.
- Recovery: Mandatorily requires **thermal EOR**; major projects use horizontal wells with **SAGD** and **steam flooding**.

*(7 marks: 2 for geological origin, 2 for migration/alteration, 2 for Orinoco specifics, 1 for diagrammatic/terminology precision.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]**  
**[KTU University Exam – KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1 Adapted]**  
A Venezuelan EHO has the following properties:
- API gravity = 8°
- Reservoir temperature T₁ = 50 °C (323 K)
- In-situ viscosity μ(T₁) = 4,000 cP
- Activation energy of viscous flow, E = 48 kJ/mol
- Steam injection temperature T₂ = 220 °C (493 K)

Calculate: **(i)** Specific gravity and density of the oil at 15.6 °C, **(ii)** Viscosity at the steam temperature, **(iii)** Comment on the engineering significance of the result for EOR design.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Specific Gravity and Density:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SG &= \frac{141.5}{\text{API} + 131.5} \\
SG &= \frac{141.5}{8 + 131.5} \\
SG &= \frac{141.5}{139.5} \\
SG &= 1.0143
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{oil}} &= SG \times 1000 \;\text{kg/m}^3 \\
\rho_{\text{oil}} &= 1.0143 \times 1000 \\
\rho_{\text{oil}} &= 1014.3 \;\text{kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

*Result: The oil is denser than water (ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³) and will not float on a water spill.*

**Part (ii) — Viscosity at Steam Temperature:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\frac{\mu(T_1)}{\mu(T_2)}\right) &= \frac{E}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) \\
&= \frac{48000}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{1}{323} - \frac{1}{493}\right) \\
&= 5773.4 \times (0.003096 - 0.002028) \\
&= 5773.4 \times 0.001068 \\
&= 6.166
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{4000}{\mu(T_2)} &= e^{6.166} \approx 476.5 \\
\mu(T_2) &= \frac{4000}{476.5} \approx 8.39 \;\text{cP}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (iii) — Engineering Significance:**

- The viscosity drops by a factor of **~ 477×** (from 4,000 cP to 8.4 cP) when the oil is heated from 50 °C to 220 °C.
- This reduction transforms EHO from a **non-producible, immobile fluid** into a **light-oil-equivalent mobile phase** (8 cP is comparable to a light condensate).
- Therefore, **thermal EOR (steam flooding / SAGD)** is not merely advantageous but **physically necessary** — without it, Darcy's law would give commercial flow rates only in the low single digits of BOPD.
- The choice of **220 °C steam** is dictated by the saturated steam pressure curve; further heating above 240 °C is impractical without compromising wellbore integrity.
- This calculation is the *quantitative justification* for the entire thermal EOR industry on EHO fields.

*(7 marks: 2 for SG/density, 3 for viscosity calculation, 2 for engineering comment.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a) [7 Marks]**  
**[KTU University Exam – KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1 Adapted]**  
Explain, with neat sketches, the **Steam-Assisted Gravity Drainage (SAGD)** process for Extra Heavy Oil recovery. Discuss the role of **steam chamber growth, gravity drainage, and the sub-cool** control parameter.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of SAGD:**  
SAGD, developed by **Dr. Roger Butler** in the late 1970s at Imperial Oil (Canada), uses a **pair of parallel horizontal wells** spaced 4 – 6 m vertically apart, drilled near the base of the EHO pay zone.

**Process Steps:**

1. **Start-up Phase (Steam Circulation):** For 2 – 4 months, steam is circulated in **both wells simultaneously** to heat the surrounding rock and oil to mobile conditions.

2. **Production Phase:** The upper well is converted to a **continuous steam injector**, and the lower well becomes the **producer**. The injected steam forms a rising **steam chamber** at the top of the reservoir.

3. **Steam Chamber Growth:** The chamber grows **upward and sideways** by conduction into the cold oil sand; the heat thins the EHO at the chamber boundary.

4. **Gravity Drainage:** The heated, mobile EHO (now with viscosity as low as 5 – 50 cP) flows **downward along the chamber edges** to the lower producer, driven purely by gravity.

5. **Continuous Operation:** The chamber expands until it reaches the cap rock above and the lateral reservoir boundaries; recovery continues for 5 – 15 years.

**The Sub-Cool Control Parameter:**

The **sub-cool** is defined as the temperature difference between the steam injection temperature and the produced fluid temperature:

$$
T_{\text{sub-cool}} = T_{\text{steam,inj}} - T_{\text{prod}}
$$

A typical operating sub-cool is **20 – 40 °C**. This ensures:

- Live steam **does not reach the producer** (which would short-circuit the chamber).
- The producer operates in a **sub-cooled liquid leg**, maintaining stable gravity drainage.
- Excessive sub-cool (> 50 °C) indicates poor chamber contact and falling production.

**Recovery Factor:** SAGD typically achieves **40 – 60 %** of original oil in place, a dramatic uplift over the 3 – 8 % of primary cold production.

*(7 marks: 2 for principle, 2 for process steps, 2 for sub-cool, 1 for recovery factor.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]**  
**[KTU University Exam – KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1 Adapted]**  
Compare and contrast the following EHO recovery methods in a tabular form: **(i) Cyclic Steam Stimulation (CSS), (ii) Steam Flooding, (iii) In-Situ Combustion (ISC) / Fire Flood, and (iv) Cold Heavy Oil Production with Sand (CHOPS).** Mention the typical recovery factor and one major limitation of each.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Method | Operating Principle | Typical RF | Major Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cyclic Steam Stimulation (CSS)** | "Huff and Puff" — steam injected, well shut in to soak, then produced. Repeated cycles in same well. | **20 – 35 %** | Declining productivity with each cycle; well-by-well operation; high steam-oil ratio (SOR) |
| 2 | **Steam Flooding (Continuous)** | Continuous steam injection in a pattern of injectors surrounding producers; drives oil bank toward producers. | **30 – 50 %** | Channeling of steam through high-perm zones; inefficient in thin/heterogeneous reservoirs |
| 3 | **In-Situ Combustion (Fire Flood)** | Air injection ignites the oil; combustion front propagates, vaporizing and cracking the oil. | **35 – 55 %** (potential) | Operational complexity; produced gases (CO₂, H₂S, SO₂) require handling; safety concerns |
| 4 | **CHOPS** | Cold production with deliberate sand co-production; creates "wormholes" that enhance drainage area. | **8 – 15 %** | Low overall recovery; sand handling at surface; limited to unconsolidated formations |

**Comparative Engineering Insight:**

- CSS is the **simplest to implement** but the **least efficient** in steam usage.
- Steam flooding requires **pattern design** and **reservoir homogeneity**.
- ISC has the **highest energy efficiency** (in-situ heat generation) but the **highest operational risk**.
- CHOPS is **low-cost and fast to deploy** but **inherently limited** in ultimate recovery.

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks per method × 4 methods = 6 marks; 1 mark for the comparative engineering summary.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Quoting API gravity without specifying the reference temperature** (60 °F / 15.6 °C). Examiners deduct **½ – 1 mark** every time this is missing.
> 2. **Confusing EHO with Bitumen.** If the question says "API < 10°", do not jump to "Bitumen" — confirm the viscosity criterion first. Misclassification costs **2–3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 3. **Skipping units in numerical answers.** A viscosity of "8" is meaningless; "8 cP" or "8 × 10⁻³ Pa·s" is what earns the mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the two-criterion logic.** EHO = API < 10° **AND** μ between 1,000 and 10,000 cP. Either criterion alone is **insufficient**.
> 5. **Not mentioning reservoir conditions** when quoting viscosity. Viscosity of EHO drops by an order of magnitude per 50 °C; examiners expect to see "at reservoir T" or "at steam T" explicitly.
> 6. **Hand-waving the Arrhenius calculation.** Always show the logarithm step, the inverse-T difference, and the final exponentiation. A single missing step = 1 mark lost.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** EHO is a non-conventional petroleum resource with **API gravity < 10°** *and* **in-situ viscosity 1,000 – 10,000 cP**.
- **Boundary rule:** API < 10° + low viscosity = EHO; API < 10° + very high viscosity = **bitumen**.
- **Conversion master formula:** $\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ (memorize + inverse form).
- **Density consequence:** EHO is **denser than water** ($\rho$ > 1000 kg/m³) for API < 10°.
- **Origin pathway:** Light crude → updip migration → biodegradation + water washing → gravity segregation → EHO.
- **Canonical example:** **Orinoco Belt, Venezuela** (largest single deposit, ~ 1,300 billion bbl OIP).
- **Thermal EOR is mandatory.** Primary cold production yields only 3–8 % RF.
- **Arrhenius viscosity law** $\mu = A \cdot e^{E/RT}$ — heating from 30 °C to 220 °C reduces EHO viscosity by **~ 100× – 500×**.
- **Top recovery methods:** **CSS, Steam Flooding, SAGD, ISC/THAI, CHOPS**.
- **SAGD is gold standard** for thick (> 15 m) EHO pay zones; sub-cool = 20 – 40 °C is the operating target.
- **Typical EHO chemistry:** high S (2.5–5.5 wt%), high V+Ni (200–600 ppm), high asphaltenes (8–25 wt%).
- **SOR (Steam-Oil Ratio)** is the key economic metric: 3 – 5 is commercial, > 5 is uneconomic.
- **Field development:** always start with a thermal front; horizontal wells + steam is the modern norm.
- **Upgrading required** because of high S and metal content — downstream hydrotreating and coker units are mandatory before refining.
- **KTU-favourite numerical:** Always show the Arrhenius logarithm step in viscosity calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sand and Bituminous Oil Shale
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT752 - NON-CONVENTIONAL PETROLEUM RESOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sand and Bituminous Oil Shale

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Oil Sand (Tar Sand / Bituminous Sand)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> Oil sand is a naturally occurring unconsolidated or loosely consolidated sedimentary deposit consisting of a mixture of **sand grains, clay minerals, water, and a dense, highly viscous hydrocarbon called bitumen**. The bitumen content is typically greater than **10 % by mass** of the total deposit, making the resource economically recoverable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams):**
> - **Bitumen** → Naturally occurring viscous petroleum that will not flow to a well in its natural state. API gravity < 10°.
> - **Tar** → Man-made product obtained by destructive distillation of coal or organic matter.
> - Therefore, the technically correct term is **"Oil Sand"** or **"Bituminous Sand"**, NOT "Tar Sand" (though the term is still used colloquially).

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of oil sand as a **"chocolate-covered sandcastle"**. Imagine clean beach sand where each grain is coated with a thick, sticky layer of cold chocolate (the bitumen) that has hardened in place. You cannot simply pump the chocolate out through a straw because it is too thick — you have to either **dig up the whole sandcastle and separate the chocolate in a washing machine** (surface mining + hot water extraction), or you must **heat the chocolate underground until it melts and flows** (in-situ thermal recovery).

**Global Reserves & Major Deposits:**
| Country | Major Deposit | Estimated Bitumen Reserve (Billion Barrels) |
|---------|---------------|---------------------------------------------|
| **Canada** | **Athabasca, Peace River, Cold Lake (Alberta)** | ~ **1.7–2.3 trillion barrels** of oil equivalent |
| **Venezuela** | Orinoco Belt | ~ **1.3 trillion barrels** |
| Russia | Volga-Ural, Timan-Pechora | Significant |
| USA | Utah, California, Texas | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Canada's Alberta province alone holds the third-largest oil reserves in the world**, after Saudi Arabia and Venezuela, when oil sands are included.

---

### 1.2 Oil Shale (Kerogen Shale / Bituminous Shale)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> Oil shale is a **fine-grained, laminated sedimentary rock** (typically marlstone or mudstone) of high organic matter content, containing **kerogen** — a solid, insoluble, high-molecular-weight organic precursor from which liquid hydrocarbons (shale oil) can be produced by **thermal decomposition (pyrolysis / retorting)**. On heating, kerogen decomposes into oil, gas, and a solid carbonaceous residue (char).

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine a **"frozen fruit cake"**. The cake itself (the rock) is solid and contains pieces of fruit (kerogen) locked inside the dough. The fruit is NOT juice yet — you must **bake the cake to release the juice**. Similarly, kerogen is **NOT oil** — it must be **"cooked" (heated to 450–550 °C)** to break down into liquid oil. No well drilled in the conventional sense can directly produce oil; you must apply heat, either by mining and cooking at the surface (ex-situ retorting) or by heating the rock underground (in-situ conversion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (Examiner's Favourite Question):**
> - **Oil Shale** contains **kerogen** (solid, immature organic matter) → must be **pyrolysed**.
> - **Shale Oil / Shale Gas** refers to hydrocarbons **trapped in low-permeability shale formations** (e.g., Bakken, Eagle Ford) → produced by **hydraulic fracturing** of existing oil/gas.
> - These two are **completely different** despite the similar names.

**Major Global Deposits:**
| Country | Major Deposit | Notes |
|---------|---------------|-------|
| **USA** | **Green River Formation (Colorado, Utah, Wyoming)** | World's largest — estimated **1.5–2.6 trillion barrels** of shale oil |
| China | Fushun, Maoming | Active commercial production |
| Estonia | kukersite deposits | Long history of retorting |
| Brazil | Irati Formation (Paraná Basin) | Significant |
| Russia, Australia, Jordan | Various | Growing interest |

---

### 1.3 Comparison Snapshot

| Property | Oil Sand | Oil Shale |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| Hydrocarbon phase | **Bitumen** (viscous liquid) | **Kerogen** (solid organic matter) |
| State in reservoir | Mobile if heated, immobile otherwise | Solid, immobile |
| Recovery method | Mining + Hot Water; **SAGD, CSS** | Surface/in-situ **Retorting (Pyrolysis)** |
| Typical API gravity of product | 8–14° (synthetic crude after upgrading) | 25–40° (shale oil) |
| Heating required? | Yes (to reduce viscosity) | Yes (to pyrolyse kerogen) |
| Solid residue | Spent sand (tailings) | Spent shale (char) |
| Tonnage to produce 1 barrel oil | ~ **2 tonnes of oil sand** | ~ **1.5–2.4 tonnes of oil shale** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Oil Sand — Composition, Geology & Recovery

#### 2.1.1 Composition of Oil Sand

A typical Athabasca oil sand sample has the following composition:

- **Mineral solids (sand + clay)**: 80 – 85 % by mass
- **Bitumen**: 10 – 15 % by mass
- **Water**: 4 – 6 % by mass

The bitumen acts as a thin film coating each sand grain. A thin film of water (the connate or "fines-free" water envelope) lies between the bitumen and the sand grain, which is the key reason **hot water + caustic (NaOH) process works**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why hot water + NaOH?**
> The caustic reacts with the natural surfactants (humic acids, fatty acids) on the bitumen surface, creating an in-situ surfactant that **detaches the bitumen from the sand grain** and **aerates the bitumen droplet with attached air bubbles**, which float to the surface as froth.

#### 2.1.2 Geological Origin

Oil sands formed when **conventional light crude oils** were degraded at the surface by **bacterial action (biodegradation)** and **water washing** over millions of years. This removed the light fractions, leaving behind heavy, viscous, sulfur-rich bitumen trapped in shallow unconsolidated sand reservoirs (typically < 500 m depth).

#### 2.1.3 Recovery Technologies

**(A) Surface Mining (for shallow deposits < 75 m depth):**
1. Open-pit mining using massive hydraulic shovels and 400-tonne trucks (e.g., Syncrude, Suncor).
2. Ore is slurried with hot water + NaOH in tumblers.
3. Bitumen floats as froth; sand settles as tailings.
4. Froth is diluted with naphtha and centrifuged.
5. Bitumen is upgraded to **Synthetic Crude Oil (SCO)** via coking + hydroprocessing.

**(B) In-Situ Recovery (for deep deposits > 75 m, where overburden removal is uneconomic):**

**(i) Cyclic Steam Stimulation (CSS) — "Huff and Puff":**
- Inject steam → soak for several days → produce (oil + water + steam) from same well.
- Repeating cycles (typically 3–5).

**(ii) Steam-Assisted Gravity Drainage (SAGD):**
- Uses **two horizontal wells** drilled one above the other (~5 m apart).
- Steam injected into upper well → forms a growing **steam chamber** that rises.
- Heated bitumen drains by gravity to lower producer well.

**(iii) CHOPS (Cold Heavy Oil Production with Sand):**
- For thinner, less viscous deposits — sand is deliberately co-produced with bitumen.

**(iv) CSS + Solvent / VAPEX:**
- Combines steam with light hydrocarbon solvents to further reduce viscosity.

#### 2.1.4 Bitumen Properties

- **Density at 15.6 °C**: 970 – 1020 kg/m³
- **API Gravity**: 6 – 12° (negative API in some cases)
- **Viscosity at reservoir T (10 °C)**: 50,000 – 1,000,000 cP (compare: water = 1 cP)
- **Sulfur content**: 3 – 5 % (high — requires extensive upgrading)
- **Metals (V, Ni)**: 100 – 500 ppm (poison catalysts during refining)

---

### 2.2 Oil Shale — Composition, Geology & Pyrolysis

#### 2.2.1 Composition

- **Mineral matrix**: 70 – 90 % (carbonates like calcite, dolomite, silica, clays)
- **Organic matter (kerogen)**: 10 – 30 %
- **Moisture**: 1 – 10 %

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "shale"?**
> The rock is fine-grained, fissile, and shale-like in appearance. The term is a **misnomer** — it is technically a **marlstone (calcareous mudstone)**, not a true shale.

#### 2.2.2 Kerogen Types Relevant to Oil Shale

| Kerogen Type | Origin | Hydrogen/Carbon | Oil Yield |
|--------------|--------|------------------|-----------|
| **Type I** | Algal (e.g., Green River) | Very high H/C | **Highest** |
| **Type II** | Mixed marine/algal | High H/C | Good |
| **Type III** | Woody/coaly | Low H/C | Gas-prone (low oil) |
| **Type IV** | Charcoal-like | Very low H/C | Negligible |

**Green River Formation kerogen is Type I** — the richest, producing the highest yield of shale oil per tonne of rock.

#### 2.2.3 Pyrolysis (Retorting) Chemistry

Kerogen $\xrightarrow{\text{heat, 450–550 °C}}$ Shale Oil + Gas + Char + Water

The Fischer Assay is the **standard laboratory test** to estimate oil yield:

$$\text{Fischer Assay Yield} = \frac{\text{mass of oil produced (g)}}{\text{mass of oil shale charged (g)}} \times 100 \quad (\%)$$

A rock yielding **> 25 gallons of oil per short ton (~100 L per tonne)** is considered economically attractive.

#### 2.2.4 Retorting Technologies

**(A) Ex-Situ (Surface) Retorting:**
- **Internal-combustion retorts** (e.g., Kiviter, Galoter, Paraho)
- Shale is mined, crushed, fed into retort, heated to ~ 500 °C.
- Vapours condensed → raw shale oil → upgraded.

**(B) In-Situ Retorting (modern, eco-friendly):**
- **Shell In-situ Conversion Process (ICP)** — heaters inserted in boreholes slowly heat the rock to 340–370 °C over 2–4 years.
- **Chevron CRUSH** — uses radio-frequency heating.
- Avoids mining, tailings, and surface retort emissions.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | **API Gravity** | $^\circ API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ | $SG$ = specific gravity at 15.6 °C |
| 2 | **Specific Gravity from API** | $SG = \dfrac{141.5}{^\circ API + 131.5}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | **Bitumen Viscosity (Andrade equation)** | $\ln \mu = A + \dfrac{B}{T}$ | $\mu$ in cP, $T$ in K; viscosity drops exponentially with T |
| 4 | **Fischer Assay Oil Yield** | $Y_{oil} = \dfrac{m_{oil}}{m_{shale}} \times 100$ | Mass % |
| 5 | **Yield in gal/ton** | $gal/ton = 7.05 \times Y_{oil}(\%)$ approx. | Conversion (1 bbl = 42 gal) |
| 6 | **Grade threshold for commercial oil sand** | $> 10\%$ bitumen by mass | < 6 % not economic |
| 7 | **Grade threshold for oil shale** | $> 25$ gal/ton (Fischer) | < 10 gal/ton not economic |
| 8 | **Mass of rock needed per barrel** | $M = \dfrac{\rho_{oil} \times 0.159}{Y_{oil}}$ | tonnes per barrel |
| 9 | **Volume of oil in place per unit area** | $V = h \cdot \phi \cdot S_{bit} \cdot \rho_{rock}^{-1}$ | For volumetric reserve estimate |
| 10 | **Thermal conductivity (typical oil shale)** | $k \approx 1.5$ – $2.5$ W/(m·K) | Used in in-situ heating design |
| 11 | **Kerogen H/C ratio (Type I)** | $H/C \approx 1.5$ – $1.7$ | Indicates high oil potential |
| 12 | **Bitumen SARA composition** | Saturates / Aromatics / Resins / Asphaltenes | Determines upgrading route |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All 12 formulas above are KTU high-yield items.** Direct 3-mark and 7-mark sub-questions have appeared on viscosity-temperature relationships, Fischer Assay, and API Gravity in past university examinations.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Upgrading plants** (Suncor, Syncrude in Alberta) use the above properties to size **cokers, hydrotreaters, and desulfurizers**.
- **Pipeline design** requires knowing bitumen viscosity-temperature curve to determine **operating temperature** and **insulation/heating requirements**.
- **Reservoir engineers** use Fischer Assay logs to estimate the **original oil-in-place (OOIP)** for shale plays and decide on **in-situ vs surface recovery**.
- **Environmental engineers** design **tailings management** for oil sands and **spent-shale disposal** for oil shale operations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Viscosity–Temperature Relationship (Andrade Equation)

**Problem Context:** Bitumen viscosity is so high at reservoir temperature that it cannot be pumped. Predicting its viscosity at elevated temperatures is essential for designing SAGD and CSS operations.

**Starting from Eyring's absolute rate theory of viscous flow**, the rate of molecular jump from one equilibrium position to the next is:

$$
k_{jump} = k_B \cdot T \cdot h^{-1} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^{\ddagger}}{RT}\right)
$$

The dynamic viscosity is inversely proportional to the molecular jump frequency:

$$
\mu = \dfrac{Nh}{V} \cdot \dfrac{1}{k_{jump}}
$$

Substituting and rearranging gives the **Andrade equation** (a 2-parameter model widely used for hydrocarbons):

$$
\mu = A' \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{B}{T}\right)
$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$
\ln \mu = \ln A' + \dfrac{B}{T}
$$

Let $A = \ln A'$ for simplicity:

$$
\boxed{\ln \mu = A + \dfrac{B}{T}}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A bitumen sample has viscosity $\mu_1 = 100{,}000$ cP at $T_1 = 288$ K (15 °C), and $\mu_2 = 500$ cP at $T_2 = 373$ K (100 °C). Find viscosity at $T_3 = 423$ K (150 °C) for steam-flood design.

**Step 1:** Find slope $B$ using two known points.

$$
B = \dfrac{\ln \mu_1 - \ln \mu_2}{\left(1/T_1 - 1/T_2\right)}
$$

$$
\ln \mu_1 = \ln(100000) = 11.5129
$$

$$
\ln \mu_2 = \ln(500) = 6.2146
$$

$$
1/T_1 = 1/288 = 0.003472 \; \text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
1/T_2 = 1/373 = 0.002681 \; \text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
B = \dfrac{11.5129 - 6.2146}{0.003472 - 0.002681} = \dfrac{5.2983}{0.000791} = 6698.6 \; \text{K}
$$

**Step 2:** Find intercept $A$.

$$
A = \ln \mu_1 - B/T_1 = 11.5129 - (6698.6 \times 0.003472) = 11.5129 - 23.254
$$

$$
A = -11.741
$$

**Step 3:** Predict viscosity at 423 K.

$$
\ln \mu_3 = A + B/T_3 = -11.741 + (6698.6 / 423)
$$

$$
\ln \mu_3 = -11.741 + 15.836 = 4.095
$$

$$
\mu_3 = e^{4.095} \approx 60.0 \; \text{cP}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Heating the bitumen from 15 °C to 150 °C reduces viscosity from 100,000 cP to ~ 60 cP — a **~ 1,700-fold reduction**. This is the fundamental engineering justification for using **steam injection** in oil sand recovery.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: API Gravity from Specific Gravity

Starting with the basic definition of specific gravity:

$$
SG = \dfrac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water}} \quad \text{at 15.6 °C}
$$

The American Petroleum Institute (API) defined gravity such that water (SG = 1) corresponds to 10° API. The empirical equation adopted is:

$$
^\circ API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5
$$

**Verification at SG = 1 (water):**

$$
^\circ API = \dfrac{141.5}{1} - 131.5 = 10 \; ^\circ API \quad \checkmark
$$

**Worked Example:** A bitumen sample has $\rho = 1015$ kg/m³. Water density = 1000 kg/m³. Find API gravity.

$$
SG = 1015 / 1000 = 1.015
$$

$$
^\circ API = \dfrac{141.5}{1.015} - 131.5 = 139.41 - 131.5 = 7.91 \; ^\circ API
$$

This confirms the bitumen is **heavier than water** (sinks in water) and is classified as **extra-heavy oil**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Mass of Oil Shale Required per Barrel of Oil

Given Fischer Assay yield $Y$ (mass %), density of shale oil $\rho_{oil}$ (kg/m³), and 1 barrel = 0.158987 m³.

Mass of oil in 1 barrel:

$$
m_{oil} = 0.158987 \times \rho_{oil} \quad \text{(kg)}
$$

Mass of oil shale needed to produce this oil:

$$
M_{shale} = \dfrac{m_{oil}}{Y/100} = \dfrac{0.158987 \times \rho_{oil} \times 100}{Y}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{shale} \; (\text{kg}) = \dfrac{15.8987 \times \rho_{oil} \; (\text{kg/m}^3)}{Y(\%)}}
$$

**Worked Example:** $Y = 12\%$, $\rho_{oil} = 920$ kg/m³.

$$
M_{shale} = \dfrac{15.8987 \times 920}{12} = \dfrac{14{,}626.8}{12} \approx 1219 \; \text{kg} \approx 1.22 \; \text{tonnes}
$$

So roughly **1.2 tonnes of oil shale** is required to obtain **one barrel of shale oil** at this yield.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Exam-Revision Tool)

| Decision Parameter | Oil Sand | Oil Shale | Implication |
|--------------------|----------|-----------|-------------|
| Phase of HC | Liquid bitumen | Solid kerogen | Determines energy input |
| Min. economic grade | 10 % bitumen | 25 gal/ton | Both = ~ 100 L/tonne oil |
| Mining ratio | < 0.4 for surface | < 0.5 for surface | Higher = more overburden |
| Water usage | 2–4 m³ per m³ bitumen | 0.5–1 m³ per m³ oil | Critical in arid regions |
| GHG intensity (per barrel) | ~ 50–90 kg CO₂e | ~ 50–110 kg CO₂e | Both > conventional (20 kg) |
| Tailings / solid waste | Mature fine tailings (MFT) | Spent shale (char + minerals) | Long-term storage issue |
| Land footprint (surface) | High | High | Drives shift to in-situ |
| By-products | Sulphur, vanadium, coke | Sulphur, ammonia, carbon black | Adds revenue |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Note
All node IDs below are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters. No reserved Mermaid keywords are used as node names. Labels are kept clean (no markdown or HTML inside quoted strings).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Oil Sand Deposit in Reservoir] --> B1[Decision: Depth less than 75 m]

    D2 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Pipeline to Refinery]

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Steam Generator] -- High Pressure Steam --> W1[Upper Horizontal Injector Well]
    W1 -- Steam Rises and Forms Chamber --> R1[Steam Chamber Growing Upward]
    R1 -- Heat Conduction to Bitumen --> B1[Bitumen Softens and Drains by Gravity]
    B1 --> W2[Lower Horizontal Producer Well]
    W2 --> S2[Emulsion: Bitumen plus Water plus Sand]
    S2 --> T1[Surface Separation Plant]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    P1 --> P2{Fischer Assay Yield above 25 gal per ton}
    P2 -- Yes --> X1[Choose Ex Situ Retorting or In Situ Conversion]
    P2 -- No --> P3[Resource Sub-Economic Reject]
    X1 --> X2[Ex Situ: Crush and Feed to Retort]
    X2 --> X3[Heat to 450 to 550 degree C in Absence of Air]
    X3 --> X4[Kerogen Pyrolysis Reaction]
    X4 --> X5[Raw Shale Oil Vapours and Non Condensable Gas]
    X5 --> X6[Condenser and Fractionator]
    X6 --> O1[Raw Shale Oil to Upgrader]
    X6 --> G1[Non Condensable Gas used as Retort Fuel]
    X3 --> X7[Spent Shale Char to Disposal or Cement Use]

```

### 4.5 Kerogen Pyrolysis Mechanism (Chemical Pathway Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[Type I Kerogen Immature Solid] -- Stage 1 Diagenesis 50 to 60 C --> K2[Bitumen Precursor]
    K2 -- Stage 2 Catagenesis 120 to 150 C --> K3[Liquid Hydrocarbons and Wet Gas]
    K3 -- Stage 3 Metagenesis above 175 C --> K4[Dry Gas Methane and Char]
    K1 -. Industrial Retorting 450 to 550 C .-> K3
    K3 --> O1[Shale Oil Product]
    K4 --> G1[Natural Gas Product]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Differentiate between **bitumen** and **kerogen** in the context of non-conventional petroleum resources.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Feature | Bitumen | Kerogen |
|---------|---------|---------|
| Phase at reservoir | Viscous liquid | Solid organic matter |
| Origin | Biodegraded crude oil | Immature fossilized organic matter |
| Composition | C, H, N, S, O + metals | Cross-linked macromolecular polymer |
| Solubility in CS₂ | Soluble | **Insoluble** |
| Conversion to oil | By **viscosity reduction** (heat) | By **pyrolysis / retorting** (heat + chemical break) |
| Source rock | Oil sand | Oil shale |

(½ mark for each valid point; any 3 distinct distinguishing points earn 3 marks.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define **Fischer Assay** and state its significance in oil shale resource evaluation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Fischer Assay** is the **standard laboratory test (ASTM D3904)** used to determine the **oil yield of an oil shale sample** by controlled pyrolysis under standardised conditions. A finely ground shale sample (~ 100 g) is heated in a Fischer retort at a controlled rate of 12 °C/min up to 500 °C, and the condensable oil vapours are collected and weighed.

**Significance (any 3 of the following earn 3 marks):**
1. Provides a **standard, comparable oil yield** for resource assessment across regions.
2. Determines whether the deposit is **economically viable** (cut-off ~ 25 gal/ton).
3. Helps estimate **original oil-in-place (OOIP)** and reserve calculation.
4. Used to **classify oil shale deposits** as rich, medium, or lean.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 marks each)

> **ESE Module Choice rule:** Answer **ONE** of the two questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)**

**Q.A) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Modified]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the **Steam-Assisted Gravity Drainage (SAGD)** process for in-situ recovery of bitumen from oil sands. Discuss the role of steam chamber formation. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The viscosity of a bitumen sample is 80,000 cP at 20 °C and 200 cP at 120 °C. Using the Andrade equation, estimate the viscosity at 180 °C. Comment on the engineering significance of the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**
- **Diagram (2 marks):** Two horizontal wells ~ 5 m apart in the pay zone; steam chamber rising from upper injector; bitumen draining to lower producer. *(Refer Section 4.3 above.)*
- **Process steps (3 marks):**
  1. Two parallel horizontal wells drilled, one above the other.
  2. High-pressure saturated steam (~ 5–10 MPa, 200–340 °C) is continuously injected into the upper well.
  3. Steam rises and forms a **growing steam chamber** that displaces heated bitumen sideways and downward.
  4. Heated, low-viscosity bitumen and condensed water drain by **gravity** to the lower producer well.
  5. Produced emulsion is lifted to surface, separated, and water is recycled to steam generation.
- **Role of steam chamber (2 marks):**
  1. Provides **continuous thermal front** to maintain bitumen mobility.
  2. Acts as a **gravity-driven displacement** mechanism (no need for high pressure gradients).
  3. Allows **high recovery factor (40–60 %)** compared to CSS.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

Given: $\mu_1 = 80{,}000$ cP at $T_1 = 293$ K; $\mu_2 = 200$ cP at $T_2 = 393$ K. Find $\mu_3$ at $T_3 = 453$ K.

Andrade equation: $\ln \mu = A + B/T$.

**Step 1:** Compute $\ln \mu$ values.

$$
\ln(80{,}000) = 11.290
$$

$$
\ln(200) = 5.298
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for B.

$$
B = \dfrac{11.290 - 5.298}{1/293 - 1/393} = \dfrac{5.992}{0.003413 - 0.002545} = \dfrac{5.992}{0.000868} = 6903.2 \; \text{K}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for A.

$$
A = 11.290 - (6903.2 / 293) = 11.290 - 23.56 = -12.27
$$

**Step 4:** Predict $\mu_3$ at 453 K.

$$
\ln \mu_3 = -12.27 + 6903.2/453 = -12.27 + 15.24 = 2.97
$$

$$
\mu_3 = e^{2.97} \approx 19.5 \; \text{cP}
$$

**Engineering Significance (2 marks):**
- Bitumen viscosity drops from **80,000 cP to ~ 20 cP** when heated to 180 °C — a **~ 4,000-fold reduction**.
- At this viscosity, bitumen is **pumpable** and **flows under gravity**, justifying the use of **thermal recovery (SAGD, CSS)** in oil sands.
- The exponential (not linear) relationship explains why **small increases in steam temperature** yield **massive mobility improvements**.

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)**

**Q.B) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Modified]**
**(a)** Explain the **formation, composition, and classification of oil shales** with reference to the **Green River Formation**. Discuss the role of **kerogen type** in determining oil yield. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** An oil shale sample weighing 110 g, when subjected to Fischer Assay, yielded 12 g of oil. Calculate (i) the Fischer Assay yield in mass %, (ii) yield in **gallons per short ton**, and (iii) the **mass of oil shale required to produce one barrel** of oil, assuming the shale oil density is 920 kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

- **Formation (2 marks):** Oil shales are fine-grained sedimentary rocks deposited in **lacustrine (lake) or marine environments** during periods of high biological productivity. The Green River Formation was deposited in a large **Eocene lake (~ 50 million years ago)** in Wyoming, Utah, and Colorado under warm, humid, alkaline conditions that promoted algal blooms and preservation of organic matter.
- **Composition (2 marks):** Typically 70–90 % mineral matrix (carbonates, clays, silica) and 10–30 % organic matter (kerogen). The Green River kerogen is **Type I**, derived from **blue-green algae (cyanobacteria)**.
- **Classification & Kerogen type (3 marks):**
  - **Type I (algal, lacustrine)** — very high H/C ratio (~ 1.5+), produces **highest oil yield** (up to 150 gal/ton for rich Green River).
  - **Type II (mixed marine/algal)** — moderate H/C, moderate oil yield.
  - **Type III (woody, coaly)** — low H/C, mainly gas-prone.
  - **Type IV (charcoal)** — negligible yield.
  - The **H/C atomic ratio** of kerogen directly determines the **oil potential**: higher H/C = more liquid hydrocarbon per unit organic carbon.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

Given: $m_{shale} = 110$ g, $m_{oil} = 12$ g, $\rho_{oil} = 920$ kg/m³.

**(i) Fischer Assay yield in mass %:**

$$
Y = \dfrac{12}{110} \times 100 = 10.91 \% \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**(ii) Yield in gallons per short ton:**

Using the conversion (1 short ton = 2000 lb = 907.185 kg, 1 gallon = 3.785 L, oil density 920 kg/m³):

$$
\text{Yield per tonne} = \dfrac{10.91}{100} \times 1000 \; \text{kg oil/tonne shale} = 109.1 \; \text{kg oil/tonne}
$$

$$
\text{Volume of oil per tonne} = \dfrac{109.1}{920} = 0.1186 \; \text{m}^3/\text{tonne} = 118.6 \; \text{L/tonne}
$$

$$
\text{Convert L → gallons: } 118.6 / 3.785 = 31.34 \; \text{gal/tonne (metric)} 
$$

Converting from metric to short ton (1 metric tonne = 1000 kg, 1 short ton = 907.185 kg):

$$
31.34 \times (1000/907.185) \approx 34.55 \; \text{gal/short ton} \quad \text{(3 marks)}
$$

**(iii) Mass of oil shale per barrel:**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.3:

$$
M_{shale} = \dfrac{15.8987 \times \rho_{oil}}{Y(\%)} = \dfrac{15.8987 \times 920}{10.91} = \dfrac{14{,}626.8}{10.91} \approx 1340 \; \text{kg} \approx 1.34 \; \text{tonnes} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Don't confuse "oil shale" with "shale oil"** in the answer — 1–2 marks deducted for this in viva and written exams.
> 2. **Use consistent units**: 1 barrel = 0.158987 m³ = 42 US gallons = 158.987 L. Mixing SI and Imperial units is the #1 reason students lose marks.
> 3. **Andrade equation is a 2-parameter model** — students often forget to use the **natural logarithm**; using log₁₀ will give wrong slope.
> 4. **Fischer Assay conversion**: 1 % mass yield ≈ 7.05 gal/short ton (a useful shortcut to remember).
> 5. **SAGD vs CSS**: Many students write them as the same process. CSS uses a single well (huff-and-puff); SAGD uses **two horizontal wells** with **continuous steam injection**.
> 6. **Don't quote viscosity in Pa·s when the question gives it in cP**. Conversion: 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s.
> 7. **Kerogen pyrolysis requires anaerobic conditions** — retorts exclude air. If you write "burning of oil shale", you will lose the chemistry mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Oil sand** = sand + bitumen + water. **Bitumen** is a viscous liquid that needs heating to flow.
- **Oil shale** = rock + kerogen. **Kerogen** is a solid that needs **pyrolysis (450–550 °C)** to release oil.
- **API Gravity** of bitumen: 6–12°; of upgraded SCO: 30–35°; of shale oil: 25–40°.
- **Fischer Assay** is the universal standard for measuring oil shale yield.
- **Major oil sand deposits** are in **Alberta (Canada) and Orinoco (Venezuela)**.
- **Major oil shale deposits** are in the **Green River Formation (USA)**, Fushun (China), and Estonia.
- **SAGD uses two horizontal wells**; **CSS uses one well in cycles**; both rely on **steam injection**.
- **Viscosity drops exponentially with temperature** — Andrade equation: $\ln \mu = A + B/T$.
- **Grade threshold**: > 10 % bitumen for oil sand; > 25 gal/ton for oil shale.
- **1 % Fischer Assay yield ≈ 7.05 gal/short ton** — a key conversion shortcut.
- **Environmental issues**: tailings ponds (oil sand), spent shale disposal (oil shale), high GHG intensity (> conventional oil).
- **Modern trend**: shift from surface mining + retorting to **in-situ thermal recovery** (SAGD, ICP) to reduce environmental footprint.
- **Kerogen Type I (algal, lacustrine)** gives the **highest oil yield** — Green River is the prime example.
- **Sulfur content in bitumen is 3–5 %** — requires extensive **upgrading (coking, hydrodesulfurization)**.
- **Bitumen metals (V, Ni) at 100–500 ppm** poison refining catalysts — must be removed in upgrading.

> **End of Module 1 — Sand and Bituminous Oil Shale Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, Course Code PECHT752.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT752 - NON-CONVENTIONAL PETROLEUM RESOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Origin and Occurrence Worldwide

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Origin and Occurrence of Petroleum (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> The **origin** of petroleum refers to the geochemical and biological processes through which hydrocarbons are generated from precursor organic matter (kerogen) buried within fine-grained sedimentary source rocks over geological timescales (typically **1–500 million years**). The **occurrence** describes the global spatial distribution, geological trapping mechanisms, and reservoir-hosting lithologies of these hydrocarbons, which may accumulate either in **conventional** porous-permeable reservoirs or remain trapped in **unconventional** low-permeability source-rock systems.

The combined study of origin and occurrence forms the foundation of **petroleum system analysis**, integrating **sedimentology**, **organic geochemistry**, **structural geology**, and **basin modeling**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker of the Earth"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:**
> Think of the Earth's crust as a giant, slow pressure cooker operating over **millions of years**.
>
> - **Ingredients (Organic Matter):** Algae, plankton, and plant debris settle at the bottom of ancient seas and swamps — like vegetables layered in a pot.
> - **The Lid (Sediments):** Sand, silt, and clay layers gradually bury the organic matter, sealing it from oxygen (anoxic conditions).
> - **The Heat (Geothermal Gradient):** As burial depth increases (~**25–30 °C/km** average geothermal gradient), temperature rises slowly, "cooking" the organic matter.
> - **The Product (Hydrocarbons):** Over millions of years, heat and pressure transform the organic soup into **oil, gas, and kerogen residues**.
> - **The Serving (Migration & Trapping):** The hydrocarbons then migrate upward through porous rocks until they hit an impermeable seal — like a lid trapping steam in the cooker.
>
> **Conventional resources** = hydrocarbons that escaped and got trapped in separate reservoirs.
> **Unconventional resources** = hydrocarbons that never left the "pot" — they stayed trapped in the source rock itself.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| Average Geothermal Gradient | **25–30 °C/km** | Drives kerogen maturation |
| Oil Window Temperature | **60–120 °C** | Peak liquid hydrocarbon generation |
| Gas Window Temperature | **120–225 °C** | Thermal cracking to methane |
| Minimum Source Rock TOC | **0.5–1.0 wt%** | Threshold for hydrocarbon expulsion |
| Time-Span of Petroleum Formation | **1–500 million years** | Geological maturation duration |
| Kerogen Activation Energy (Eₐ) | **~50–80 kcal/mol** | Energy barrier for hydrocarbon generation |
| Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro) – Oil Window | **0.6–1.3 %** | Maturity indicator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Module 1 Learning Outcome (CO1):**
> *"Understand the origin, occurrence, and global distribution of non-conventional petroleum resources."* — Students must internalize that **unconventional** resources dominate remaining global reserves (~**70–80%** of total hydrocarbon endowment) when shale oil, oil sands, tight gas, gas hydrates, and coalbed methane are aggregated.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Organic Theory of Petroleum Origin (Modern Paradigm)

The **organic theory** is universally accepted today. Proposed by **Engler and Hofer (1911)**, it postulates that petroleum is derived from the thermal maturation of **kerogen** — a complex, insoluble macromolecular organic matter deposited in anoxic (oxygen-deficient) sedimentary environments.

### 2.1.1 Stages of Petroleum Generation

The transformation follows a four-stage sequential pathway:

**Stage 1 — Deposition (0–1000 m burial, <50 °C)**
- Organic matter (plankton, algae, bacteria, land plants) accumulates in fine-grained sediments.
- Under anoxic conditions, bacterial action forms **sapropel**.
- This is the **diagenesis** phase.

**Stage 2 — Kerogen Formation (1000–2500 m burial, 50–60 °C)**
- Geopolymerization transforms sapropel into insoluble **kerogen**.
- This is the end of diagenesis / beginning of **catagenesis**.

**Stage 3 — Catagenesis — Oil & Gas Generation (2500–4000 m, 60–120 °C)**
- Thermal cracking of kerogen → **bitumen → crude oil + wet gas**.
- **Main oil window** — peak hydrocarbon generation.

**Stage 4 — Metagenesis — Dry Gas (4000–6000+ m, >120 °C)**
- Secondary cracking of oil → **dry gas (methane)**.
- Beyond ~225 °C, all organics convert to **graphite** (metamorphism).

---

## 2.2 Kerogen Types and Hydrocarbon Potency

> [!NOTE]
> **Van Krevelen Diagram Concept:**
> The H/C vs O/C atomic ratio plot is the master diagnostic tool for classifying kerogen type. It determines whether a source rock is **oil-prone**, **gas-prone**, or **inert**.

| Kerogen Type | Origin (Precursor) | H/C Ratio | O/C Ratio | Hydrocarbon Potential | Example Deposits |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Type I (Algal/Sapropelic)** | Algae, lacustrine plankton | >1.5 | <0.1 | **Highly oil-prone** | Green River Shale (USA), Torbanite (Scotland) |
| **Type II (Marine/Mixed)** | Marine plankton, zooplankton | 1.0–1.5 | 0.1–0.2 | **Oil + Gas prone** | Posidonia Shale (Germany), Bakken (USA) |
| **Type III (Humic/Coaly)** | Higher land plants, woody tissue | <1.0 | 0.2–0.3 | **Gas-prone** | Coal seams, deltaic mudstones |
| **Type IV (Inert/Residual)** | Oxidized, recycled organic matter | <0.5 | Variable | **None** (dead carbon) | Highly oxidized shales |

---

## 2.3 The Inorganic Theory (Historical Note)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although largely superseded, students must know the inorganic theory for **KTU 2-mark questions**.

- **Mendeleev (1876) — Carbide Hypothesis:** Petroleum formed by reaction of **deep-Earth metallic carbides** (e.g., iron carbide) with percolating groundwater, producing acetylene → heavier hydrocarbons.
- **Berthelot (1860):** Proposed similar mineral-based synthesis.
- **Modern Re-Assessment:** Recent **abiotic methane** discoveries in **serpentinized ultramafic rocks** (e.g., Lost City hydrothermal vents) have revived interest in inorganic contributions — but quantitative significance is negligible (<0.1% of global reserves).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description & Units | Application |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$ | **Arrhenius Equation** — Kerogen maturation rate constant; A = frequency factor (s⁻¹), Eₐ = activation energy (J/mol), R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), T = temperature (K) | Predicting hydrocarbon generation timing |
| 2 | $$Ro\ \% = f(\text{Max Paleo-T})$$ | **Vitrinite Reflectance** — empirical maturity indicator (0–5% scale) | Determining oil vs gas window |
| 3 | $$TTI = \sum_{n} \Delta t_n \cdot r^{n}$$ | **Time-Temperature Index (Lopatin, 1971)** — r = 2, n = temperature index step (every 10 °C) | Burial history modeling |
| 4 | $$HC_{generated} = TOC \cdot V_{rock} \cdot HI \cdot \rho_{kerogen} / 10^6$$ | **Hydrocarbon Yield** (tons); TOC = Total Organic Carbon (wt%), HI = Hydrogen Index (mg HC/g TOC), ρ = rock density (g/cm³) | Volumetric resource estimation |
| 5 | $$Q_{expelled} = Q_{generated} - S_{retained} - S_{adsorbed}$$ | **Expulsion Efficiency** — mass balance for hydrocarbon migration | Source rock evaluation |
| 6 | $$G = \int_{t_0}^{t_1} A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT(t)} dt$$ | **Integrated Generation Kinetics** — total transformation ratio over geological time | Basin modeling simulation |
| 7 | $$\text{Saturates/Aromatics/Resins/Asphaltenes} = \text{SARA}$$ | **SARA Fractionation** — compositional classification of crude oil | Oil characterization |
| 8 | $$\rho_{hyd} = 0.91 + 0.0004 \cdot (\text{depth in m} / 100)$$ | Approximate **hydrate stability depth** (simplified) | Gas hydrate zone estimation |
| 9 | $$\text{Vitrinite}\ \% \to \text{Oil Window at}\ Ro = 0.6\%\ \text{to}\ 1.3\%$$ | Maturity boundary (no math, factual cutoff) | Kerogen stage identification |
| 10 | $$P_{pore} = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h + \sigma_{lith}$$ | Pore pressure estimation in source rock | Overpressure generation in shales |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip (KTU Valuation):**
> Always state the **Ro maturity window** (0.6–1.3% for oil; 1.3–2.0% for wet gas; 2.0–4.0% for dry gas) in any kerogen question. Examiners explicitly award **+1 mark** for this range.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Significance

- **Upstream E&P Sector:** Origin understanding dictates where exploration companies focus capital expenditure (e.g., Type II marine source rocks for shale plays).
- **Unconventional Resource Economics:** Knowing global reserves reshapes geopolitics — e.g., Canada's oil sands make it the **world's 3rd largest oil reserve holder** (after Venezuela and Saudi Arabia when oil sands are counted).
- **Climate & Energy Transition:** Recognition that **>70% of remaining fossil carbon** is in unconventional form fundamentally affects long-term CO₂ emissions scenarios.
- **Carbon Capture Synergy:** Mature organic-rich shales (overmature) are also being evaluated as **CO₂ sequestration targets**, linking origin theory to modern climate engineering.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Arrhenius-Based Kerogen Maturation Timing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style 14-mark question):**
> A Type II kerogen source rock is buried at **3500 m** depth. The regional geothermal gradient is **30 °C/km**, and the surface temperature is **20 °C**. If the activation energy for hydrocarbon generation is **Eₐ = 55 kcal/mol** and the frequency factor is **A = 1 × 10¹³ s⁻¹**, calculate:
>
> (a) The expected maturation temperature at this burial depth.
> (b) The rate constant k using the Arrhenius equation.
> (c) The time required to achieve **50% transformation ratio** of kerogen to oil.

---

### Part (a) — Temperature at 3500 m Burial Depth

The temperature at any depth in a sedimentary basin is governed by:

$$T(z) = T_{surface} + \left( \frac{dT}{dz} \right)_{grad} \cdot z$$

Where:
- $T(z)$ = Temperature at depth z (in °C)
- $T_{surface}$ = Mean surface temperature = **20 °C**
- $(dT/dz)_{grad}$ = Geothermal gradient = **30 °C/km**
- $z$ = Depth = **3500 m = 3.5 km**

Substituting the values:

$$T(z) = 20 + (30) \cdot (3.5)$$

$$T(z) = 20 + 105$$

$$\boxed{T(z) = 125\ ^\circ C}$$

---

### Part (b) — Arrhenius Rate Constant k

The Arrhenius equation relates the rate constant to absolute temperature:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R \cdot T)}$$

**Step 1 — Convert Temperature to Kelvin:**

$$T_K = 125 + 273.15 = 398.15\ K$$

**Step 2 — Convert Activation Energy to SI Units (J/mol):**

$$E_a = 55\ \text{kcal/mol} \times 4184\ \text{J/kcal} = 230{,}120\ \text{J/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Exponential Term:**

$$-\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T} = -\frac{230{,}120}{8.314 \times 398.15}$$

$$\frac{230{,}120}{8.314 \times 398.15} = \frac{230{,}120}{3309.7} = 69.53$$

$$-69.53$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Exponential:**

$$e^{-69.53}$$

This requires careful calculation. Using the identity $\ln(10) = 2.3026$:

$$\frac{69.53}{2.3026} = 30.197$$

$$e^{-69.53} = 10^{-30.197} = 6.34 \times 10^{-31}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by Frequency Factor A:**

$$k = (1 \times 10^{13}) \times (6.34 \times 10^{-31})$$

$$\boxed{k = 6.34 \times 10^{-18}\ \text{s}^{-1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This extremely small rate constant is consistent with the geological reality that hydrocarbon generation is a **slow process measured in millions of years**, not minutes. If k were large, oil would form too quickly to be preserved.

---

### Part (c) — Time to 50% Transformation

For a **first-order reaction** (valid assumption for kerogen pyrolysis):

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = k \cdot (1 - X)$$

Where X = transformation ratio (fraction of kerogen converted). Separating variables:

$$\int_0^{0.5} \frac{dX}{1-X} = \int_0^t k\, dt$$

$$-\ln(1 - X) \Big|_0^{0.5} = k \cdot t$$

$$-\ln(0.5) = k \cdot t$$

$$t = \frac{-\ln(0.5)}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k}$$

Substituting:

$$t = \frac{0.693}{6.34 \times 10^{-18}\ \text{s}^{-1}}$$

$$t = 1.093 \times 10^{17}\ \text{seconds}$$

**Converting to Years:**

$$t = \frac{1.093 \times 10^{17}}{3.156 \times 10^{7}\ \text{s/year}}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 3.46\ \text{million years (Myr)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Insight:** This result (~3.5 Myr) is geologically consistent — most prolific petroleum source rocks have been in the oil window for **5–50 Myr**, allowing for massive hydrocarbon accumulation. Show all unit conversions explicitly in KTU answers; examiners frequently deduct marks for missing unit transformations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time-Temperature Index (TTI) by Lopatin Method

**Problem:** A source rock passes through successive burial stages with temperature indices n = 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 (each step = 10 °C increment) for durations of 5, 8, 12, 15, and 10 million years respectively. Compute the TTI.

The Lopatin TTI is given by:

$$TTI = \sum_{n} \Delta t_n \cdot 2^{n}$$

Where $\Delta t_n$ is the duration spent in temperature index n, and 2 is the doubling factor per 10 °C.

| Temperature Index (n) | Temperature Range (°C) | Duration $\Delta t_n$ (Myr) | $2^n$ | Contribution $\Delta t_n \cdot 2^n$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 3 | 30–40 | 5 | 8 | 40 |
| 4 | 40–50 | 8 | 16 | 128 |
| 5 | 50–60 | 12 | 32 | 384 |
| 6 | 60–70 | 15 | 64 | 960 |
| 7 | 70–80 | 10 | 128 | 1280 |
| **SUM** | — | — | — | **TTI = 2792** |

The maturity zone is then identified:

| TTI Range | Maturity Stage | Hydrocarbon Phase |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| <10 | Immature | None |
| 10–75 | Early mature | Heavy oil |
| **75–160** | **Peak oil generation** | **Light oil** |
| 160–500 | Late mature | Wet gas + condensate |
| 500–1000 | Condensate zone | Gas-condensate |
| >1000 | Dry gas zone | Dry methane |

In this example, TTI = **2792** → source rock is in the **dry gas window** (overmature).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Hydrocarbon Yield from Source Rock

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam-Style Problem (14 marks):**
> A 10 m thick shale source rock covers an area of **50 km²**. Laboratory analysis yields **TOC = 4.0 wt%**, **Hydrogen Index (HI) = 350 mg HC/g TOC**, rock density **ρ = 2.4 g/cm³**. Compute the total generated hydrocarbon mass.

The formula is:

$$HC_{generated} = TOC \cdot V_{rock} \cdot HI \cdot \rho_{kerogen} / 10^6$$

**Step 1 — Compute Rock Volume:**

$$V_{rock} = \text{Area} \times \text{Thickness} = (50 \times 10^6\ \text{m}^2) \times (10\ \text{m})$$

$$V_{rock} = 5 \times 10^8\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Convert Volume to Mass (using density):**

$$M_{rock} = V_{rock} \times \rho = (5 \times 10^8\ \text{m}^3) \times (2.4 \times 10^6\ \text{g/m}^3)$$

$$M_{rock} = 1.2 \times 10^{15}\ \text{g}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of Organic Carbon (using TOC):**

$$M_{TOC} = M_{rock} \times (TOC/100) = (1.2 \times 10^{15}) \times 0.04$$

$$M_{TOC} = 4.8 \times 10^{13}\ \text{g} = 4.8 \times 10^{10}\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Convert TOC to Total Organic Matter (kerogen):**
Assuming kerogen contains ~75% carbon:

$$M_{kerogen} = \frac{M_{TOC}}{0.75} = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{13}}{0.75} = 6.4 \times 10^{13}\ \text{g}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Hydrogen Index (HI = 350 mg HC/g TOC):**

$$HC_{generated} = 4.8 \times 10^{13}\ \text{g TOC} \times 350\ \text{mg/g} \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kg/mg}$$

$$HC_{generated} = 4.8 \times 10^{13} \times 350 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kg}$$

$$HC_{generated} = 4.8 \times 10^{13} \times 3.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{kg}$$

$$HC_{generated} = 1.68 \times 10^{10}\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Million Tonnes (Mt):**

$$HC_{generated} = \frac{1.68 \times 10^{10}\ \text{kg}}{10^9\ \text{kg/Mt}}$$

$$\boxed{HC_{generated} \approx 16.8\ \text{million tonnes}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** For an area of 50 km² (~size of medium Indian district), this is geologically realistic. Most producing unconventional plays generate **hundreds of Mt** of hydrocarbons, supporting decades of commercial production.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Origin of Petroleum (Geological Pathway)

This diagram illustrates the complete geological pathway from organic matter deposition to hydrocarbon accumulation, with both conventional and unconventional pathways.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Organic Matter Input: Algae, Plankton, Plant Debris] --> B[Anoxic Deposition in Marine/Lacustrine Basin]
    B --> C[Diagenesis Stage: 0 to 2500m, less than 60°C]
    C --> D[Kerogen Formation Insoluble Macromolecule]
    D --> E[Catagenesis Stage: 2500 to 4000m, 60 to 120°C]
    E --> F[Main Oil Window Peak Liquid HC Generation]
    F --> G[Bitumen and Crude Oil Formation]
    D --> H[Gas Window Stage: 4000 to 6000m, 120 to 225°C]
    G --> I[Migration: Buoyant Rise Through Porous Rock]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Conventional Trapping: Anticline Fault Stratigraphic Trap]
    J --> K[Conventional Reservoir Accumulation]
    I --> L[Failed Migration: Pore Throat too Small]
    L --> M[Unconventional Accumulation: Stays in Source Rock]
    M --> N[Shale Oil, Shale Gas, Tight Oil Reservoir]
    H --> O[Continued Burial: Overmaturation]
    O --> P[Metagenesis: Dry Gas Formation]
    P --> Q[Methane Accumulates in Tight Formations]
    H --> R[Low Temperature Thermogenic or Microbial Path]
    R --> S[Biogenic Methane: Coalbed Methane Source]
```
