# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

### Question A.1 — Definition Question
**"Differentiate between Kerogen Types I, II, and III with respect to their origin and hydrocarbon generating potential." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Kerogen is the insoluble organic matter in sedimentary rocks and is classified into four types based on **H/C and O/C atomic ratios** (Van Krevelen diagram).

- **Type I:** Derived from **lacustrine algae**; H/C ratio >1.5, low O/C. Highly **oil-prone** (e.g., Green River Shale, USA).
- **Type II:** Derived from **marine plankton**; H/C ratio 1.0–1.5. **Oil and gas-prone** (e.g., Bakken Shale, Posidonia Shale).
- **Type III:** Derived from **terrestrial higher plants (humic)**; H/C <1.0, O/C >0.2. Mainly **gas-prone** (e.g., coal-bearing formations, CBM source rocks).

(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for table-style classification + 1 mark for examples)

---

### Question A.2 — Conceptual Short Answer
**"What is the significance of Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro) in determining petroleum generation windows?" (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro) is the percentage of incident light reflected from the polished surface of **vitrinite** (a coal maceral derived from woody plant tissue) embedded in source rocks. It is the **most widely used thermal maturity indicator** in petroleum exploration.

- Ro < **0.6%** → **Immature** (no significant hydrocarbon generation).
- Ro **0.6–1.3%** → **Oil window** (peak liquid hydrocarbon generation).
- Ro **1.3–2.0%** → **Gas window** (wet and dry gas generation).
- Ro > **2.0%** → **Overmature** (only dry methane; eventually graphite).

(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for numerical boundaries + 1 mark for geological interpretation)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU End-Semester Examination — Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### ❓ Question B-A (14 Marks) — Origin and Maturation Focus

**[KTU University Exam — December 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **organic theory of petroleum origin**. Discuss the various stages of kerogen maturation with a clearly labelled **burial depth vs temperature** schematic. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Type II source rock contains **TOC = 5.0 wt%** and **HI = 400 mg HC/g TOC**. The rock is buried at **3000 m** depth in a basin with geothermal gradient **28 °C/km** and surface temperature **18 °C**. Using the **Lopatin TTI method**, determine whether the rock has reached the oil window. Assume the rock has spent equal time (10 Myr each) in temperature indices n = 3, 4, 5, 6, 7. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

The **organic theory** states that petroleum is generated from the **thermal decomposition of kerogen** (insoluble organic matter) accumulated in fine-grained sedimentary source rocks over geological timescales.

**Four Stages of Maturation:**

1. **Deposition and Diagenesis (0–2500 m, <60 °C):** Organic matter accumulates under anoxic conditions. Bacterial activity forms sapropel. Geopolymerization produces kerogen. Ro < 0.6%.

2. **Catagenesis — Oil Generation (2500–4000 m, 60–120 °C):** Thermal cracking of kerogen produces **bitumen → crude oil + wet gas**. This is the **main oil window** (Ro = 0.6–1.3%).

3. **Late Catagenesis — Gas Generation (4000–5500 m, 120–200 °C):** Secondary cracking of oil and kerogen produces **wet gas, then dry gas (methane)**. Ro = 1.3–2.0%.

4. **Metagenesis (>5500 m, >200 °C):** Residual organics convert to **graphite** via metamorphism. No further hydrocarbon generation.

**Key Burial Curve Schematic (text version for answer script):**

```
Depth (m)       Ro%      Maturity
─────────────────────────────────
0       ─────────────────── Surface
        0.4   ─── Immature
2500    ─────────────────── Diagenesis End
        0.6   ─── Oil Window Start
3500    ─────────────────── Peak Oil
        1.0   ───
4000    ─────────────────── Oil Window End
        1.3   ─── Wet Gas
4500    ─────────────────── 
        1.6   ─── Gas Window
5500    ─────────────────── Dry Gas
        2.0   ─── Overmature
>5500   ─────────────────── Metamorphism
```

(Mark distribution: 2 marks for theory + 2 marks for stages + 2 marks for curve + 1 mark for Ro values)

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1 — Calculate Subsurface Temperature:**

$$T = 18 + (28 \times 3) = 18 + 84 = 102\ ^\circ C$$

This temperature falls within the **oil window** (60–120 °C).

**Step 2 — Calculate TTI using Lopatin Equation:**

$$TTI = \sum \Delta t_n \cdot 2^n$$

| Index n | $\Delta t_n$ (Myr) | $2^n$ | Contribution |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 3 | 10 | 8 | 80 |
| 4 | 10 | 16 | 160 |
| 5 | 10 | 32 | 320 |
| 6 | 10 | 64 | 640 |
| 7 | 10 | 128 | 1280 |
| **Total** | — | — | **TTI = 2480** |

**Step 3 — Interpret TTI:**

According to Lopatin's maturity classification:
- TTI 75–160 → Peak Oil
- TTI 160–500 → Late mature (wet gas)
- TTI 500–1000 → Condensate
- TTI > 1000 → Dry gas zone

Computed **TTI = 2480** >> 1000 → **Overmature Dry Gas Window**

**Conclusion:** Despite the current temperature (102 °C) being within the oil window, the integrated thermal history (TTI = 2480) indicates the rock is **overmature and lies in the dry gas zone**.

**Note on TOC and HI:** The given TOC = 5.0 wt% and HI = 400 mg HC/g TOC indicate an **excellent source rock** (industry threshold for good source: TOC > 2%, HI > 200). However, the maturity state determines what hydrocarbon phase is present — in this case, only **dry methane** would have been generated.

(Mark distribution: 2 marks temperature calc + 3 marks TTI table + 1 mark interpretation + 1 mark conclusion)

---

### ❓ Question B-B (14 Marks) — Worldwide Occurrence Focus

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss the **global occurrence and distribution** of the three most important unconventional petroleum resources, citing specific formation names, host countries, and estimated reserves. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Oil Sands (Canada)**, **Oil Shale (USA Green River)**, and **Shale Gas (USA Marcellus)** in terms of geological age, lithology, kerogen type, recovery method, and estimated reserves using a structured analytical table. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

The three most significant unconventional petroleum resources globally are:

**1. Oil Sands (Heavy Oil / Bitumen)**
- **Major Locations:** Athabasca, Peace River, Cold Lake (Alberta, **Canada**); Orinoco Belt (**Venezuela**).
- **Estimated Global Reserves:** ~**1.7–2.4 trillion barrels** of bitumen in place.
- **Geology:** Unconsolidated Cretaceous sands saturated with extra-heavy oil (API 6–10°). Hydrocarbons formed from **Type II marine kerogen** but biodegraded in-situ to bitumen due to shallow burial and meteoric water influx.
- **Significance:** Canada ranks **3rd globally** in total oil reserves when oil sands are included (after Venezuela).

**2. Oil Shale**
- **Major Locations:** Green River Formation (Wyoming/Utah/Colorado, **USA**); Fushun and Maoming (**China**); Kukersite (**Estonia**); Timahdit (**Morocco**).
- **Estimated Global Reserves:** ~**3 trillion barrels** of shale oil equivalent (in-place).
- **Geology:** Fine-grained lacustrine/marine sedimentary rocks (marlstones, dolomitic shales) rich in **Type I/II kerogen**. Hydrocarbons generated but **not expelled** — they remain bound within the rock matrix.
- **Recovery:** Requires **in-situ retorting** (e.g., Shell's In-situ Conversion Process at 350–400 °C) or **ex-situ mining + surface retorting**.

**3. Shale Gas (and Shale Oil)**
- **Major Locations:** Marcellus and Barnett (**USA**); Sichuan Basin (**China**); Vaca Muerta (**Argentina**); Bakken (**USA**, shale oil).
- **Estimated Global Reserves:** ~**7,000+ Tcf** gas; **350+ Bb** oil.
- **Geology:** Organic-rich **Type II marine shales** (e.g., Marcellus is Middle Devonian, ~390 Ma). Thermogenic methane generated and retained within the nanoporous shale matrix.
- **Recovery:** Horizontal drilling + **multi-stage hydraulic fracturing** ("fracking") to create artificial permeability.

(Mark distribution: 1 mark per resource introduction + 1 mark geology + 1 mark recovery + 1 mark global context)

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

| Parameter | Oil Sands (Athabasca, Canada) | Oil Shale (Green River, USA) | Shale Gas (Marcellus, USA) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Resource Type** | Bitumen from sand-hosted reservoir | Kerogen-rich shale (solid organic matter) | Thermogenic methane in shale |
| **Geological Age** | Early Cretaceous (~110 Ma) | Eocene (~50 Ma) | Middle Devonian (~390 Ma) |
| **Host Lithology** | Unconsolidated quartz sand + bitumen | Lacustrine dolomitic marlstone | Black marine organic shale |
| **Burial Depth** | 0–750 m (surface-mineable) | Outcrop to 2000 m | 1200–2700 m |
| **Kerogen Type** | Type II degraded (biodegraded) | Type I lacustrine algal | Type II marine planktonic |
| **API Gravity** | 6–10° (extra-heavy) | ~30° (after surface retorting) | N/A (gaseous) |
| **Estimated Reserves** | ~170 Bb recoverable | ~1.5–2.0 trillion bbl in-place | ~500 Tcf recoverable |
| **Recovery Method** | Surface mining + SAGD (Steam-Assisted Gravity Drainage) | Surface retorting, in-situ heating (ICP) | Horizontal drilling + multistage hydraulic fracturing |
| **Key Technical Challenge** | High water usage, tailings management, GHG emissions | Energy-intensive retorting, water content | Frac water management, induced seismicity |
| **Commercial Status** | Mature, major contributor to Canadian economy | Pilot/early commercial | Fully commercial, revolutionizing US energy |

(Mark distribution: 1 mark intro + 3 marks table completeness + 2 marks comparison interpretation + 1 mark conclusion)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting numerical Ro boundaries** (0.6%, 1.3%, 2.0%) — examiners explicitly state "write all four boundaries" → losing **2 marks** if skipped.
> 2. **Confusing kerogen types** — many students wrongly classify **Type II as only gas-prone**. Correct answer: Type II is **oil AND gas prone**.
> 3. **Unit conversion mistakes in Arrhenius problems** — Eₐ must be in **J/mol** (not kcal/mol) and T must be in **Kelvin**. Mark deduction: **1.5 marks** per error.
> 4. **Not labelling Mermaid/schematics** — for diagram questions, failing to label **depth axis (m)** and **temperature axis (°C)** leads to -1 mark.
> 5. **In TTI calculation** — students often forget the **2ⁿ factor** or compute $2^n$ as ordinary doubling rather than exponential. Always write $2^3 = 8$, $2^4 = 16$, etc., explicitly.
> 6. **Geographic specificity** — listing "USA has shale gas" earns only 50% marks. Always cite **specific formations** (Marcellus, Barnett) — examiners reward specificity with **+1 mark bonus**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Origin and Occurrence Worldwide**

### 🔹 Origin Fundamentals
- ✅ Petroleum = **thermal decomposition product of kerogen** (organic theory, Engler-Hofer 1911).
- ✅ Kerogen = insoluble macromolecular organic matter in source rocks.
- ✅ Four maturation stages: **Diagenesis → Catagenesis → Metagenesis → Metamorphism**.
- ✅ Maturity indicator: **Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro)** — boundary values 0.6%, 1.3%, 2.0%.
- ✅ Alternative indicator: **Time-Temperature Index (TTI, Lopatin method)**.

### 🔹 Kerogen Classification
- ✅ **Type I** = lacustrine algal → **highly oil-prone** (H/C >1.5).
- ✅ **Type II** = marine planktonic → **oil + gas prone** (H/C 1.0–1.5).
- ✅ **Type III** = terrestrial humic → **gas-prone** (H/C <1.0).
- ✅ **Type IV** = oxidized/recycled → **inert, no hydrocarbon potential**.

### 🔹 Generation Kinetics
- ✅ **Arrhenius Equation:** $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ — governs reaction rate.
- ✅ Typical Eₐ ≈ **50–80 kcal/mol** for kerogen cracking.
- ✅ Time to 50% conversion = **0.693/k** (first-order kinetics).
- ✅ Oil window temperature: **60–120 °C**; Gas window: **120–225 °C**.

### 🔹 Global Occurrence — Must-Memorize Reserves
- ✅ **Oil Sands:** Athabasca (Canada), Orinoco (Venezuela) → ~**1.7–2.4 trillion bbl**.
- ✅ **Oil Shale:** Green River (USA), Fushun (China) → ~**3 trillion bbl in-place**.
- ✅ **Shale Gas:** Marcellus (USA), Sichuan (China) → ~**7,000+ Tcf**.
- ✅ **Coalbed Methane:** Powder River (USA), Jharia (India) → ~**7,000–9,000 Tcf**.
- ✅ **Gas Hydrates:** Nankai Trough (Japan), KG Basin (India) → ~**10,000–20,000 Gt C**.

### 🔹 Production Methods
- ✅ Oil Sands: **SAGD (Steam-Assisted Gravity Drainage)** + Surface Mining.
- ✅ Oil Shale: **In-situ retorting** (Shell ICP, Estonian Galoter process).
- ✅ Shale Gas: **Horizontal drilling + multi-stage hydraulic fracturing**.
- ✅ CBM: **Degasification wells + reservoir pressure reduction**.
- ✅ Gas Hydrates: **Depressurization + CO₂-CH₄ exchange**.

### 🔹 KTU Numerical Must-Knows
- ✅ $T(z) = T_0 + (dT/dz) \cdot z$ — subsurface temperature calculation.
- ✅ $TTI = \sum \Delta t_n \cdot 2^n$ — Lopatin method.
- ✅ $HC_{generated} = TOC \cdot V_{rock} \cdot HI$ — source rock yield.
- ✅ $t_{50\%} = 0.693/k$ — first-order reaction half-life.
- ✅ Industry thresholds: **TOC > 2% (good source)**, **HI > 200 mg HC/g TOC**.

### 🔹 Critical Definitions for 1-Mark Questions
- ✅ **Sapropel** → anaerobic decomposition product of organic matter.
- ✅ **Bitumen** → soluble intermediate product of kerogen cracking.
- ✅ **Catagenesis** → thermal cracking phase (oil + gas generation).
- ✅ **Expulsion** → migration of hydrocarbons out of source rock.
- ✅ **Primary Migration** → movement from source rock; **Secondary Migration** → movement within carrier bed to trap.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT752 - NON-CONVENTIONAL PETROLEUM RESOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Non-Conventional Petroleum Resources?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> **Non-conventional petroleum resources** are naturally occurring hydrocarbon deposits that require enhanced extraction, recovery, or conversion techniques beyond standard primary production methods used for conventional crude oil and natural gas. These resources are typically found in low-permeability formations, trapped in solid/semi-solid form, or exist as highly viscous fluids that do not flow readily to a wellbore under natural reservoir pressure.

In simpler terms, think of a conventional oil reservoir as **water held in a sponge** — it drips out easily when squeezed. Non-conventional petroleum is more like **chocolate syrup frozen inside a solid block of ice** — you cannot just pump it out; you have to melt the ice, fracture the block, or dissolve the syrup first to recover it.

## 1.2 The Conventional vs. Non-Conventional Spectrum

| Feature | Conventional | Non-Conventional |
|---|---|---|
| **Mobility** | Flows naturally to wellbore | Requires stimulation/heat/solvents |
| **Permeability** | > 1 mD (milli-Darcy) | < 0.1 mD (often nano-Darcy) |
| **API Gravity** | Light/medium (20°–45° API) | Heavy/extra-heavy (< 20° API) or solid |
| **Extraction Cost** | Low (USD 20–40/bbl) | High (USD 40–80+/bbl) |
| **Viscosity** | < 10 cP at reservoir conditions | > 50 cP (up to millions for bitumen) |
| **Geological Setting** | Trapped in structural/stratigraphic traps | Source-rock or low-perm reservoirs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations:**
> The defining *technical* boundary between conventional and non-conventional is the **economic producibility** using standard vertical wells with natural drive mechanisms. If you need horizontal drilling, hydraulic fracturing, steam injection, or in-situ upgrading — it is non-conventional.

## 1.3 Major Categories of Non-Conventional Petroleum Resources

The syllabus recognizes the following six major classes:

1. **Heavy Oil** — API gravity 10°–20°, viscosity 50–10,000 cP
2. **Extra-Heavy Oil / Natural Bitumen** — API gravity < 10°, viscosity > 10,000 cP (e.g., Athabasca Oil Sands, Canada)
3. **Oil Shales** — Fine-grained sedimentary rocks containing **kerogen** (solid organic matter) that yields oil upon pyrolysis
4. **Coal Bed Methane (CBM)** — Methane adsorbed on coal seams; produced by dewatering
5. **Shale Gas / Tight Gas** — Natural gas trapped in shale or low-perm sandstone formations
6. **Gas Hydrates** — Ice-like crystalline compounds where methane molecules are trapped in water cages at low temperature and high pressure

## 1.4 Real-World Analogy: The Hydrocarbon "Ease-of-Drinking" Model

Imagine a glass of water on a table:

- **Conventional oil** = Drinking water through a normal straw (easy, free flow).
- **Heavy oil** = Drinking a thick milkshake (need to suck harder).
- **Oil sand bitumen** = Trying to drink frozen ice cream (need to melt it first with heat = steam injection).
- **Oil shale** = The "milk" is chemically bound to the cereal — you have to heat the whole bowl to extract it (pyrolysis/surface retorting).
- **Shale gas** = Tiny air bubbles trapped inside a sealed rubber ball — you must puncture the ball in many places (fracturing) to release them.
- **Gas hydrates** = Methane molecules frozen in ice cubes — you must depressurize or warm the cubes to release the trapped gas.

## 1.5 Standard Global Metrics (Bold for KTU Recall)

- **1 barrel (bbl)** = **0.158987 m³** = **42 US gallons**
- **1 tonne of oil** ≈ **7.33 bbl** (for 35° API crude)
- **Standard Temperature & Pressure (STP)** = 15.56 °C (60 °F) and 101.325 kPa (14.7 psi)
- **Surface conversion factor for OOIP:** **7758** (acre-ft × %φ × (1−Sw)/Bo → reservoir bbl)
- **Surface conversion factor for OGIP:** **43,560** (acre-ft conversion)
- **API Gravity formula:** °API = (141.5 / SG) − 131.5
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Resource Classification Frameworks (Why They Matter)

A petroleum engineer must be able to differentiate between **resources** (total in-place quantities) and **reserves** (recoverable quantities under current economics and technology). The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests this through module-1 questions.

### 2.1.1 McKelvey Diagram (USGS, 1972) — Conceptual Foundation

The McKelvey diagram is a **2-axis matrix** mapping resources by:

- **Vertical Axis (Y-axis):** Degree of geological assurance (from "Identified" to "Hypothetical" to "Speculative")
- **Horizontal Axis (X-axis):** Degree of economic feasibility (from "Economic" to "Marginal" to "Sub-Economic")

The vertical axis is further divided into:
- **Reserves** (Identified + Economic)
- **Marginal Reserves** (Identified + Marginal)
- **Subeconomic Resources** (Identified + Subeconomic)
- **Inferred Resources** (Discovered but uneconomic)

### 2.1.2 UNFC (United Nations Framework Classification)

The UNFC uses three criteria evaluated simultaneously:

$$\text{Resource Category} = f(\text{Environmental-Socio-Economic Viability}, \text{Technical Feasibility}, \text{Level of Confidence})$$

Each axis is classified by a numerical code (1, 2, 3) where **1 = high confidence / viable** and **3 = low confidence / not viable**.

### 2.1.3 SPE-PRMS (Society of Petroleum Engineers – Petroleum Resources Management System)

The most widely used modern classification in industry, using a **3×3 matrix** of:
- **Status:** Producing, Developed, Undeveloped
- **Commerciality:** Commercial, Sub-Commercial
- **Chance of Commerciality:** Low Estimate (1P), Best Estimate (2P), High Estimate (3P)

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Classification Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Question:** *How much oil/gas is actually there, and how confident are we that we can extract it economically?*

This produces a hierarchy:

$$\boxed{\text{Total Resource} \geq \text{Discovered Resource} \geq \text{Reserve}}$$

- **Resource** = All hydrocarbons in the Earth's crust (theoretical upper bound)
- **Discovered Resource** = Confirmed by drilling and testing
- **Reserve** = Discovered + economically recoverable with current technology

## 2.3 Recovery Factor: The Bridge Between In-Place and Recoverable

> **Recovery Factor (RF)** is the fraction of the Original Oil/Gas In Place (OOIP/OGIP) that can be extracted. It is the most critical parameter separating *resource* from *reserve*.

For non-conventional resources, RF is typically much lower than conventional:

| Resource Type | Typical RF | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Conventional Oil | **30% – 50%** | Good permeability, water/gas drive |
| Heavy Oil (Cold) | **5% – 15%** | High viscosity limits flow |
| Oil Sands (SAGD/CSS) | **20% – 60%** | Steam reduces viscosity |
| Shale Gas | **15% – 35%** | Limited by fracture network extent |
| CBM | **40% – 70%** | Desorption of methane from coal matrix |
| Gas Hydrates | **~10% – 50%** | Limited by depressurization radius |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY KTU Formula Table — Memorize All Entries**

| Symbol | Formula Name | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| OOIP (Oil) | Volumetric Oil In Place | $7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1-S_w) \,/\, B_{oi}$ | **STB** (stock tank barrels) | All oil-bearing reservoirs |
| OOIP (Oil, Field Units) | Standard Volumetric | $A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1-S_w) \,/\, (5.615 \cdot B_{oi})$ | **STB** | When A in acres, h in ft |
| OOIP (Oil, SI Units) | SI Volumetric | $A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1-S_w) \,/\, B_{oi}$ | **m³** | When A in m², h in m, B in m³/m³ |
| OGIP (Gas) | Volumetric Gas In Place | $43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1-S_w) \,/\, B_{gi}$ | **scf** | Gas reservoirs |
| OGIP (Gas, SI) | SI Volumetric Gas | $A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1-S_w) \,/\, B_{gi}$ | **m³ at STP** | SI units |
| Recoverable Reserve | Ultimate Recovery | $\text{OOIP} \cdot R_F$ | **STB or m³** | Both oil and gas |
| API Gravity | API Conversion | $\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ | **dimensionless** | Density classification |
| Bitumen in Place | Oil-Sand Bitumen | $A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1-S_w) \cdot \rho_b \,/\, 100$ | **tonnes** | Alberta/Athabasca |
| CBM Gas In Place | Coal Volume Method | $A \cdot h \cdot \rho_c \cdot G_c$ | **scf or m³** | Volumetric CBM estimate |
| Gas Hydrate | STP Volume | $V \cdot \phi \cdot S_h \cdot E \cdot 164$ | **m³ CH₄ at STP** | Marine hydrate, per m³ of sediment |
| Resource Triangle | Conceptual Model | $\log(\text{Cumulative Production}) \propto \log(\text{Grade})$ | — | Visualization tool |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Conversion Factors:**
> - **7758** appears in the OOIP equation when Area is in **acres**, thickness in **ft**, porosity in **fraction**, and Bo in **rbbl/STB**.
> - **43,560** = number of ft² in 1 acre.
> - **5.615** = number of ft³ in 1 barrel.
> - These constants combine as: $43{,}560 \div 5.615 \approx 7{,}758$

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Formulas

- **Country-level planning:** USGS uses the volumetric method to estimate global resource endowment.
- **Company valuation:** Reserves × Net Present Value (NPV) determines asset value.
- **Government policy:** Resource classification drives royalty and taxation frameworks.
- **Investment decisions:** Distinguishes 1P reserves (bankable) from prospective resources (speculative).

## 2.6 The "Resource Triangle" — A Foundational Concept

The **Resource Triangle** is a visualization model showing that:

- **High-quality, easy-to-extract resources** are **small in volume** but **high in concentration** (top of triangle).
- **Low-quality, hard-to-extract resources** are **abundant in volume** but **dispersed in concentration** (bottom of triangle).

This is why **non-conventional resources** (bottom of triangle) — though vast in quantity — are economically marginal without technology.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Volumetric OOIP Equation (Foundation Equation)

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU module-1. We will derive it from first principles.

### Step 1: Start With the Bulk Rock Volume

The bulk volume of the reservoir rock is the product of the **area** and the **thickness** (height) of the pay zone:

$$V_b = A \cdot h$$

where:
- $V_b$ = bulk rock volume
- $A$ = reservoir area (acre or m²)
- $h$ = average net pay thickness (ft or m)

### Step 2: Account for Porosity to Get the Pore Volume

Only the **pore space** contains fluids. Porosity ($\phi$) is the fraction of bulk volume that is void:

$$V_p = V_b \cdot \phi = A \cdot h \cdot \phi$$

where $V_p$ is the pore volume.

### Step 3: Subtract Connate Water to Get the Hydrocarbon Pore Volume

Not all pore space contains hydrocarbons — some is occupied by **connate (irreducible) water** with saturation $S_w$:

$$V_{HC} = V_p \cdot (1 - S_w) = A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)$$

where $V_{HC}$ is the hydrocarbon pore volume (HCVP).

### Step 4: Convert Reservoir-Conditions Volume to Surface-Conditions Volume

The oil occupies a smaller volume at surface conditions than at reservoir conditions because dissolved gas evolves out. The ratio of reservoir volume to surface volume is the **oil formation volume factor**, $B_{oi}$ (rbbl/STB — reservoir barrels per stock-tank barrel):

$$V_{oil, \text{surface}} = \dfrac{V_{HC}}{B_{oi}} = \dfrac{A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{oi}}$$

### Step 5: Apply the Unit Conversion to Oilfield Field Units

In KTU examinations, the standard field-unit equation is:

$$N = \dfrac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{oi}}$$

where $N$ = OOIP in **STB** (stock-tank barrels).

**Derivation of the constant 7758:**

$$\text{Constant} = \dfrac{43{,}560 \, \text{ft}^2/\text{acre}}{5.615 \, \text{ft}^3/\text{bbl}} = 7{,}758 \, \text{bbl/(acre·ft)}$$

**Final Aligned Equation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{OOIP} &= 7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w) \cdot \dfrac{1}{B_{oi}} \\
\text{Units:} \quad \text{STB} &= \dfrac{\text{bbl}}{\text{acre·ft}} \times \text{acre} \times \text{ft} \times (-) \times (-) \times \dfrac{\text{STB}}{\text{rbbl}}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Full Worked Example: Estimating OOIP for a Heavy Oil Reservoir

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU 14-Mark Style):**
> A heavy oil reservoir in Upper Assam has the following properties:
> - Area: $A = 1500$ acres
> - Net pay thickness: $h = 40$ ft
> - Average porosity: $\phi = 0.22$
> - Connate water saturation: $S_w = 0.30$
> - Oil formation volume factor: $B_{oi} = 1.15$ rbbl/STB
> - Recovery factor: $R_F = 0.12$ (typical for cold heavy-oil production)
>
> **Calculate:** (a) Original Oil In Place (OOIP); (b) Recoverable Reserve.

### Solution — Part (a): OOIP Calculation

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{OOIP} &= 7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w) \cdot \dfrac{1}{B_{oi}} \\
&= 7758 \times 1500 \times 40 \times 0.22 \times (1 - 0.30) \times \dfrac{1}{1.15} \\
&= 7758 \times 1500 = 11{,}637{,}000 \\
&\times 40 = 465{,}480{,}000 \\
&\times 0.22 = 102{,}405{,}600 \\
&\times 0.70 = 71{,}683{,}920 \\
&\div 1.15 = 62{,}334{,}713 \, \text{STB} \\
&\approx 62.33 \times 10^6 \, \text{STB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer (a):** $\text{OOIP} \approx 62.33$ **MMSTB** (Million Stock Tank Barrels)

### Solution — Part (b): Recoverable Reserve

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Recoverable Reserve} &= \text{OOIP} \times R_F \\
&= 62{,}334{,}713 \times 0.12 \\
&= 7{,}480{,}166 \, \text{STB} \\
&\approx 7.48 \, \text{MMSTB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer (b):** Recoverable Reserve $\approx 7.48$ **MMSTB**

## 3.3 Derivation: Bitumen In-Place for Oil Sands (Surface-Mining Case)

For oil sands amenable to surface mining, we estimate the **mass of bitumen** rather than volume.

### Step 1: Compute the Bitumen Mass Fraction

The bitumen mass per unit reservoir volume is:

$$m_{bit} = \rho_{rock} \cdot \phi \cdot S_o \cdot \rho_{bit, \, R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\rho_{bit, \, S}}$$

where:
- $\rho_{rock}$ = density of oil-sand bulk (typically 2.0–2.2 g/cm³)
- $S_o$ = oil saturation in oil sand (≈ 1 − $S_w$)
- $\rho_{bit, R}$ = bitumen density at reservoir conditions
- $\rho_{bit, S}$ = bitumen density at surface (lower due to gas exsolution)

### Step 2: The Simplified "Weight-Percent" Method

A more practical engineering form uses the **bitumen weight fraction** ($w_{bit}$):

$$\text{Bitumen In Place (tonnes)} = A \cdot h \cdot \rho_{sand} \cdot w_{bit}$$

**Numerical Example (Indian context — hypothetical Upper Assam deposit):**

Given:
- $A = 5 \times 10^6$ m²
- $h = 30$ m
- $\rho_{sand} = 2150$ kg/m³
- $w_{bit} = 0.08$ (8% by weight)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Bitumen In Place} &= 5 \times 10^6 \times 30 \times 2150 \times 0.08 \\
&= 5 \times 10^6 \times 30 = 1.5 \times 10^8 \, \text{m}^3 \text{ of sand} \\
&\times 2150 = 3.225 \times 10^{11} \, \text{kg of sand} \\
&\times 0.08 = 2.58 \times 10^{10} \, \text{kg of bitumen} \\
&= 2.58 \times 10^7 \, \text{tonnes of bitumen} \\
&= 25.8 \, \text{million tonnes of bitumen}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion to barrels** (assuming bitumen density = 1010 kg/m³ ≈ 8.0 bbl/tonne):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Volume} &= 2.58 \times 10^7 \times 8.0 \\
&= 2.064 \times 10^8 \, \text{bbl} \\
&\approx 206 \, \text{MMbbl (million barrels)}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Resource Parameters by Type

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a high-yield KTU exam resource — memorize the bold values:**

| Parameter | Heavy Oil | Bitumen | Oil Shale | CBM | Shale Gas | Gas Hydrate |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **API Gravity (°)** | **10–20** | **< 10** | N/A (kerogen) | N/A | N/A | N/A |
| **Viscosity (cP)** | 50–10,000 | **> 10,000** | Solid | N/A | N/A | N/A |
| **Porosity (%)** | 25–35 | 25–35 | 4–15 | 1–5 | 2–10 | < 5 |
| **Permeability (mD)** | 10–1000 | 100–5000 | < 0.01 | 1–100 | 0.0001–0.001 | ~0 |
| **Gas Content** | — | — | — | **100–800 scf/ton** | 50–300 scf/ton | 150 m³/m³ |
| **Depth (m)** | 200–2000 | 0–750 | Surface–2000 | 200–1500 | 1000–4000 | 300–2000 (marine) |
| **Typical RF (%)** | **5–15** | **20–60** (with steam) | 50–90 (retorting) | **40–70** | **15–35** | 10–50 |
| **Primary Recovery Method** | CHOPS, CSS | SAGD, Mining | Surface retorting | Dewatering | Multistage fracturing | Depressurization |

**Legend:** CHOPS = Cold Heavy Oil Production with Sand; CSS = Cyclic Steam Stimulation; SAGD = Steam-Assisted Gravity Drainage.

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Shale Gas Volumetric Estimate

> **Problem:** A shale gas formation has $A = 80{,}000$ acres, $h = 200$ ft, $\phi = 0.08$, $S_w = 0.30$, $B_{gi} = 0.005$ rbbl/scf, RF = 0.20.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{OGIP} &= 43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w) \cdot \dfrac{1}{B_{gi}} \\
&= 43560 \times 80000 \times 200 \times 0.08 \times 0.70 \times \dfrac{1}{0.005} \\
&\approx 7.81 \times 10^{12} \, \text{scf} \\
&= 7.81 \, \text{TCF (Trillion Cubic Feet)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Recoverable Reserve} &= 7.81 \times 10^{12} \times 0.20 \\
&= 1.562 \times 10^{12} \, \text{scf} \\
&\approx 1.56 \, \text{TCF}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Approximately 1.56 TCF of recoverable shale gas — roughly the annual consumption of a mid-sized industrial country.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
