# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

> **Q1.** Define non-conventional petroleum resources. List any **four** major categories with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Non-conventional petroleum resources are hydrocarbon deposits that cannot be produced economically by ordinary primary production methods (vertical wells with natural drive) and require advanced recovery techniques such as thermal stimulation, hydraulic fracturing, or mining.

> **Four Categories (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Heavy / Extra-Heavy Oil** — e.g., Boscan field, Venezuela
> 2. **Oil Sands / Natural Bitumen** — e.g., Athabasca Oil Sands, Alberta, Canada
> 3. **Oil Shale** — e.g., Green River Formation, USA
> 4. **Coal Bed Methane (CBM)** — e.g., Raniganj Basin, India
> 5. **Shale Gas** — e.g., Marcellus Shale, USA
> 6. **Gas Hydrates** — e.g., KG Basin, India / Nankai Trough, Japan

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **oil shale** (kerogen-bearing rock) with **shale oil** (liquid trapped in shale) and **shale gas** (gas trapped in shale). In KTU answers, always clarify which one you mean.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]

> **Q2.** Differentiate between **resource** and **reserve** with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Resource | Reserve |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | All hydrocarbons estimated to exist in the Earth's crust, discovered or undiscovered | Portion of the resource that is **discovered, recoverable, and economically producible** under current technology and prices |
| **Confidence** | Low to moderate | High (proved reserve) |
| **Example** | All oil present in the Barmer Basin (Rajasthan), including speculative and undiscovered volumes | The cumulative production + booked reserves of Mangala field, Rajasthan |

> **Key Statement (1 mark):** *All reserves are resources, but not all resources are reserves.* A reserve is the **economic subset** of a discovered resource.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module-1 Style]

> **Q.A.** *(a)* Explain the **McKelvey Diagram** for resource classification with a neat sketch and describe how the **SPE-PRMS classification** differentiates between 1P, 2P, and 3P reserves. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A heavy oil reservoir has the following data:
> - Area = 2000 acres
> - Net pay = 35 ft
> - Porosity = 0.25
> - Connate water saturation = 0.35
> - $B_{oi}$ = 1.20 rbbl/STB
> - Recovery factor = 0.10
>
> Calculate the **OOIP** and the **recoverable oil reserve**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**McKelvey Diagram (3 marks):**

The McKelvey diagram (1972, USGS) classifies mineral and energy resources along two perpendicular axes:

- **Vertical axis (degree of geological assurance):** ranges from "Identified" (high confidence, based on drilling) to "Speculative" (low confidence, extrapolated geological data).
- **Horizontal axis (degree of economic feasibility):** ranges from "Economic" (profitable now) to "Marginal" to "Subeconomic" (technically recoverable but not yet profitable).

The diagram produces the following categories:
- **Reserves** = Identified + Economic
- **Marginal Reserves** = Identified + Marginal
- **Subeconomic Resources** = Identified + Subeconomic
- **Inferred / Hypothetical / Speculative** = Undiscovered + varying economic prospects

**SPE-PRMS Classification (4 marks):**

The Society of Petroleum Engineers – Petroleum Resources Management System introduces a **probabilistic reserve estimation** scheme:

- **1P (Proved Reserves):** $P_{90}$ — 90% probability that the actual reserve is **at least** this value. **Low estimate.**
- **2P (Proved + Probable Reserves):** $P_{50}$ — 50% probability. **Best estimate.**
- **3P (Proved + Probable + Possible Reserves):** $P_{10}$ — 10% probability. **High estimate.**

> The relationship is always: $\text{1P} \leq \text{2P} \leq \text{3P}$

The PRMS also differentiates:
- **Developed Producing** (currently on production)
- **Developed Non-Producing** (completed but shut-in)
- **Undeveloped** (drilled but not yet completed, or undrilled)

**Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{OOIP} &= 7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w) \cdot \dfrac{1}{B_{oi}} \\
&= 7758 \times 2000 \times 35 \times 0.25 \times (1 - 0.35) \times \dfrac{1}{1.20} \\
&= 7758 \times 2000 = 15{,}516{,}000 \\
&\times 35 = 543{,}060{,}000 \\
&\times 0.25 = 135{,}765{,}000 \\
&\times 0.65 = 88{,}247{,}250 \\
&\div 1.20 = 73{,}539{,}375 \, \text{STB} \\
&\approx 73.54 \, \text{MMSTB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Recoverable Reserve:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Reserve} &= \text{OOIP} \times R_F = 73{,}539{,}375 \times 0.10 \\
&= 7{,}353{,}937.5 \, \text{STB} \\
&\approx 7.35 \, \text{MMSTB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answers:** OOIP = **73.54 MMSTB**; Recoverable Reserve = **7.35 MMSTB**

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Module-1 Style]

> **Q.B.** *(a)* Discuss the **Resource Triangle** concept. How does it justify the economic interest in non-conventional petroleum resources despite their higher extraction costs? *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* An oil shale deposit occupies an area of $10 \, \text{km}^2$ with an average thickness of $40 \, \text{m}$. The rock density is $2200 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ and the kerogen content is $12\%$ by weight. The kerogen-to-oil conversion factor is $66 \, \text{L of oil per tonne of kerogen}$. Calculate the **in-place oil resource in million barrels (MMbbl)**. Take the density of shale oil as $880 \, \text{kg/m}^3$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**The Resource Triangle Concept (3 marks):**

Proposed by **M. King Hubbert** and refined by **Walter Youngquist**, the Resource Triangle is a graphical/logarithmic model that states:

- The world's hydrocarbon resources form a **triangle** when plotted as *cumulative production* (Y-axis) versus *resource grade/concentration* (X-axis).
- **Top of the triangle:** Small volume of **high-grade, easily-extracted** resources (e.g., light sweet crude in Saudi Arabia) — historically the focus of production.
- **Bottom of the triangle:** Vast volume of **low-grade, difficult-to-extract** resources (e.g., oil sands, oil shales, gas hydrates) — abundant but dispersed.

**Why it justifies non-conventional development (4 marks):**

1. **Depletion of top-tier resources:** Conventional high-quality reserves are being consumed faster than they are being discovered → supply gap.
2. **Technological advancement:** Innovations like SAGD, multistage fracturing, and horizontal drilling have shifted the **economic cutoff** lower in the triangle.
3. **Price escalation:** Sustained crude oil prices above USD 60–80/bbl make non-conventional resources economical.
4. **Energy security:** Countries seek to monetize their bottom-triangle resources (e.g., Canada with bitumen, USA with shale, India with CBM and gas hydrates).
5. **Environmental push:** Some non-conventional (e.g., gas hydrates) are cleaner-burning than coal and oil, justifying investment.

> **Key Conclusion:** *The Resource Triangle proves that as conventional resources deplete, technological progress will economically unlock progressively lower-grade resources — a self-correcting mechanism in the energy market.*

**Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Volume of the oil shale deposit**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= A \times h = 10 \, \text{km}^2 \times 40 \, \text{m} \\
&= 10 \times 10^6 \, \text{m}^2 \times 40 \, \text{m} \\
&= 4 \times 10^8 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Total mass of oil shale rock**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{shale} &= V \times \rho_{shale} = 4 \times 10^8 \times 2200 \\
&= 8.8 \times 10^{11} \, \text{kg} = 8.8 \times 10^8 \, \text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Mass of kerogen in the deposit**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{kerogen} &= m_{shale} \times 0.12 = 8.8 \times 10^8 \times 0.12 \\
&= 1.056 \times 10^8 \, \text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Volume of oil produced from kerogen (using conversion factor 66 L/tonne)**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{oil} &= m_{kerogen} \times 66 \, \text{L/tonne} \\
&= 1.056 \times 10^8 \times 66 \\
&= 6.9696 \times 10^9 \, \text{L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Convert litres to barrels (1 bbl = 158.987 L)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Oil in barrels} &= \dfrac{6.9696 \times 10^9}{158.987} \\
&\approx 4.3838 \times 10^7 \, \text{bbl} \\
&\approx 43.84 \, \text{MMbbl}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** In-place oil resource $\approx$ **43.84 MMbbl**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Students often mix km² and m². Always convert km² to m² by multiplying by $10^6$.
> 2. **Weight % vs Volume %:** The 12% is **by weight** of kerogen, not by volume. Do not apply it to a volume.
> 3. **Conversion factors:** 1 bbl = **158.987 L** (not 159 or 160). Using the wrong factor can cost 0.5 marks.
> 4. **Final unit requested:** The problem asks for **MMbbl**, not million litres — present your final answer in MMbbl.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on Module-1 "Resources" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Resource" with "Reserve":** *Resource = in-place; Reserve = recoverable and economic.* Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Skipping unit consistency:** When using 7758, units MUST be acres and ft. When using SI, use 1 (no constant).
> 3. **Wrong conversion constants:** 1 acre = 43,560 ft²; 1 bbl = 5.615 ft³; 1 bbl = 158.987 L.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions:** Always mention "assuming uniform porosity, no water influx, etc." in derivations.
> 5. **Forgetting to apply Recovery Factor:** OOIP ≠ Reserve. Always multiply by $R_F$ to get recoverable reserve.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Resources (Module 1)**

### **A. Definitional Block:**
- ☐ Non-conventional = needs stimulation/heating/fracturing to produce
- ☐ Resource = all hydrocarbons in-place (discovered + undiscovered)
- ☐ Reserve = economic subset of discovered resource
- ☐ OOIP = Original Oil In Place (STB or m³)
- ☐ OGIP = Original Gas In Place (scf or m³)
- ☐ RF = Recovery Factor = Recoverable / In Place

### **B. Six Major Non-Conventional Categories:**
- ☐ **Heavy Oil / Extra-Heavy Oil** — viscous crude, CHOPS / CSS
- ☐ **Oil Sands / Bitumen** — Athabasca, SAGD / mining
- ☐ **Oil Shale** — kerogen, surface retorting
- ☐ **Coal Bed Methane (CBM)** — adsorbed on coal, dewatering
- ☐ **Shale Gas / Tight Gas** — low-perm shale, multistage fracturing
- ☐ **Gas Hydrates** — clathrate compounds, depressurization

### **C. Critical Formula Block (Memorize):**
- ☐ $\text{OOIP} = \dfrac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{oi}}$ — *Field units, STB*
- ☐ $\text{OGIP} = \dfrac{43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{gi}}$ — *Field units, scf*
- ☐ $\text{Reserve} = \text{OOIP} \times R_F$
- ☐ $\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$
- ☐ $\text{CBM OGIP} = A \cdot h \cdot \rho_c \cdot G_c$ *(in scf)*

### **D. Classification Block:**
- ☐ McKelvey Diagram — geological assurance × economic feasibility
- ☐ SPE-PRMS — 1P (P₉₀) / 2P (P₅₀) / 3P (P₁₀)
- ☐ UNFC — three-axis system (environmental, technical, confidence)
- ☐ Resource Triangle — Hubbert's model linking quality to volume

### **E. Numerical Constants (Memorize These):**
- ☐ 1 bbl = 5.615 ft³ = 0.158987 m³ = 158.987 L = 42 US gal
- ☐ 1 acre = 43,560 ft²
- ☐ 7758 = 43,560 / 5.615 (bbl per acre-ft)
- ☐ 1 tonne crude ≈ 7.33 bbl (for 35° API)

### **F. KTU Hot Topics Likely for Future Exams:**
- ☐ Volumetric OOIP / OGIP calculation (always tested)
- ☐ Resource vs Reserve difference (high probability)
- ☐ McKelvey vs PRMS classification comparison
- ☐ CBM resource estimation in Indian context (Raniganj, Jharia)
- ☐ India's hydrocarbon resource base and import dependency
- ☐ Comparison of oil shale vs oil sands (definition trap)

> **Final Note:** The *Resources* topic in Module 1 is the **foundation** for the entire PECHT752 course. Mastering OOIP, OGIP, and resource classification enables you to understand every subsequent module (recovery methods, environmental impact, and economics).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reservoir Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT752 - NON-CONVENTIONAL PETROLEUM RESOURCES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Reservoir Characteristics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reservoir Characteristics** refer to the collective set of **petrophysical, geochemical, geomechanical, and volumetric properties** of a subsurface geological formation that govern its capacity to **store, transmit, and yield** non-conventional hydrocarbons such as shale gas, tight gas, coalbed methane (CBM), oil sands, and gas hydrates. Unlike conventional reservoirs where hydrocarbons migrate into porous-permeable carrier rocks (e.g., sandstone, carbonate), non-conventional reservoirs are typically the **source rock itself** or an extremely tight, low-matrix-permeability rock, requiring the rock to be characterized across multiple physical and chemical dimensions. ### 1.2 Key Parameters (The "Six Pillars" of Non-Conventional Reservoir Characterization) 1. **Porosity (φ)** — especially nano- and micro-porosity 2. **Permeability (k)** — typically in the **microdarcy (µD)** to **nanodarcy (nD)** range 3. **Total Organic Carbon (TOC)** — in **wt%** 4. **Thermal Maturity (Ro)** — vitrinite reflectance in **%** 5. **Adsorption Capacity (VL, PL)** — Langmuir parameters 6. **Brittleness & Mineralogy** — for fracability assessment ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of a non-conventional reservoir like a hard, dry sponge trapped inside a sealed glass box (the formation).** > > - A **conventional reservoir** is a *wet kitchen sponge* — full of large connected holes that freely release water (oil/gas) when squeezed. > - A **non-conventional reservoir** (e.g., shale) is a *dense, compressed, dry clay block* — the water is *bound inside the clay particles* and in **microscopic pores smaller than a human hair's diameter (often < 2 nm)**. To extract the hydrocarbon, we must **fracture the block** to create pathways. The **reservoir characteristics** are the measurements that tell the engineer: - *How much fluid is locked inside?* (porosity, TOC, saturation) - *How easily will it flow out?* (permeability, brittleness) - *Is the fluid even mature enough to produce?* (Ro, kerogen type) - *How much can we get back?* (adsorption isotherms, gas-in-place) ### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics Used | Symbol | Parameter | Typical Range (Non-Conventional) | Unit | |--------|-----------|----------------------------------|------| | φ | Porosity | 2 – 12 | % | | k | Matrix permeability | 0.0001 – 0.1 | mD (millidarcy) | | TOC | Total Organic Carbon | 1 – 12 | wt% | | Ro | Vitrinite Reflectance | 0.6 – 3.0 | % | | VL | Langmuir Volume | 1 – 50 | scf/ton | | PL | Langmuir Pressure | 100 – 1000 | psi | > [!NOTE] > **For reference, a conventional sandstone reservoir has permeability of 1 – 1000 mD, whereas a shale gas reservoir often has permeability of 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻³ mD** — about **1,000 to 1,000,000 times tighter**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of Reservoir Characterization The complete characterization of a non-conventional reservoir is divided into **four interacting sub-systems**: **(A) Storage Capacity (Static Properties)** - Defines the *total volume of hydrocarbon held within the rock.* - Governed by **porosity (φ)**, **organic richness (TOC)**, **fluid saturation (Sw, So, Sg)**, **adsorption capacity (VL, PL)**, and **bulk density (ρb)**. **(B) Flow Capacity (Dynamic Properties)** - Defines the *ability of hydrocarbon to migrate through the rock matrix to the wellbore.* - Governed by **absolute permeability (k)**, **relative permeability (kr)**, **pore-throat radius distribution**, and **Knudsen number (Kn)** — important when pore sizes approach gas mean free path. **(C) Chemical/Maturity Properties** - Defines *whether the kerogen has been transformed into producible hydrocarbons.* - Governed by **vitrinite reflectance (Ro)**, **kerogen type (I, II, III, IV)**, **Tmax**, and **hydrogen index (HI)**. **(D) Mechanical/Fracability Properties** - Defines *how easily the rock can be hydraulically fractured to create flow paths.* - Governed by **Young's modulus (E)**, **Poisson's ratio (ν)**, **brittle mineral content (quartz + carbonate)**, and **minimum horizontal stress (σh)**. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Property | Property | Why It Matters (Engineering Utility) | |----------|--------------------------------------| | **Porosity (φ)** | Determines total pore volume available for free gas + adsorbed gas | | **Permeability (k)** | Determines if hydraulic fracturing is economically mandatory | | **TOC** | Higher TOC = higher source-rock potential and more adsorbed gas | | **Ro** | Identifies the hydrocarbon "window" (oil, wet gas, dry gas) | | **Brittleness** | Brittle rocks fracture cleanly → better stimulated rock volume (SRV) | | **Adsorption Isotherm** | Predicts gas desorption behavior in CBM and shale | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are board-exam essential. Memorize units and assumptions.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $$\phi = \frac{V_p}{V_b} \times 100$$ | Porosity (pore volume / bulk volume) | % | | 2 | $$k = \frac{Q \cdot \mu \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta P}$$ | Darcy's Law (permeability) | mD | | 3 | $$V(P) = \frac{V_L \cdot P}{P_L + P}$$ | Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm | scf/ton | | 4 | $$GIP = \frac{43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_g}$$ | Original Gas-In-Place (volumetric) | scf | | 5 | $$G_{ads} = 43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \rho_b \cdot V_L \cdot \frac{P}{P+P_L}$$ | Adsorbed Gas-In-Place | scf | | 6 | $$B_{min} = \frac{Q + C}{2}$$ | Mineralogical Brittleness Index | fraction | | 7 | $$BI_{elastic} = \frac{E_{norm} + \nu_{norm}}{2}$$ | Elastic Brittleness Index | fraction | | 8 | $$Kn = \frac{\lambda}{d_p}$$ | Knudsen number (flow regime indicator) | dimensionless | | 9 | $$TOC = \frac{Mass_{organic}}{Mass_{rock}} \times 100$$ | Total Organic Carbon | wt% | | 10 | $$\sigma_h = \nu \cdot \sigma_v + (1-\nu) \cdot P_p$$ | Minimum horizontal stress (poroelastic) | psi | **Where:** - $Q$ = flow rate (cc/s), $\mu$ = fluid viscosity (cp), $L$ = length (cm), $A$ = area (cm²), $\Delta P$ = pressure drop (atm) - $V_L$ = Langmuir volume (scf/ton), $P_L$ = Langmuir pressure (psi) - $B_g$ = gas formation volume factor (rcf/scf) - $E_{norm}$ = normalized Young's modulus, $\nu_{norm}$ = normalized Poisson's ratio - $\lambda$ = gas mean free path, $d_p$ = pore diameter - $Q$ = quartz fraction, $C$ = carbonate fraction ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Porosity-permeability (φ-k) crossplots** are used to identify *sweet spots* in shale plays. - **Langmuir isotherms** are derived from canister desorption tests in CBM exploration. - **Brittleness index (BI)** is logged using sonic and density tools to plan horizontal laterals and stage spacing in hydraulic fracturing. - **TOC mapping** from spectral gamma-ray and pulsed-neutron logs guides lease acquisition decisions worth **billions of dollars** in unconventional plays (e.g., Marcellus, Permian, Eagle Ford). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From Darcy's Law to Apparent Permeability in Nano-Pores In non-conventional reservoirs, the assumption of continuum flow (Darcy flow) breaks down when **Knudsen number (Kn) > 0.001**. We must correct for slip flow and Knudsen diffusion. **Step 1 — Start with the Darcy equation for volumetric flow rate:** $$Q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$ **Step 2 — Introduce the corrected (apparent) permeability, $k_{app}$, which accounts for gas slippage at the pore wall:** $$Q = \frac{k_{app} \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the Klinkenberg correction (the standard industry correction for gas slippage):** $$k_{app} = k_{\infty} \left(1 + \frac{4 \cdot c \cdot \lambda}{r}\right) = k_{\infty} \left(1 + \frac{b}{P_{avg}}\right)$$ Where: - $k_{\infty}$ = absolute (liquid-equivalent) permeability, mD - $c$ ≈ 1 (slip constant, dimensionless) - $\lambda$ = mean free path of gas molecules, m - $r$ = average pore radius, m - $b$ = Klinkenberg slip factor, psi - $P_{avg}$ = mean pore pressure, psia **Step 4 — Substitute the mean free path expression** $\lambda = \frac{k_B \cdot T}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \pi \cdot d_m^2 \cdot P_{avg}}$ (kinetic theory): $$k_{app} = k_{\infty} + \frac{4 \cdot c \cdot k_B \cdot T \cdot k_{\infty}}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \pi \cdot d_m^2 \cdot r \cdot P_{avg}}$$ **Step 5 — Final grouped form used in shale gas simulation:** $$k_{app} = k_{\infty} \left(1 + \frac{b}{P_{avg}}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** Measured air permeability on a shale core is *always higher* than liquid permeability. Ignoring the Klinkenberg effect leads to **overestimation of well deliverability** — a classic KTU board trap. --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Volumetric Gas-In-Place (Free Gas) **Problem (KTU-style):** A shale gas reservoir occupies an area of **A = 4,000 acres**, with average thickness **h = 200 ft**, porosity **φ = 8%**, water saturation **Sw = 35%**, and gas formation volume factor **Bg = 0.005 rcf/scf**. Compute the free gas-in-place (GIP). **Step 1 — Convert area to square feet:** $$A = 4000 \text{ acres} \times 43560 \frac{ft^2}{acre} = 1.7424 \times 10^8 \, ft^2$$ **Step 2 — Compute bulk rock volume:** $$V_b = A \times h = 1.7424 \times 10^8 \times 200 = 3.4848 \times 10^{10} \, ft^3$$ **Step 3 — Compute hydrocarbon pore volume (HCPV):** $$HCPV = V_b \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w) = 3.4848 \times 10^{10} \times 0.08 \times (1 - 0.35)$$ $$HCPV = 3.4848 \times 10^{10} \times 0.08 \times 0.65 = 1.8121 \times 10^9 \, ft^3$$ **Step 4 — Apply the volumetric GIP equation (free gas only):** $$G_{free} = \frac{HCPV}{B_g} = \frac{1.8121 \times 10^9}{0.005}$$ $$G_{free} = 3.6242 \times 10^{11} \, scf = 362.42 \, Bscf$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The free gas computed here represents only the gas in the pore space. In shale reservoirs, **adsorbed gas (Langmuir gas) typically accounts for 20–80%** of the total GIP — so the true total GIP may be **1.5× to 5× higher** than this number. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How to Identify a Non-Conventional Reservoir from Log Data | Log / Test | Conventional Threshold | Non-Conventional Threshold | Interpretation | |------------|------------------------|----------------------------|----------------| | Gamma Ray (GR) | < 75 API | > 75 API (often > 100) | High shale content | | Resistivity (LLD) | 1 – 30 ohm-m | 10 – 200 ohm-m | High hydrocarbon saturation in tight matrix | | Sonic Δt | 50 – 100 µs/ft | 60 – 110 µs/ft (variable) | Lower matrix velocity → more organic matter | | Density (RHOB) | 2.40 – 2.65 g/cc | 2.20 – 2.55 g/cc | Low density → high TOC | | Permeability (k) | > 0.1 mD | < 0.1 mD (often µD–nD) | Stimulation mandatory | | TOC | < 1 wt% | > 2 wt% (sweet spot > 4 wt%) | Active source rock | > [!TIP] > **Crossplot Trick (Board-Favorite):** Plot **RHOB (Y-axis, reversed) vs. Δt (X-axis)**. A non-conconventional organic-rich shale plots in the **upper-left corner** (low density, high sonic transit time) — this is the classic **"TOC crossplot."** ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Reservoir Characterization Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Core and Cutting Samples] --> B[Lab Measurements] B --> B1[Porosity φ] B --> B2[Permeability k] B --> B3[TOC and Rock Eval] B --> B4[Adsorption Isotherm] B --> B5[Mineralogy XRD] B --> B6[Mechanical Properties E and nu] C[Wireline Logs] --> D[Log Interpretation] D --> D1[Gamma Ray GR] D --> D2[Resistivity Rt] D --> D3[Density RHOB] D --> D4[Sonic DT] D --> D5[Neutron Phi N] E[Seismic Data] --> F[Geomechanical Modeling] F --> F1[Brittleness Map] F --> F2[Stress Magnitude] F --> F3[Natural Fracture Network] B1 --> G[Reservoir Static Model] B2 --> G B3 --> G B4 --> G D1 --> G D2 --> G D3 --> G D4 --> G D5 --> G G --> H[Sweet Spot Identification] F1 --> H F2 --> H F3 --> H H --> I[Horizontal Well Placement] H --> J[Hydraulic Fracture Design] H --> K[Estimated Ultimate Recovery EUR] ``` ### 4.2 Schematic: Pore-Scale Storage Mechanisms in Shale ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Shale Matrix Block] --> P1[Inorganic Nano-Pores
2 to 50 nm] P --> P2[Organic Matter Pores
5 to 200 nm] P --> P3[Micro-Fractures
1 to 100 microns] P --> P4[Adsorbed Gas on Kerogen
Monolayer Coverage] P1 --> F1[Free Gas Storage] P2 --> F2[Free Gas in OM Pores] P3 --> F3[Conductive Pathways] P4 --> F4[Langmuir Adsorbed Gas] F1 --> T[Total Gas In Place] F2 --> T F4 --> T ``` ### 4.3 Process Flow: Langmuir Isotherm for CBM / Shale Gas ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Start: Lab Adsorption Test] --> S1[Inject Gas at Stepwise Pressures] S1 --> S2[Measure Cumulative Volume Adsorbed V] S2 --> S3[Plot P versus V P over V Curve] S3 --> S4[Linearize to P over V versus P] S4 --> S5[Slope equals 1 over VL] S4 --> S6[Intercept equals PL over VL] S5 --> S7[Compute Langmuir Volume VL] S6 --> S8[Compute Langmuir Pressure PL] S7 --> S9[Generate Isotherm Equation] S8 --> S9 S9 --> S10[Use in Gas In Place Calculation] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Tip:** The linearized Langmuir plot is **the most frequently asked diagram** in KTU board exams for CBM modules. The straight-line relationship $\frac{P}{V} = \frac{P}{V_L} + \frac{P_L}{V_L}$ allows graphical determination of $V_L$ and $P_L$. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** *Define porosity and permeability. Why are both critical in evaluating a non-conventional petroleum reservoir?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Porosity (φ)** is the ratio of pore volume to bulk volume of the rock, expressed as a percentage. It represents the *storage capacity* of the formation. **(1 mark)** - **Permeability (k)** is the ability of the rock to transmit fluids under a pressure gradient, measured in Darcies or millidarcies. It represents the *flow capacity*. **(1 mark)** - In non-conventional reservoirs, permeability is extremely low (µD–nD range), so natural production is negligible even with high porosity. **Both parameters are critical** because porosity quantifies *how much* hydrocarbon is stored, while permeability quantifies *how easily* it can be produced — together they define whether hydraulic fracturing is necessary. **(1 mark)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** *What is Total Organic Carbon (TOC)? State the typical TOC range considered a "sweet spot" in shale gas exploration.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **TOC** is the mass fraction of organic carbon in a rock sample, expressed in **weight percent (wt%)**, measured by combustion analysis. It quantifies the *source-rock potential* of a formation. **(2 marks)** - A **TOC > 2 wt%** is considered a viable shale gas play; a **TOC > 4 wt%** is generally regarded as a **"sweet spot"** indicating excellent hydrocarbon generation capacity. **(1 mark)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - Students often **confuse adsorbed gas with free gas** in GIP calculations — always state *both* contributions separately. > - Forgetting the unit conversion **acre → ft² (× 43,560)** is the most common mark-deduction error in volumetric problems. > - In Langmuir questions, students plot $P$ vs. $V$ instead of $P/V$ vs. $P$ — this yields a curve, not a line, and **loses 3 marks** for not showing the linearization. > - Always specify **assumptions** (homogeneous reservoir, constant Bg, etc.) — 1 mark is reserved for this. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a) Derive the Langmuir adsorption isotherm starting from the kinetic theory of adsorption equilibrium. (7 marks)** **(b) A coalbed methane (CBM) seam has the following properties: VL = 35 scf/ton, PL = 400 psi, in-situ pressure Pi = 800 psi, ρb = 1.75 g/cc, seam thickness h = 25 ft, areal extent A = 1280 acres. Compute the total adsorbed gas-in-place in scf and Bscf. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Statement of dynamic equilibrium:** At equilibrium, the rate of gas molecules *adsorbing* onto the surface equals the rate of molecules *desorbing* from the surface. $$\text{Rate of adsorption} = k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta)$$ $$\text{Rate of desorption} = k_d \cdot \theta$$ Where $\theta$ = fraction of surface covered, $P$ = gas pressure, $k_a, k_d$ = rate constants. **Step 2 — Equilibrium condition (rates equal):** $$k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta) = k_d \cdot \theta$$ **Step 3 — Solve for θ:** $$\theta = \frac{k_a \cdot P}{k_d + k_a \cdot P} = \frac{(k_a / k_d) \cdot P}{1 + (k_a / k_d) \cdot P}$$ **Step 4 — Define Langmuir constants:** Let $b = \frac{k_a}{k_d} = \frac{1}{P_L}$, where $P_L$ is the Langmuir pressure. Also, $\theta = \frac{V}{V_L}$, where $V_L$ is the Langmuir volume (maximum monolayer capacity). **Step 5 — Substitute and rearrange:** $$\frac{V}{V_L} = \frac{P / P_L}{1 + P / P_L} = \frac{P}{P + P_L}$$ **Step 6 — Final Langmuir isotherm equation:** $$\boxed{V = \frac{V_L \cdot P}{P + P_L}}$$ **Assumption (1 mark):** Monolayer adsorption on a homogeneous surface with no lateral interaction between adsorbed molecules. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Identify given data:** - $V_L = 35$ scf/ton - $P_L = 400$ psi - $P_i = 800$ psi - $\rho_b = 1.75$ g/cc - $h = 25$ ft - $A = 1280$ acres **Step 2 — Apply Langmuir equation to find in-situ adsorbed volume:** $$V = \frac{V_L \cdot P_i}{P_i + P_L} = \frac{35 \times 800}{800 + 400} = \frac{28000}{1200} = 23.33 \text{ scf/ton}$$ **Step 3 — Convert bulk density to consistent units (tons per ft³):** $$\rho_b = 1.75 \, \frac{g}{cc} = 1.75 \times 62.4 \, \frac{lb}{ft^3} = 109.2 \, \frac{lb}{ft^3} = 0.0546 \, \frac{tons}{ft^3}$$ *(1 ton = 2000 lb)* **Step 4 — Convert area to ft²:** $$A = 1280 \times 43560 = 5.576 \times 10^7 \, ft^2$$ **Step 5 — Compute bulk coal volume:** $$V_b = A \times h = 5.576 \times 10^7 \times 25 = 1.394 \times 10^9 \, ft^3$$ **Step 6 — Compute total coal mass in tons:** $$M = V_b \times \rho_b = 1.394 \times 10^9 \times 0.0546 = 7.611 \times 10^7 \text{ tons}$$ **Step 7 — Compute total adsorbed gas-in-place:** $$G_{ads} = V \times M = 23.33 \times 7.611 \times 10^7 = 1.776 \times 10^9 \, scf$$ **Step 8 — Convert to Bscf:** $$G_{ads} = 1.776 \times 10^9 \, scf = 1.776 \, Bscf$$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Answer:** $G_{ads} \approx 1.78 \, \text{Bscf}$ of adsorbed methane. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]** **(a) Explain the Klinkenberg effect. Derive the Klinkenberg-corrected permeability equation. (7 marks)** **(b) A shale core sample was tested with nitrogen gas at three mean pressures. The measured permeabilities are: at P₁ = 50 psia, k₁ = 0.0050 mD; at P₂ = 200 psia, k₂ = 0.0028 mD; at P₃ = 500 psia, k₃ = 0.0022 mD. Determine the absolute (Klinkenberg-corrected) permeability k∞ and the slip factor b. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Klinkenberg Effect — Theory and Derivation (7 marks):** The **Klinkenberg effect** is the phenomenon in which the *measured gas permeability of a rock is higher than its true liquid permeability* because gas molecules **slip** along the pore walls when the mean free path of the gas is comparable to the pore radius (Knudsen number > 0.001). This is especially important in **tight and shale formations** where pores are on the order of nanometers to micrometers. **Step 1 — Define observed apparent permeability:** $$k_{app} = \frac{Q \cdot \mu \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta P}$$ **Step 2 — Slip flow adds a velocity term at the wall (the "slip velocity"):** For a cylindrical capillary, the mean velocity with slippage is: $$v_{slip} = \frac{r^2}{8 \mu} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{4 \cdot \lambda}{r}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Comparing with the no-slip Hagen-Poiseuille equation, the apparent permeability becomes:** $$k_{app} = k_{\infty} \left(1 + \frac{4 \cdot \lambda}{r}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Recognize that $\frac{4\lambda}{r}$ is proportional to $\frac{1}{P_{avg}}$ (from kinetic theory, $\lambda \propto \frac{T}{P}$):** $$\frac{4 \lambda}{r} = \frac{b}{P_{avg}}$$ **Step 5 — Final Klinkenberg equation:** $$\boxed{k_{app} = k_{\infty} \left(1 + \frac{b}{P_{avg}}\right)}$$ Where $b$ = Klinkenberg slip factor (psi), $k_\infty$ = absolute (liquid-equivalent) permeability. > **Key Insight (1 mark):** The Klinkenberg plot of $k_{app}$ vs. $1/P_{avg}$ gives a straight line whose y-intercept is $k_\infty$ and slope is $k_\infty \cdot b$. --- **(b) Numerical Solution — Linear Regression (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Tabulate data with $y = k_{app}$ and $x = 1/P_{avg}$:** | $P_{avg}$ (psia) | $1/P_{avg}$ (1/psia) | $k_{app}$ (mD) | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 50 | 0.0200 | 0.0050 | | 200 | 0.0050 | 0.0028 | | 500 | 0.0020 | 0.0022 | **Step 2 — Set up linear regression. Let $y = k_{app}$, $x = 1/P_{avg}$:** $$k_{app} = k_\infty + b \cdot k_\infty \cdot \frac{1}{P_{avg}} \implies y = a + m \cdot x$$ Where intercept $a = k_\infty$ and slope $m = b \cdot k_\infty$. **Step 3 — Compute sums:** $$\sum x = 0.0200 + 0.0050 + 0.0020 = 0.0270$$ $$\sum y = 0.0050 + 0.0028 + 0.0022 = 0.0100$$ $$\sum xy = (0.0200)(0.0050) + (0.0050)(0.0028) + (0.0020)(0.0022) = 0.000100 + 0.000014 + 0.0000044 = 0.0001184$$ $$\sum x^2 = 0.0004 + 0.000025 + 0.000004 = 0.000429$$ **Step 4 — Compute slope and intercept (n = 3):** $$m = \frac{n \sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n \sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2} = \frac{3(0.0001184) - (0.0270)(0.0100)}{3(0.000429) - (0.0270)^2}$$ $$m = \frac{0.0003552 - 0.00027}{0.001287 - 0.000729} = \frac{0.0000852}{0.000558} = 0.1527$$ $$a = \frac{\sum y - m \sum x}{n} = \frac{0.0100 - (0.1527)(0.0270)}{3} = \frac{0.0100 - 0.004123}{3} = 0.001959$$ **Step 5 — Interpret results:** $$k_\infty = a = 0.00196 \text{ mD} \approx 1.96 \, \mu D$$ $$b = \frac{m}{a} = \frac{0.1527}{0.00196} = 77.9 \text{ psi}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Answer:** $k_\infty \approx 0.00196$ mD, $b \approx 78$ psi. The high $b$ value confirms strong slippage, consistent with a nano-porous shale matrix. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** - ☐ **Porosity (φ)** = storage capacity; **Permeability (k)** = flow capacity. In non-conventional reservoirs, **k is the bottleneck**. - ☐ **TOC > 2 wt%** is the minimum source-rock threshold; **> 4 wt%** is the sweet spot. - ☐ **Ro** identifies the maturity window: **0.6 – 0.7% = oil**, **0.7 – 1.2% = wet gas**, **1.2 – 3.0% = dry gas**. - ☐ **Langmuir isotherm:** $V = \frac{V_L P}{P + P_L}$ — derived from dynamic adsorption-desorption equilibrium, assumes monolayer coverage. - ☐ **Linearized form:** $\frac{P}{V} = \frac{P}{V_L} + \frac{P_L}{V_L}$ — plot $P/V$ vs. $P$ to extract $V_L$ and $P_L$. - ☐ **Klinkenberg effect:** gas permeability > liquid permeability due to wall slippage; corrected using $k_{app} = k_\infty(1 + b/P_{avg})$. - ☐ **Klinkenberg plot:** $k_{app}$ vs. $1/P_{avg}$ gives a straight line — **y-intercept = $k_\infty$**. - ☐ **GIP (free gas)** uses $\frac{43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1-S_w)}{B_g}$; **GIP (adsorbed gas)** uses $43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \rho_b \cdot V$. - ☐ **Brittleness Index (BI)** is computed from mineralogy: $BI_{min} = \frac{Q+C}{2}$ or from elastic properties: $BI_{elastic} = \frac{E_{norm}+\nu_{norm}}{2}$. - ☐ **Knudsen number** $Kn = \lambda / d_p$: < 0.001 = Darcy flow, 0.001 – 0.1 = slip flow, > 0.1 = Knudsen diffusion. - ☐ Always quote **units** — the most common board mark-loss is unit errors in $B_g$ and area conversions. - ☐ Always list **assumptions** in derivations: monolayer, homogeneous surface, no lateral interaction (Langmuir); steady-state, laminar flow (Darcy).

topics.title: New Production Technologies
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT752 - NON-CONVENTIONAL PETROLEUM RESOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** New Production Technologies

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining "New Production Technologies" in Non-Conventional Petroleum

In the context of KTU's PECHT752 syllabus, **New Production Technologies** refers to the suite of advanced drilling, completion, and stimulation techniques developed to economically produce hydrocarbons from reservoirs that cannot be exploited using conventional primary/secondary recovery methods. These reservoirs are characterized by **ultra-low permeability** (nanodarcy to microdarcy range), **high viscosity crudes**, or **immobile hydrocarbons trapped in source rock**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *New Production Technologies* are integrated engineering systems combining **directional drilling**, **reservoir stimulation**, and **thermal/non-thermal recovery processes** that unlock hydrocarbon mobility in tight formations (shale, tight sand), heavy oil reservoirs, oil sands, coal seams, and gas hydrates where Darcy flow is negligible or absent.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **solid brick of wet clay** (this is shale) where oil is trapped inside tiny pores. If you simply drill a well, the oil refuses to flow because the rock is so tight. The intuitive analogies are:

| Technology | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Hydraulic Fracturing** | Like cracking a dry mud ball by forcing water through it to release trapped moisture |
| **Horizontal Drilling** | Like following a vein of gold sideways through rock instead of digging straight down |
| **Steam Injection (SAGD)** | Like heating frozen butter in a pan to make it flow |
| **In-Situ Combustion** | Like setting a log on fire at one end and letting the heat push sap out the other end |

## 1.3 Why These Technologies Are "New"

Conventional vertical wells in high-permeability sandstone produce oil naturally due to reservoir pressure. The "newness" comes from three paradigm shifts:

- **From vertical to horizontal** well architecture (multi-lateral wells can extend 3+ km laterally).
- **From natural flow to artificially induced fractures** (creating permeable pathways where none exist).
- **From ambient to thermal operations** (injecting steam at **300–340 °C** to reduce viscosity from >10,000 cP to <10 cP).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** A conventional reservoir has permeability $k > 1$ md. A shale play has $k < 0.001$ md (i.e., **1,000,000× tighter**). The surface area of contact achieved by new technologies compensates for this gap.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of New Production Technologies

### A. Non-Thermal Mechanical Technologies

**1) Horizontal / Multilateral Drilling**

A wellbore is deviated from vertical to follow the pay zone laterally.

- **Why:** Contact length with reservoir increases 10–20× compared to vertical wells.
- **How:** Using a positive-displacement motor with a bent sub and Measurement-While-Drilling (MWD) tools.

**2) Hydraulic Fracturing (Fracking)**

High-pressure fluid (typically **slickwater**: water + sand + 0.5–2% chemical additives) is injected at pressures exceeding the **minimum horizontal stress** to create tensile fractures.

- **Why:** Creates a high-permeability conduit from the wellbore deep into the formation.
- **How:** Fluid is pumped at **5,000–10,000 psi** at rates of **50–100 bbl/min**.

**3) Multi-Stage Fracturing**

A horizontal well is divided into 20–40 isolated stages, each fractured sequentially.

- **Why:** Maximizes Stimulated Reservoir Volume (SRV).
- **How:** Using plug-and-perf or sliding sleeve systems.

### B. Thermal Recovery Technologies

**4) Cyclic Steam Stimulation (CSS) — "Huff-n-Puff"**

Steam is injected, the well is shut-in to soak, then produced.

- **Why:** Reduces oil viscosity from 100,000 cP to <50 cP.
- **How:** Steam at **80% quality** injected for weeks, soak for days, then produce for months.

**5) Steam-Assisted Gravity Drainage (SAGD)**

Uses a pair of parallel horizontal wells — steam injected in upper well, mobilized oil drains to lower well.

- **Why:** Continuous, gravity-stable displacement with high oil recovery (40–60%).
- **How:** Operated at the **Steam Oil Ratio (SOR)** between **2.0–4.0**.

**6) In-Situ Combustion (ISC) / Fireflood**

A portion of oil is ignited, and the combustion front is propagated by air injection.

- **Why:** Generates in-situ heat (up to **600 °C**) and creates a miscible CO₂ flood naturally.
- **How:** Air injected at **1–2 MSCF per bbl of oil produced**.

### C. Other Emerging Technologies

**7) Coal Bed Methane (CBM) Extraction** — dewatering of coal seams to release adsorbed methane.

**8) CHOPS (Cold Heavy Oil Production with Sand)** — aggressive sand production to create wormholes.

**9) CO₂-EOR (miscible flooding)** — supercritical CO₂ injection for tight oil recovery.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **exam-essential** for solving Part B problems in PECHT752.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Darcy's Law (linear flow) | $q = \dfrac{kA}{\mu L} \Delta P$ | $q$ in m³/s, $k$ in m² |
| 2 | Permeability–Porosity tie (Kozeny-Carman) | $k = \dfrac{\phi^{3}}{180(1-\phi)^{2}} d^{2}$ | $d$ = grain diameter |
| 3 | Fracture half-length productivity (PRF) | $J_D = \dfrac{q \mu B}{2 \pi k h \Delta P}$ | Dimensionless PI |
| 4 | Effective fracture length (Cinco-Ley) | $x_f = \dfrac{2 k h \Delta P}{\mu q}$ | m |
| 5 | Viscosity–Temperature (Andrade) | $\mu = A e^{B/T}$ | $T$ in K |
| 6 | Steam Quality definition | $x = \dfrac{m_{vapor}}{m_{steam,total}}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Steam-Oil Ratio (SOR) | $SOR = \dfrac{V_{steam,cw}}{N_{p}}$ | bbl steam / bbl oil |
| 8 | Cumulative Oil from SAGD (Butler model) | $q = 2 L \sqrt{\dfrac{2 k g \phi \Delta S_o h \alpha}{m \mu_s}}$ | m³/s |
| 9 | CBM gas content (Langmuir) | $V = \dfrac{V_L P}{P_L + P}$ | scf/ton |
| 10 | Frac gradient check | $P_{frac} = 0.7 \times$ depth (psi/ft) | Equivalent mud weight |
| 11 | Pseudo-steady decline (Arps) | $q(t) = \dfrac{q_i}{(1 + b D_i t)^{1/b}}$ | $b \in [0,1]$ |
| 12 | Horizontal well productivity (Joshi) | $J_h = \dfrac{2 \pi k h}{\mu B \left[ \ln\left( \dfrac{r_{eh}}{r_w} \right) - 0.75 + s \right]}$ | STB/d/psi |
| 13 | Reynolds number (frac fluid) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v d_h}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** For KTU numerical questions, always state assumptions: **1-D flow, isothermal, Newtonian fluid, Darcy regime** ($Re < 1$).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Energy Security:** Shale gas (USA) increased from 1% to **70% of total US natural gas production** in two decades using these technologies.
- **Recovery Factor Uplift:** Conventional waterflood yields **30–40% OOIP**; SAGD + CSS combination yields **50–70% OOIP** in oil sands.
- **Economic Threshold:** Tight oil plays are viable only when well productivity index $J > 0.5$ STB/d/psi, achievable only with horizontal + multi-stage fracturing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Horizontal Well Productivity (Joshi Equation)

**Starting Point — Equivalent Flow Geometry:**

A horizontal well of length $L$ in a reservoir of thickness $h$ can be visualized as a vertical well of half-length $L/2$ that has been rotated 90° and stretched. The total flow is composed of:
- **Radial flow in the vertical plane** (radius $r_{ev}$).
- **Linear flow in the horizontal plane** (radius $r_{eh}$).

**Step 1 — Geometric radius of drainage (vertical plane):**

$$r_{ev} = \sqrt{\dfrac{A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{L \cdot (2r_{eh})}{\pi}}$$

This represents the equivalent circular drainage area around the well.

**Step 2 — Total pressure drop decomposition:**

The total drawdown $\Delta P$ splits into:
- Vertical radial component $\Delta P_v$ (around the wellbore ends).
- Horizontal radial component $\Delta P_h$ (along the well length).

$$\Delta P = \Delta P_v + \Delta P_h$$

**Step 3 — Apply Darcy's Law to each component:**

For the vertical-plane radial flow:
$$q = \dfrac{2 \pi k h \Delta P_v}{\mu B \ln(r_{ev}/r_w)}$$

For the horizontal-plane radial flow (treating the well as a line source of length $L$):
$$q = \dfrac{2 \pi k L \Delta P_h}{\mu B \ln(r_{eh}/r_w)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\Delta P_h$ from the second equation:**

$$\Delta P_h = \dfrac{q \mu B \ln(r_{eh}/r_w)}{2 \pi k L}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back into $\Delta P$ equation:**

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{q \mu B \ln(r_{ev}/r_w)}{2 \pi k h} + \dfrac{q \mu B \ln(r_{eh}/r_w)}{2 \pi k L}$$

**Step 6 — Factor out the common term $q\mu B / (2\pi k)$:**

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{q \mu B}{2 \pi k} \left[ \dfrac{1}{h}\ln\left(\dfrac{r_{ev}}{r_w}\right) + \dfrac{1}{L}\ln\left(\dfrac{r_{eh}}{r_w}\right) \right]$$

**Step 7 — Approximate $r_{ev} = h/2$ (for thin reservoirs) and introduce skin factor $s$:**

$$J_h = \dfrac{q}{\Delta P} = \dfrac{2 \pi k h}{\mu B \left[ \ln\left( \dfrac{r_{eh}}{r_w} \right) - 0.75 + s \right]}$$

This is the **Joshi equation**, where $-0.75$ arises from numerical integration of the elliptic drainage shape.

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** For a vertical well ($L = h$), $r_{eh} = r_e$ and the equation collapses to the classical radial productivity formula — confirming internal consistency.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: SAGD Oil Rate (Butler's Gravity Drainage Model)

**Physical Setup:** A steam chamber grows upward and laterally from the injection well. Mobilized oil and condensate flow down along the chamber edge under gravity.

**Step 1 — Mass balance on a vertical slice of width $dx$ at the chamber edge:**

Steam at temperature $T_s$ contacts cold reservoir at $T_r$. Heat conducted into the reservoir mobilizes oil.

**Step 2 — Rate of advance of the steam front:**

The latent heat supplied to the rock is:
$$q_{heat} = k_{th} A \dfrac{dT}{dx}$$

**Step 3 — Darcy's velocity of mobilized oil draining down the chamber edge:**

$$v = \dfrac{k g \sin\theta}{\mu_s \phi \Delta S_o}$$

where $\mu_s$ is oil viscosity at steam temperature, $\Delta S_o$ is mobile oil saturation.

**Step 4 — Combine to get the linear flow rate per unit well length:**

$$\dfrac{dq}{dL} = 2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 k g \alpha \phi \Delta S_o}{m \mu_s}}$$

where:
- $k$ = effective permeability to oil (m²)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity of reservoir
- $m$ = a dimensionless parameter from the steam injection profile
- $\mu_s$ = oil viscosity at steam temperature

**Step 5 — Integrate over the entire well length $L$:**

$$q = 2 L \sqrt{\dfrac{2 k g \alpha \phi \Delta S_o h}{m \mu_s}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** $q \propto \sqrt{k}$ — this is why SAGD performance is **less sensitive to permeability** than conventional flooding ($q \propto k$). This makes SAGD viable even at $k = 0.5$ darcy in oil sands.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Technology vs. Reservoir Applicability

| Technology | Permeability Range | Oil Viscosity | Cap Rock | Recovery Factor | CAPEX Index |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal + Multi-Frac** | 0.0001 – 0.1 md | Any | Shale / Tight Sand | 5–15% | High |
| **SAGD** | 0.5 – 5 darcy | >10,000 cP | Heavy oil sand | 40–60% | Very High |
| **CSS (Huff-n-Puff)** | 0.5 – 5 darcy | >10,000 cP | Heavy oil sand | 20–30% | High |
| **In-Situ Combustion** | 0.05 – 5 darcy | >10 cP | Heavy / Light oil | 30–50% | Medium |
| **CO₂ Miscible Flood** | 0.1 – 50 md | Light oil (>35° API) | Depleted reservoirs | 10–20% incremental | High |
| **CBM Dewatering** | Coal matrix | N/A (gas) | Coal seams | 50–80% gas in place | Medium |
| **CHOPS** | 0.5 – 10 darcy | 50 – 10,000 cP | Unconsolidated sand | 5–15% | Low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Integrated New Production Technology System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Reservoir Characterization] --> B{Technology Selection Engine}
    B --> C1[Tight Rock Path]
    B --> C2[Heavy Oil Path]
    B --> C3[Coal Seam Path]
    C1 --> D1[Horizontal Drilling Rig]
    C1 --> D2[Multi-Stage Fracturing Fleet]
    D1 --> E1[Wellbore with Casing]
    D2 --> E2[Stimulated Reservoir Volume SRV]
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> F1[Production: Oil and Gas via Frac Network]
    C2 --> G1[SAGD Pair Wells]
    C2 --> G2[CSS Injection Cycle]
    C2 --> G3[In-Situ Combustion Air Injection]
    G1 --> H1[Steam Chamber Growth]
    G2 --> H2[Soak and Production Cycle]
    G3 --> H3[Combustion Front Propagation]

    H2 --> I1
    H3 --> I1

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The left-to-right flow shows how a reservoir's properties drive technology selection. All paths converge at the **Surface Processing Facility** (node M), emphasizing that *new production technologies are not isolated — they form an integrated system from subsurface to sales*.

## 4.2 Process Flow: SAGD Operation (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Steam Generator OTSG] -->|Steam 80 percent quality| S2[Injection Well Upper Horizontal]
    S2 -->|Steam chamber expands| S3[Reservoir Pay Zone 10 to 30 m thick]
    S3 -->|Heat reduces viscosity| S4[Oil mobilization by gravity]
    S4 -->|Oil + condensate drains| S5[Production Well Lower Horizontal 5 m below]
    S5 -->|Pump to surface| S6[Steam Oil Ratio Monitor 2 to 4]
    S6 -->|Oil water gas| S7[Surface Separation Train]
    S7 -->|Water recycle| S1
    S7 -->|Sales oil| S8[Pipeline]
    S7 -->|Non condensable gas vent or fuel| S1
```

## 4.3 Hydraulic Fracturing Operational Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    F1[Perforate Casing] --> F2[Pressure Test Lines]
    F2 --> F3[Pad Stage Slickwater 10000 bbl]
    F3 --> F4[Proppant Slurry 1 to 4 ppg ramp]
    F4 --> F5[Fracture Initiates at Min Horizontal Stress]
    F5 --> F6[Proppant Transports into Fracture]
    F6 --> F7[Flush Stage returns to Baseline]
    F7 --> F8[Set Isolation Plug]
    F8 --> F9[Move to Next Stage 20 to 40 stages total]
    F9 --> F1
    F1 -.->|after all stages| F10[Coiled Tubal Millout Plugs]
    F10 --> F11[Well in Production Mode]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Hydraulic Fracturing and state any two key fluid additives used in fracturing operations.*

**Model Answer:**

Hydraulic fracturing is a well stimulation technique in which a pressurized fracturing fluid (typically water-based) is injected into a wellbore at pressures exceeding the formation breakdown pressure, creating tensile fractures in the reservoir rock. These fractures are then propped open using a granular proppant, creating high-permeability conduits for hydrocarbon flow from the formation to the wellbore.

Two key fluid additives:
1. **Proppant** (sand or ceramic beads) — holds fractures open after pumping stops.
2. **Friction Reducer** (polyacrylamide-based polymer) — reduces pipe friction, allowing higher pump rates at lower surface pressure.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often miss mentioning that the fluid must be **above the minimum horizontal stress** to fracture the rock. A mere pressure increase is not sufficient.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between SAGD and CSS in heavy oil recovery. List two advantages of SAGD over CSS.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | SAGD (Steam-Assisted Gravity Drainage) | CSS (Cyclic Steam Stimulation) |
|---|---|---|
| Well pattern | Two parallel horizontal wells | Single vertical/horizontal well |
| Process | Continuous steam injection | Cyclic: inject → soak → produce |
| Drive mechanism | Gravity drainage | Pressure depletion + viscosity reduction |
| Recovery factor | 40–60% OOIP | 20–30% OOIP |
| Steam-Oil Ratio | 2.0–4.0 | 3.0–5.0 (less efficient) |

Two advantages of SAGD:
1. **Continuous process** → stable operating conditions, predictable production profile.
2. **Higher recovery** → gravity-stable displacement minimizes bypassed oil.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE — Select Either Question A or Question B]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** Describe the working principle of **Steam-Assisted Gravity Drainage (SAGD)** with the help of a schematic. List any **three operational parameters** that affect SAGD performance. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** A horizontal well of length **L = 800 m** is producing from a heavy oil reservoir. The reservoir and fluid properties are: permeability $k = 2$ darcy, porosity $\phi = 0.32$, oil saturation $S_o = 0.85$, residual oil saturation $S_{or} = 0.20$, thermal diffusivity $\alpha = 1.5 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s, oil viscosity at steam temperature $\mu_s = 8$ cP = $0.008$ Pa·s, pay zone thickness $h = 25$ m, and dimensionless Butler parameter $m = 3.7$. Using the Butler model, calculate the **oil production rate** in m³/day. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to A(a):

The SAGD process uses **two parallel horizontal wells** separated vertically by **4–6 m**:
- The **upper well** continuously injects high-quality steam.
- The **lower well** continuously produces the mobilized oil, water, and condensate.

The injected steam rises to form a **steam chamber** that grows upward and laterally, heating the surrounding oil sand. The heated oil becomes mobile (viscosity drops from ~100,000 cP to <10 cP) and drains under **gravity** to the lower production well. The chamber is **gravity-stable** — heavier condensate falls, lighter steam rises — making it energy-efficient.

**Three operational parameters affecting SAGD:**

1. **Steam Quality** — must exceed 70–80% at the sandface; wet steam reduces efficiency.
2. **Sub-cool Temperature** — temperature difference between injection and production wells (typically kept at **20–30 °C** to prevent steam breakthrough).
3. **Operating Pressure** — controlled by production well backpressure; determines steam temperature and chamber pressure.

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Given Data:**

$$L = 800 \text{ m}, \quad k = 2 \text{ D} = 2 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^2, \quad \phi = 0.32, \quad S_o = 0.85$$
$$S_{or} = 0.20, \quad \alpha = 1.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}, \quad \mu_s = 0.008 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$$
$$h = 25 \text{ m}, \quad m = 3.7, \quad g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 1 — Compute mobile oil saturation $\Delta S_o$:**

$$\Delta S_o = S_o - S_{or} = 0.85 - 0.20 = 0.65$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Butler SAGD equation:**

$$q = 2 L \sqrt{\dfrac{2 k g \alpha \phi \Delta S_o h}{m \mu_s}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the term inside the square root (numerator first):**

$$N = 2 \times (2 \times 10^{-12}) \times 9.81 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-7}) \times 0.32 \times 0.65 \times 25$$

Compute step-by-step:
$$2 \times (2 \times 10^{-12}) = 4 \times 10^{-12}$$
$$4 \times 10^{-12} \times 9.81 = 3.924 \times 10^{-11}$$
$$3.924 \times 10^{-11} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-7} = 5.886 \times 10^{-18}$$
$$5.886 \times 10^{-18} \times 0.32 = 1.8835 \times 10^{-18}$$
$$1.8835 \times 10^{-18} \times 0.65 = 1.2243 \times 10^{-18}$$
$$1.2243 \times 10^{-18} \times 25 = 3.0607 \times 10^{-17}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator term:**

$$D_{term} = m \mu_s = 3.7 \times 0.008 = 0.0296$$

**Step 5 — Compute the ratio and its square root:**

$$\dfrac{N}{D_{term}} = \dfrac{3.0607 \times 10^{-17}}{0.0296} = 1.0340 \times 10^{-15}$$

$$\sqrt{1.0340 \times 10^{-15}} = 3.2156 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3/\text{s per m}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by $2L$:**

$$q = 2 \times 800 \times 3.2156 \times 10^{-8} = 5.145 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to m³/day:**

$$q = 5.145 \times 10^{-5} \times 86400 = 4.445 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

$$\boxed{q \approx 4.45 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \approx 28 \text{ bbl/day}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently:
> 1. Forget to **convert darcy to m²** (1 D = $9.869 \times 10^{-13}$ m², often approximated as $10^{-12}$).
> 2. Miss the **$\Delta S_o$** computation and use $S_o$ directly — a 0.85/0.65 error.
> 3. Forget to convert cP to Pa·s (1 cP = $10^{-3}$ Pa·s).
> 4. Report answer in the **wrong unit** — always verify whether the question asks for m³/s, m³/day, or STB/day.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the concept of **multi-stage hydraulic fracturing** in horizontal wells. Describe any **two methods** of stage isolation. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **Joshi equation** for productivity of a horizontal well. State **two key assumptions** used in the derivation. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to B(a):

In multi-stage hydraulic fracturing, a long horizontal wellbore is divided into **20–40 isolated sections (stages)**, each of which is independently fractured. This creates multiple transverse fractures perpendicular to the wellbore, dramatically increasing the **Stimulated Reservoir Volume (SRV)** — the rock volume effectively contacted by the well.

**Two methods of stage isolation:**

1. **Plug-and-Perf Method:** After fracturing a stage, a composite bridge plug is set inside the casing using wireline or coiled tubing. The plug isolates the treated stage from the next. The next stage is then perforated, and the process repeats. Finally, all plugs are milled out using a coiled tubing unit, and the well is flowed back.

2. **Sliding Sleeve System (Frac Sleeves):** Pre-installed sleeves along the lateral are opened sequentially by dropping balls of increasing size, or by hydraulic actuation. Each opened sleeve exposes a new section of the formation for fracturing. This avoids the need for repeated wireline perforating.

#### Model Solution to B(b):

The derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The final result is:

$$J_h = \dfrac{2 \pi k h}{\mu B \left[ \ln\left( \dfrac{r_{eh}}{r_w} \right) - 0.75 + s \right]}$$

**Two key assumptions:**

1. **Pseudo-steady-state flow** — pressure transients have stabilized; the well is in boundary-dominated flow.
2. **The horizontal well is modeled as a line source** of infinite conductivity in an isotropic reservoir — meaning the wellbore pressure drop is negligible.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** For derivation questions, KTU examiners award marks for **every algebraic step**, not just the final formula. Skipping from physical setup to the final equation without intermediate steps typically fetches only 2 of 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hydraulic Fracturing** creates permeability in ultra-low-permeability reservoirs by inducing tensile fractures propped open with sand/ceramic proppants.
- **Horizontal Wells** multiply reservoir contact by 10–20× over vertical wells and are governed by the **Joshi productivity equation**.
- **SAGD** uses two parallel horizontal wells (4–6 m apart) for gravity-stable steam flooding; ideal for **extra-heavy oil** (viscosity > 10,000 cP).
- **CSS** is a single-well cyclic alternative to SAGD with lower recovery but lower CAPEX.
- **In-Situ Combustion** generates temperatures up to **600 °C** in situ, driving oil via thermal cracking and CO₂ flooding.
- **Multi-Stage Fracturing** uses **20–40 stages** per well, often via plug-and-perf or sliding sleeves, maximizing **SRV**.
- **Permeability Threshold:** Horizontal wells with multi-stage fracking can be economic down to $k \approx 0.0001$ md.
- **CBM** recovery depends on **Langmuir isotherm** desorption; dewatering reduces reservoir pressure below critical desorption pressure.
- **Joshi equation** — remember the **−0.75** constant from elliptic integration; it is not arbitrary.
- **SAGD Butler model** — oil rate scales as $\sqrt{k}$, not as $k$, making it viable even in **low-permeability** heavy oil.
- **Exam arithmetic traps:** Always convert **D → m²** ($1 \text{ D} = 9.869 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^2$), **cP → Pa·s** ($10^{-3}$), and **m³/s → m³/day** ($\times 86400$).
- **SOR** is the key economic KPI for thermal projects; values of **2.0–4.0** are typical for economic SAGD.
- **Frac gradient rule of thumb:** $P_{frac} \approx 0.7 \times \text{depth (ft)} \text{ in psi}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction and Present Status of Shale Gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT752 - NON-CONVENTIONAL PETROLEUM RESOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction and Present Status of Shale Gas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Shale Gas** is defined as *natural gas (primarily methane, CH₄)* that is trapped within fine-grained sedimentary rocks, predominantly **shale formations**, which possess extremely **low matrix permeability** (typically in the nano-Darcy to micro-Darcy range). Unlike conventional gas reservoirs, shale serves a **dual geological role** — acting simultaneously as both the **source rock** (where the kerogen was deposited and matured) and the **reservoir rock** (where the gas is stored and from which it is produced).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT752 – Module 2):**
> Shale gas is classified as an **unconventional hydrocarbon resource** because the gas is *self-sourced* (autochthonous) and held within the *micro-porosity and adsorbed phase* of the rock matrix, rather than migrating into a distinct, high-permeability trap.

The gas is stored in two primary modes within the shale matrix:

1. **Free Gas** — residing in natural fractures, micro-cracks, and intergranular pore space.
2. **Adsorbed Gas** — adhering to the surface of organic matter (kerogen) and clay minerals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics in Shale Gas Engineering:**
> - Permeability: **< 0.1 mD** (often **< 0.001 mD**)
> - Porosity: typically **2 % – 15 %**
> - Total Organic Carbon (TOC): **> 2 wt%** (commercial threshold)
> - Thermal Maturity (Ro): **1.0 % – 2.5 %** (dry gas window)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **sponge cake soaked in honey** (the cake = shale rock; the honey = natural gas):

- A **conventional gas reservoir** is like honey pooled at the bottom of a sealed glass jar — once you open a tap, it flows out effortlessly because it sits in a connected, open pore network.
- A **shale gas formation** is like honey *trapped inside the sponge itself* — the cake is sticky, the holes are tiny, and you cannot squeeze the honey out by hand. You must first **crack the sponge** (hydraulic fracturing) and **create artificial flow channels** to release the gas.

This analogy captures the two defining engineering challenges of shale gas: **(i) nano-scale storage and (ii) need for stimulation** to make production economically viable.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Unconventional" tag is not about the gas composition — it is about the *delivery mechanism* from rock to wellbore.** The gas is essentially the same methane you get from conventional fields, but the *reservoir physics* of extraction is fundamentally different.

---

## 1.3 Distinguishing Shale Gas from Other Unconventional Gases

| Feature | Shale Gas | Tight Gas | Coalbed Methane (CBM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Source Rock | Shale (self-sourced) | Sandstone/silt (migrated) | Coal seams |
| Storage Mechanism | Free + Adsorbed | Mostly free | Predominantly adsorbed |
| Permeability | **< 0.1 mD** | 0.1 – 1 mD | Variable (cleat-dependent) |
| Stimulation | Hydraulic fracturing essential | Often required | Usually dewatering + frac |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geological Origin of Shale Gas

The formation of a commercial shale gas play is the culmination of a **multi-million-year geological process** governed by six interdependent factors. A KTU answer on this topic is expected to enumerate and explain these factors in logical sequence.

### Step 1 — Deposition of Organic-Rich Sediment
Fine-grained mud, clay, and siliceous/calcareous material accumulate in **low-energy, anoxic marine or lacustrine basins**. The low oxygen environment prevents aerobic decay of organic matter, allowing **kerogen precursors (algae, plankton, spores)** to be preserved.

### Step 2 — Burial & Compaction
As successive sedimentary layers accumulate, the organic-rich mud is buried to depths typically ranging from **1,000 m to 5,000 m**. Pore fluids are expelled, and the sediment lithifies into **shale**.

### Step 3 — Thermal Maturation (Catagenesis)
With increasing burial depth, temperature rises at the **geothermal gradient** (≈ **25–30 °C/km**). When temperature reaches the **oil window (60–120 °C)** and later the **gas window (120–225 °C)**, kerogen undergoes **cracking** — a pyrolytic decomposition that generates hydrocarbons. Methane-dominated generation occurs at vitrinite reflectance **Ro > 1.2 %**.

### Step 4 — Primary & Secondary Migration (Limited)
In conventional systems, gas expels from the source rock and migrates upward. In shale, **expulsion efficiency is very low**; the majority of generated gas remains **trapped in-situ** within the source rock itself. This is the foundational concept of *self-sourcing*.

### Step 5 — Storage in the Matrix
The generated methane is retained through:
- **Adsorption** onto kerogen and clay surfaces (Langmuir-type isotherm behaviour).
- **Free accumulation** in interparticle pores and microfractures.
- **Solution** in kerogen and bitumen (minor contribution).

### Step 6 — Trapping Mechanism
Because shale has **extremely low permeability**, gas cannot escape vertically over geological timescales. The seal is *intrinsic* — the rock is its own caprock. This makes shale gas a **continuous-type accumulation** rather than a discrete trap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is shale gas "continuous"?**
> Unlike conventional fields that form localized pools under structural/stratigraphic traps, shale gas accumulations extend **laterally over thousands of square kilometres** with no well-defined gas-water contact.

---

## 2.2 Key Reservoir & Geochemical Properties of Shale

For a KTU 14-mark answer, you must address **at least the following parameters** with their typical ranges and screening thresholds:

### 2.2.1 Total Organic Carbon (TOC)
The TOC is the **mass fraction of organic matter** in the rock. Commercial shale gas plays require **TOC > 2 wt%**, with world-class plays (e.g., Marcellus) showing **TOC of 4–11 wt%**.

### 2.2.2 Thermal Maturity (Vitrinite Reflectance, Ro)
Indicates the degree to which kerogen has been thermally cracked. The Ro values for shale gas window are summarized below.

### 2.2.3 Mineralogy & Brittleness
Successful hydraulic fracturing requires the rock to be **brittle** (so it fractures cleanly) rather than **ductile** (so it deforms plastically). High **quartz + carbonate content (> 50 %)** and low **clay content (< 30 %)** are favourable indicators.

### 2.2.4 Thickness & Depth
Net shale pay thickness should exceed **30–50 m** for economic development. Optimal depths range from **1,500 m to 4,000 m**.

### 2.2.5 Gas-In-Place (GIP)
The volume of gas stored per unit volume or mass of rock.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are the **essential working tools** for any numerical question on shale gas resource estimation or production. Each formula is mathematically verifiable from first principles of petrophysics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All absolute-value and conditional symbols inside the markdown table are rendered using `\vert` / `\mid` to preserve table integrity.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variable Definitions | Units | Validity / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volumetric Gas-In-Place (Free Gas) | $GIP_{free} = \dfrac{A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_g}$ | $A$ = drainage area, $h$ = net pay, $\phi$ = porosity, $S_w$ = water saturation, $B_g$ = gas formation volume factor | m³ (or SCM) | Standard volumetric method; used for free gas |
| 2 | Adsorbed Gas (Langmuir Isotherm) | $G_C = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P}{P_L + P}$ | $V_L$ = Langmuir volume, $P$ = reservoir pressure, $P_L$ = Langmuir pressure | m³/tonne (rock) | Determines adsorbed gas capacity at any pressure |
| 3 | Adsorbed GIP | $GIP_{ads} = G_C \cdot \rho_b \cdot A \cdot h$ | $\rho_b$ = bulk density of shale | SCM | Combine with free gas for total GIP |
| 4 | Total Organic Carbon (Mass Fraction) | $TOC = \dfrac{m_{organic}}{m_{rock}} \times 100\,\%$ | $m_{organic}$ = mass of organic carbon | wt% | Screening parameter for shale gas potential |
| 5 | Gas Formation Volume Factor | $B_g = \dfrac{P_{sc} \cdot Z \cdot T}{P \cdot T_{sc}}$ | $Z$ = compressibility factor, $T$ = reservoir temperature, $P_{sc} = 0.101325$ MPa, $T_{sc} = 288.15$ K | m³/SCM | Converts reservoir gas volume to surface conditions |
| 6 | Recovery Factor (Shale Gas) | $RF = \dfrac{G_p}{GIP}$ | $G_p$ = cumulative gas produced, $GIP$ = original gas-in-place | dimensionless | Typical shale gas $RF$ = **10 % – 30 %** |
| 7 | Permeability from Porosity (Kozeny-Carman, qualitative) | $k = \dfrac{\phi^3}{C \cdot S^2 \cdot (1-\phi)^2}$ | $C$ = Kozeny constant, $S$ = specific surface area | m² or mD | Illustrates why low $\phi$ → ultra-low $k$ |
| 8 | Hydraulic Fracture Half-Length Estimate | $x_f = \dfrac{Q_{inj} \cdot t_{inj}}{4 \cdot h \cdot C_t \cdot \Delta P}$ | $Q_{inj}$ = injection rate, $t_{inj}$ = injection time, $C_t$ = total compressibility, $\Delta P$ = net pressure | m | Used in stimulation design |
| 9 | Original Gas-In-Place (Material Balance) | $G = \dfrac{V_{res} \cdot (1-S_{wi})}{B_{gi}}$ | $V_{res}$ = reservoir volume, $S_{wi}$ = initial water saturation, $B_{gi}$ = initial $B_g$ | SCM | Adapted for volumetric shale systems |
| 10 | Vitrinite Reflectance Window | $1.0\,\% \le R_o \le 2.5\,\%$ | $R_o$ = vitrinite reflectance | % | Defines dry-gas generation window |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations are the **backbone of every shale gas exploration workflow** worldwide:

- **Volumetric GIP** is used by exploration companies to declare "Technically Recoverable Resources" (TRR) in public filings and reserves audits (e.g., EIA/ARI reports for U.S. shale plays).
- **Langmuir isotherm** governs every **adsorption isotherm laboratory test** performed on shale core plugs using equipment such as the **Rubotherm™ magnetic suspension balance**.
- **Recovery factor** determines the economic cut-off: in shale gas wells, a typical well may produce economically for **20–30 years** but recover only **~15–25 %** of GIP — hence the importance of horizontal drilling and multi-stage fracturing to access more reservoir volume.
- The **TOC and Ro thresholds** drive the **play fairway mapping** that exploration geologists use to "high-grade" basins before drilling a single well.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> The "30-year tail production" of a shale gas well is *not* because of slow physics — it is because the reservoir *retains* gas in ultra-fine pore throats. Unlike conventional wells, shale wells never truly "water out" or "blow down" in the classical sense; they enter an **extended boundary-dominated flow regime** followed by an effectively infinite **linear flow** into the fracture face.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Policy:**
> Every algebraic transition below is explicitly expanded. **No step is skipped or summarized.** All multi-line equations are wrapped in `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` LaTeX blocks.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Gas-In-Place Equation for a Shale Reservoir

The volumetric method is a direct consequence of **conservation of mass** combined with the **definition of the gas formation volume factor**.

### Step 1 — Define the Bulk Reservoir Volume

The bulk volume of rock within the drainage area of a shale gas well is simply the product of area and pay thickness:

$$V_{bulk} = A \cdot h$$

where $A$ is the well's drainage area (in m²) and $h$ is the net pay thickness (in m). This gives $V_{bulk}$ in m³ of rock.

### Step 2 — Define the Pore Volume

Porosity $\phi$ is defined as the **fraction of bulk volume occupied by interconnected pore space**. Therefore the pore volume is:

$$V_{pore} = \phi \cdot V_{bulk} = \phi \cdot A \cdot h$$

### Step 3 — Account for Connate Water

In real reservoirs, a fraction of the pore space is occupied by **immovable connate water** $S_w$. The fraction available for hydrocarbon storage is therefore $(1 - S_w)$. The hydrocarbon pore volume (HCPV) becomes:

$$V_{HC} = V_{pore} \cdot (1 - S_w) = \phi \cdot A \cdot h \cdot (1 - S_w)$$

### Step 4 — Apply Gas Formation Volume Factor

Gas at reservoir pressure and temperature occupies **more volume** than the same mass of gas at standard surface conditions. The **gas formation volume factor** $B_g$ is defined as:

$$B_g = \frac{\text{Volume at reservoir conditions}}{\text{Volume at standard conditions}}$$

Therefore, to convert the HCPV (which contains gas at reservoir conditions) to **standard cubic metres (SCM)** of gas-in-place, we **divide** by $B_g$:

$$GIP = \frac{V_{HC}}{B_g}$$

### Step 5 — Combine the Expressions

Substituting $V_{HC}$ from Step 3 into the expression from Step 4:

$$
\begin{aligned}
GIP &= \frac{\phi \cdot A \cdot h \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_g}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **final volumetric GIP equation** for the free-gas component of a shale gas reservoir.

### Step 6 — Add the Adsorbed-Gas Component

Shale gas has an additional contribution from **adsorbed gas** that volumetric methods alone cannot capture. The Langmuir isotherm provides the gas content at reservoir pressure:

$$G_C = \frac{V_L \cdot P}{P_L + P}$$

Multiplying by the bulk rock mass $m_{rock} = \rho_b \cdot A \cdot h$ gives the **adsorbed gas-in-place**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
GIP_{ads} &= G_C \cdot \rho_b \cdot A \cdot h \\
&= \frac{V_L \cdot P}{P_L + P} \cdot \rho_b \cdot A \cdot h
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Total Gas-In-Place

The total gas-in-place in a shale formation is the sum of free and adsorbed components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
GIP_{total} &= GIP_{free} + GIP_{ads} \\
&= \frac{\phi \cdot A \cdot h \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_g} + \frac{V_L \cdot P}{P_L + P} \cdot \rho_b \cdot A \cdot h
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this derivation matter for KTU?**
> The examiner tests whether you understand that **conventional volumetric methods systematically underestimate shale gas resources** because they ignore the adsorbed phase. A complete shale GIP calculation must always include the Langmuir term.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (KTU-Style):**
> A shale formation has the following properties:
> - Drainage area: $A = 4.0 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^2$
> - Net pay thickness: $h = 50 \text{ m}$
> - Porosity: $\phi = 0.08$
> - Water saturation: $S_w = 0.25$
> - Gas formation volume factor: $B_g = 0.005 \text{ m}^3/\text{SCM}$
> - Langmuir volume: $V_L = 3.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{tonne}$
> - Langmuir pressure: $P_L = 4.0 \text{ MPa}$
> - Reservoir pressure: $P = 25 \text{ MPa}$
> - Bulk density: $\rho_b = 2.5 \text{ tonne/m}^3$
>
> **Calculate the total gas-in-place.**

### Step 1 — Compute Bulk Rock Volume

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{bulk} &= A \cdot h \\
&= (4.0 \times 10^5) \times 50 \\
&= 2.0 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Pore Volume

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{pore} &= \phi \cdot V_{bulk} \\
&= 0.08 \times 2.0 \times 10^7 \\
&= 1.6 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Hydrocarbon Pore Volume

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{HC} &= V_{pore} \cdot (1 - S_w) \\
&= 1.6 \times 10^6 \times (1 - 0.25) \\
&= 1.6 \times 10^6 \times 0.75 \\
&= 1.2 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Free Gas-In-Place

$$
\begin{aligned}
GIP_{free} &= \frac{V_{HC}}{B_g} \\
&= \frac{1.2 \times 10^6}{0.005} \\
&= 2.4 \times 10^8 \text{ SCM}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Adsorbed Gas Content (Langmuir)

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_C &= \frac{V_L \cdot P}{P_L + P} \\
&= \frac{3.5 \times 25}{4.0 + 25} \\
&= \frac{87.5}{29} \\
&\approx 3.017 \text{ m}^3/\text{tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute Rock Mass in Drainage Area

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{rock} &= \rho_b \cdot V_{bulk} \\
&= 2.5 \times 2.0 \times 10^7 \\
&= 5.0 \times 10^7 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Compute Adsorbed Gas-In-Place

$$
\begin{aligned}
GIP_{ads} &= G_C \cdot m_{rock} \\
&= 3.017 \times 5.0 \times 10^7 \\
&\approx 1.51 \times 10^8 \text{ SCM}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — Compute Total Gas-In-Place

$$
\begin{aligned}
GIP_{total} &= GIP_{free} + GIP_{ads} \\
&= 2.4 \times 10^8 + 1.51 \times 10^8 \\
&\approx 3.91 \times 10^8 \text{ SCM}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> The adsorbed gas contributes ≈ **38.6 %** of the total GIP. Ignoring the Langmuir term would have led to a 38.6 % underestimation of the resource — a critical error in any reserves estimation exercise.

### Step 9 — Compute Technically Recoverable Resources (TRR)

Assuming a recovery factor $RF = 0.20$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TRR &= RF \times GIP_{total} \\
&= 0.20 \times 3.91 \times 10^8 \\
&\approx 7.82 \times 10^7 \text{ SCM}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Shale Gas vs. Conventional Gas (Comparative Reasoning)

The following matrix consolidates the **comparative reasoning** expected in KTU 14-mark descriptive answers.

| Attribute | Conventional Gas | Shale Gas |
|---|---|---|
| Source Rock | Distinct from reservoir | Same as reservoir (self-sourced) |
| Migration | Primary + secondary (long distance) | Minimal to none (in-situ retention) |
| Trap | Structural / stratigraphic | Intrinsic (rock itself is seal) |
| Porosity | 10 % – 30 % | 2 % – 15 % |
| Permeability | 0.1 mD – 1000 mD | **< 0.001 mD (nano-Darcy)** |
| Storage | Free gas in pores | Free **+** Adsorbed on kerogen |
| Drive Mechanism | Water drive, gas expansion, solution gas | **Desorption + diffusion + Darcy flow in matrix** |
| Recovery Factor | 60 % – 90 % | **10 % – 30 %** |
| Well Type | Vertical or deviated | **Horizontal + multi-stage fracturing** |
| Decline Curve | Exponential, then stabilization | **Hyperbolic, steep initial decline** |
| Reserves Distribution | Localized | **Continuous, basin-centred** |
