<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three major environmental concerns associated with hydraulic fracturing for shale gas extraction.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Water resource depletion** — Each horizontal shale well requires **2–7 million gallons** of water for hydraulic fracturing, creating supply stress in arid regions.
2. **Groundwater contamination** — Risk of methane and frac-chemical migration to shallow aquifers through faulty well casing, abandoned wells, or fracture propagation.
3. **Induced seismicity** — Injection of large volumes of wastewater can trigger **M ≥ 2.0** earthquakes by elevating pore pressure on critically stressed faults.

*(Acceptable: air emissions, methane leakage, NORM, truck traffic, land use, noise)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define the term "Naturally Occurring Radioactive Materials (NORM)" in the context of shale gas production.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**NORM** refers to radioactive isotopes of elements naturally present in subsurface rock formations — primarily **Radium-226** and **Radium-228** from the uranium-238 and thorium-232 decay chains.

In shale gas operations, radium ions (Ra²⁺) **co-precipitate with barium sulfate (BaSO₄) scale** that forms in production tubing and surface equipment as pressure and temperature decrease. This concentrates NORM at the surface in:
- **Scale deposits** inside pipes and separators
- **Filter socks** and sludge from produced water treatment
- **Drill cuttings** brought to the surface

NORM is regulated under atomic energy acts and requires licensed handling, storage, and disposal to prevent worker exposure and environmental release.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often write "fracking causes earthquakes" **without** specifying the **mechanism** (pore-pressure increase → effective stress reduction → fault slip). Always state the physics.
> 2. The **3.2% methane leak threshold** is a frequently asked number — students lose marks by saying "shale gas is cleaner than coal" without the *quantitative boundary condition*.
> 3. For 14-mark questions, KTU requires **at least 4–5 distinct points** plus a **diagram or formula**. Always include a Mermaid-style or block flow representation, or a key equation.
> 4. **Do not confuse** flowback water (returns in 2–4 weeks, lower TDS) with **produced water** (returns over decades, very high TDS).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[A] (a)** Explain in detail the major **water-related environmental issues** in shale gas exploration. Discuss the sources, volumes, and treatment options for flowback and produced water. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Hydraulic fracturing for shale gas requires **2–7 million gallons of water per well**, mixed with **0.5%–2.0% chemical additives** and **2–6 million pounds of sand proppant**. This industrial-scale water use creates three categories of issues: **water sourcing, water consumption, and water disposal.**

**Issue 1 — Water Sourcing Stress (2 marks):**
- In arid plays (e.g., Permian Basin, Denver-Julesburg), fracturing competes with **agricultural and municipal demand**.
- Surface water withdrawal can lower **stream flows**, harming aquatic ecosystems.
- Some operators use **brackish groundwater** from deep saline aquifers to reduce freshwater demand.
- **Quantitative example:** A 4-well pad requires 16–28 million gallons ≈ 60,000–106,000 m³ of water.

**Issue 2 — Flowback and Produced Water (2 marks):**
- **Flowback water** returns in 2–4 weeks with TDS 10,000–200,000 mg/L.
- **Produced water** continues for the well's life, with TDS up to **300,000 mg/L**.
- Contaminants: heavy metals (Ba, Sr, As, Pb), NORM (Ra-226, Ra-228), residual frac chemicals, total dissolved solids.
- Management options:
  1. **Deep well injection** (UIC Class II wells) — linked to induced seismicity.
  2. **Treatment and recycling** — desalination (RO), mechanical evaporation, crystallization.
  3. **Centralized treatment facilities** — beneficial reuse in subsequent fracturing operations.

**Issue 3 — Surface and Groundwater Contamination (2 marks):**
- **Spills** during chemical handling, transfer, and storage.
- **Runoff** from well pads after rainfall.
- **Groundwater contamination pathways:**
  1. Subsurface fracture growth intersecting overlying aquifers.
  2. Cement/casing failures creating annular leakage.
  3. Migration along **abandoned legacy wells** acting as preferential conduits.

**Conclusion:**
Water management is the single largest **logistical and environmental challenge** in shale development. Recycling and brackish water sourcing are industry trends, but the **legacy of produced water disposal** remains a long-term liability.

---

**[A] (b)** Discuss **methane emissions and their climate impact** in shale gas operations. Derive the leakage rate at which shale gas electricity becomes no better than coal in climate-forcing terms. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Why Methane Matters (2 marks):**
- Methane (CH₄) has a **100-year GWP of 28–34** (IPCC AR5) — meaning 1 kg CH₄ traps 28–34× the heat of 1 kg CO₂.
- Atmospheric lifetime ≈ **12 years** (shorter than CO₂).
- Shale gas is **90–95% methane** → small fugitive losses have large climate consequences.

**Step 2 — Sources of Methane Emissions (2 marks):**
1. **Venting during well completion** — historically a major source; now reduced by "green completion" mandates.
2. **Fugitive leaks** — valves, flanges, compressor stations, pneumatic controllers.
3. **Equipment venting** — dehydrators, acid gas removal units.
4. **Liquids unloading** from gas wells.
5. **Pipeline and distribution leaks.**

**Step 3 — Derivation of the 3.2% Threshold (3 marks):**

Using the energy-equivalent climate parity condition:

$$E_{shale} = E_{coal}$$

Shale life-cycle emissions:

$$E_{shale} = E_{combustion} + E_{upstream,CH_4}$$

CO₂ from combustion (CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O):

$$E_{combustion} \approx 450 \text{ g CO}_2\text{eq/kWh} \quad (\text{modern NGCC plant})$$

Upstream methane leak contribution:

$$E_{upstream,CH_4} = L \cdot m_{CH_4, \text{prod}} \cdot GWP_{100}$$

Coal plant emissions:

$$E_{coal} \approx 1{,}000 \text{ g CO}_2\text{eq/kWh}$$

Setting up the equation and solving:

$$L^* = \dfrac{E_{coal} - E_{combustion}}{GWP_{100} \cdot (M_{CH_4}/M_{CO_2}) \cdot E_{combustion, \text{per mass CH}_4}}$$

$$\boxed{L^* \approx 3.2\%}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
- If fugitive leakage is **< 3.2%**, shale gas electricity is cleaner than coal.
- If leakage is **> 3.2%**, the climate benefit **disappears** or reverses.
- Field measurements (Howarth 2011, Stanford studies) have reported leakage rates in the **2.3%–11%** range depending on basin and operator practices.
- **Mitigation:** Mandatory **LDAR** (Leak Detection and Repair) programs, replacement of **pneumatic devices** with instrument air, vapor recovery units.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[B] (a)** Explain the phenomenon of **induced seismicity** associated with shale gas operations. Discuss the mechanisms, observed events, and mitigation strategies. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Induced seismicity refers to **earthquakes triggered by human industrial activities**, particularly wastewater injection and, to a lesser extent, hydraulic fracturing in shale gas operations.

**Mechanism 1 — Pore Pressure Diffusion (2 marks):**
Injection of fluids increases **pore pressure** ($p_p$) in subsurface formations. The **effective normal stress** on a fault is:

$$\sigma_n' = \sigma_n - p_p$$

As $p_p$ increases, $\sigma_n'$ decreases. When the fault reaches the **Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion**:

$$\tau \geq \tau_0 + \mu_s \cdot \sigma_n'$$

the fault slips, releasing seismic energy. The magnitude is bounded by the **seismic moment** of the slipped fault area.

**Mechanism 2 — Hydraulic Fracture Direct Activation (1 mark):**
The high-pressure frac fluid can:
- Lubricate pre-existing faults
- Reduce fault strength
- Cause slip on optimally oriented critically stressed planes

**Observed Events (1 mark):**
- **Oklahoma (2011–2016):** Magnitude 5.0+ earthquakes linked to **massive wastewater injection** from nearby shale plays.
- **Saskatchewan, Canada (2017):** M 4.5 event near a hydraulic fracturing operation.
- **Blackpool, UK (2011):** M 2.3 + M 1.5 events led to **temporary fracturing moratorium**.
- **Ohio (2014):** M 2.1 event within 0.5 km of an injection well.

**Mitigation Strategies (2 marks):**
1. **Traffic Light Systems** — Green: M < 2.0 (continue); Amber: M 2.0–4.0 (review); Red: M > 4.0 (suspend).
2. **Injection rate and volume limits** — control pressure diffusion.
3. **Geological screening** — avoid basement-rooted critically stressed faults.
4. **Real-time seismic monitoring** with downhole arrays.
5. **Use of produced-water recycling** to reduce disposal volumes.

---

**[B] (b)** Describe the **air quality impacts** of shale gas exploration. Include discussion of diesel emissions, methane leaks, VOCs, and the corresponding health effects. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Diesel Emissions (2 marks):**
- Drilling rigs, pumps, compressors, and trucks use **diesel engines** — significant sources of:
  - **NOx** (nitrogen oxides) — respiratory irritant, smog precursor.
  - **PM2.5 and PM10** (particulate matter) — cardiovascular and pulmonary risk.
  - **SOx** (sulfur oxides) — acid rain contributor.
- A multi-well pad generates **1,000–4,000 truck trips** for water, sand, and chemical delivery.
- **Mitigation:** Transition to **electric frac fleets (e-fracs)** powered by grid electricity or natural-gas-powered turbines reduces diesel by 60–80%.

**Methane Emissions (2 marks):**
- Discussed in detail in Question A(b). Primary climate concern.
- Sources: **fugitive leaks, venting, pneumatic devices, completions**.
- **Health effect:** While methane itself is non-toxic, the **VOC co-emissions** include **BTEX** (benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, xylene) — known carcinogens.

**VOCs and HAPs (2 marks):**
- **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs):** BTEX, hexane, heptane — emitted from flowback water tanks, dehydrators, and condensate storage.
- **Hazardous Air Pollutants (HAPs):** Benzene is classified as a **Group 1 IARC carcinogen**.
- **Ozone formation:** VOCs + NOx in sunlight → **ground-level ozone** (smog), causing respiratory illness.
- **Health effects:** Headache, nausea, neurological damage (acute); cancer risk (chronic).

**Mitigation (1 mark):**
1. **Vapor recovery units (VRUs)** on storage tanks.
2. **Combustion of waste gas** (flare or enclosed combustor).
3. **Green completion** technology (reduced emission completion, REC).
4. **LDAR programs** for fugitive VOC monitoring.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- [ ] Shale gas wells use **HVHF** = 2–7 Mgal water + 0.5–2.0% chemicals + 2–6 Mlbs sand.
- [ ] Five lifecycle phases: **Pad construction → Drilling → Fracking → Production → Abandonment**.
- [ ] **Methane climate-parity threshold:** ≈ **3.2%** leak rate.
- [ ] **Methane GWP₁₀₀ = 28–34**; GWP₂₀ = ≈ **84**.
- [ ] **Flowback water** (2–4 weeks) vs. **Produced water** (decades) — different volumes and TDS.
- [ ] **TDS range:** Flowback 10,000–200,000 mg/L; Produced up to 300,000 mg/L.
- [ ] **NORM** = Ra-226, Ra-228 co-precipitate with **BaSO₄ scale**.
- [ ] **Induced seismicity mechanism:** $p_p$ increase → $\sigma_n' = \sigma_n - p_p$ decrease → fault slip when Mohr-Coulomb exceeded.
- [ ] **Traffic light system:** Green M < 2.0; Amber M 2.0–4.0; Red M > 4.0.
- [ ] **Groundwater contamination pathways:** (1) Fracture growth; (2) Casing failure; (3) Legacy wells.
- [ ] **Air pollutants:** NOx, SOx, PM2.5, CO₂, CH₄, VOCs (BTEX), ozone precursors.
- [ ] **Land use:** 3–7 acres per multi-well pad; 4–16 wells per pad.
- [ ] **Truck traffic:** 1,000–4,000 trips per well — major diesel and road-wear source.
- [ ] **Noise levels:** 85–110 dB at pad boundary.
- [ ] **Mitigation hierarchy:** Source reduction → Recycling → Treatment → Disposal.
- [ ] **Green completion** = reduced emission completion (REC) — captures gas normally vented.
- [ ] **LDAR** = Leak Detection and Repair — key methane mitigation program.
- [ ] **E-fracs** = electric-powered hydraulic fracturing fleets — reduce diesel emissions.
- [ ] **Key risk equation (fracture containment):** $z_c = p_{f,\text{net}} / [(\rho_r - \rho_w) g]$.
- [ ] **Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion:** $\tau \geq \tau_0 + \mu_s \sigma_n'$.

> **End of KTU Module 2 — Environmental Issues in Shale Gas Exploration Notes**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Future Prospects of Shale Gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT752 - NON-CONVENTIONAL PETROLEUM RESOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Future Prospects of Shale Gas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Future Prospects of Shale Gas

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Shale Gas** is *natural gas* trapped within fine-grained, organic-rich, low-permeability (< 0.1 mD) sedimentary shale formations, occurring either as **free gas** in pore spaces and natural fractures or as **adsorbed gas** on the surface of kerogen and clay minerals. When the discussion shifts to **"Future Prospects,"** the term refers to the projected techno-economic, geopolitical, and environmental trajectory of shale gas as a critical pillar of the global energy mix over the next 30–50 years, encompassing resource estimation, production forecasting, capital expenditure trends, breakeven economics, decarbonization pathways, and policy frameworks that will determine whether shale gas serves as a *transition fuel* or a *bridge fuel* in the move toward net-zero emissions.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT752 – Module 2)**, future prospects specifically emphasize:
- The estimated **Technically Recoverable Resources (TRR)** at the global, national, and basin scale.
- The **Estimated Ultimate Recovery (EUR)** per well and per play.
- The **Breakeven Price** below which shale projects become economically viable.
- The long-term role of shale gas in **energy security, import substitution, and emissions reduction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Shale Gas):** Students must be able to distinguish between **Original Gas In Place (OGIP)** and **Technically Recoverable Resources (TRR)**, identify the global "Big Four" shale plays, evaluate India's domestic shale prospects, and critically appraise the environmental footprint of hydraulic fracturing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a giant **sponge cake (the shale formation)** soaked in honey (the natural gas). In a conventional reservoir, the honey pools in a thin, porous layer — you just tilt the cake and the honey pours out freely. In a **shale reservoir**, the honey is locked *inside the cake itself* — trapped in tiny pores and stuck to the cake's inner surfaces. To get it out, you must **crack the cake open (hydraulic fracturing)** and let the honey seep out slowly.

**"Future Prospects"** is essentially the question: *"How big is this cake? How much honey can we recover? At what cost? And is it worth eating compared to other sweeteners?"* It integrates geology, drilling engineering, economics, and environmental science into a single forecast.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following industry-standard metrics govern all quantitative discussions of shale gas prospects:

- **1 BCF (Billion Cubic Feet) = 0.0283 BCM (Billion Cubic Meters)**
- **1 TCF (Trillion Cubic Feet) ≈ 0.0283 TCM**
- **1 BOE (Barrel of Oil Equivalent) ≈ 5,800 cubic feet of natural gas**
- **1 Quad BTU ≈ 1 TCF of natural gas**
- **Global annual natural gas consumption ≈ 4,000 BCM (≈ 142 TCF)**
- **Heating value of methane ≈ 1,000 BTU/scf (37.3 MJ/m³)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Resource Classification:** Resources are conventionally categorized as **Discovered vs. Undiscovered**, then sub-divided into **Recoverable vs. Unrecoverable**, and finally classified by certainty into **Proved (1P), Probable (2P), and Possible (3P)** reserves under the PRMS (Petroleum Resources Management System) framework.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Framework — Five Pillars of Shale Gas Future Prospects

The "future prospects" of shale gas are evaluated along **five interrelated pillars**. Each pillar must be quantitatively assessed to project the trajectory of a shale play.

### Pillar 1: Geological Endowment (How much gas *exists*?)
- **Original Gas In Place (OGIP):** Total volume of gas stored in the formation, independent of recoverability.
- **Total Organic Carbon (TOC):** Weight percent of organic matter in the shale — a direct proxy for hydrocarbon generation potential. *Sweet spot:* TOC > 2 wt%.
- **Thermal Maturity (Ro):** Vitrinite reflectance, indicating whether the kerogen has generated gas. *Gas window:* Ro = 1.0% – 2.0%.
- **Thickness & Depth:** Optimal shale plays are 30–90 m thick at depths of 1,500–4,000 m.

### Pillar 2: Technically Recoverable Resources (How much can we *get out*?)
- **TRR = OGIP × Recovery Factor (RF)**
- Typical shale gas RF: **5% – 30%** (compared to 60%–80% for conventional gas).
- The RF depends on fracturing efficiency, adsorption isotherm behavior, and matrix permeability.

### Pillar 3: Production Decline & EUR (How *fast* and how *much* per well?)
- Shale wells exhibit **hyperbolic or exponential decline**, with first-year decline rates of **60%–80%**.
- **EUR (Estimated Ultimate Recovery)** per well: typically **1–10 BCF** in major US plays; 2–4 BCF in India's preliminary plays.
- **30-year cumulative production** is the standard reporting horizon.

### Pillar 4: Economic Breakeven (At what price is it *viable*?)
- **Breakeven Price** = (CapEx + OpEx + Taxes + Royalty + Transport) / EUR
- Current global breakeven prices range from **$1.50/MMBtu (Permian Basin core)** to **>$6/MMBtu (frontier plays)**.
- A shale project is economic when Henry Hub price ≥ Breakeven Price.

### Pillar 5: Environmental & Social License (Can it be *done sustainably*?)
- **Carbon Footprint:** Lifecycle CO₂ emissions of shale gas ≈ **30–50% lower than coal** but **higher than conventional gas** due to methane leakage during completion.
- **Water Consumption:** 10,000–30,000 m³ per multi-well pad.
- **Induced Seismicity Risk:** Correlates with deep injection wells (>2 km).
- **Methane Leakage Rate:** Must remain < 3% to retain climate advantage.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Governing Equation / Metric | Typical Range or Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Original Gas In Place (OGIP)** | $OGIP = \dfrac{A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{w})}{B_{g}}$ | SCM, BCF, TCF | Total gas volume in place |
| 2 | **Gas Formation Volume Factor** | $B_{g} = \dfrac{0.0283 \cdot z \cdot T}{P}$ | res m³/scm, scf/rcf | Converts surface to reservoir volume |
| 3 | **Technically Recoverable Resource** | $TRR = OGIP \times R_{F}$ | TRR in BCF, RF dimensionless | Used in reserve booking |
| 4 | **Recovery Factor (Shale Gas)** | Empirical; from decline analysis | **0.05 – 0.30** | Drive of EUR |
| 5 | **Adsorbed Gas Volume (Langmuir)** | $G_{s} = V_{L} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P + P_{L}}$ | SCF/ton, scf/ton rock | Models gas adsorbed on kerogen |
| 6 | **Decline Curve (Exponential)** | $q(t) = q_{i} \cdot e^{-D t}$ | q in Mscf/day, D in 1/day | Production forecasting |
| 7 | **Decline Curve (Hyperbolic)** | $q(t) = \dfrac{q_{i}}{(1 + b D_{i} t)^{1/b}}$ | b-factor 0 < b ≤ 1 | More realistic for shale |
| 8 | **Cumulative Production** | $N_{p} = \dfrac{q_{i} - q(t)}{D}$ | Mscf, BCF | EUR computation |
| 9 | **Breakeven Price** | $P_{be} = \dfrac{CapEx + OpEx_{life}}{EUR}$ | $/MMBtu | Economic screening |
| 10 | **Energy Return on Investment** | $EROI = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}}$ | Dimensionless | Sustainability indicator |
| 11 | **Methane Leakage Rate** | $L = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{leaked}}{\dot{m}_{produced}} \times 100\%$ | % | Climate impact |
| 12 | **CO₂ Emission Factor (Shale Gas)** | Lifecycle emission | **~50 g CO₂eq/MJ** | Comparison vs. coal (~95 g) |
| 13 | **TOC Required for Gas Window** | Empirical | **TOC ≥ 2 wt%** | Play screening |
| 14 | **Thermal Maturity (Ro)** | Vitrinite Reflectance | **Ro = 1.0 – 2.0%** | Gas window classification |
| 15 | **World Shale Gas TRR (EIA 2013)** | Total global estimate | **7,299 TCF** | Geopolitical benchmarking |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Safety Note for KTU Markdown:** All absolute-value and conditional bars in this sheet are written using `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $P - P_{L}\vert$) so that the markdown table parser never misreads a vertical pipe `|` as a column delimiter.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations are the **lingua franca** of:
- **National Oil Companies (ONGC, Shell, ExxonMobil)** for reserve booking and capex allocation.
- **Financial analysts (Goldman Sachs, Wood Mackenzie)** for valuation of upstream portfolios.
- **Policy makers (MoPNG, EIA, IEA)** for projecting energy import dependence and emission trajectories.
- **Service companies (Halliburton, Schlumberger)** for designing fracture stimulation jobs and forecasting revenue per stage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Original Gas In Place (OGIP) for a Shale Play

**Problem Setup.** Consider a candidate shale play spread across area $A$ (in acres), with average net pay thickness $h$ (ft), average porosity $\phi$, average water saturation $S_{w}$, and gas formation volume factor $B_{g}$ (res bbl/scf). Derive the OGIP in standard cubic feet (scf).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Rock Volume.** The bulk volume of shale rock within the drainage area is:

$$V_{bulk} = A \cdot h$$

where $A$ is in ft² and $h$ is in ft, yielding bulk volume in ft³.

**Step 2 — Pore Volume.** Multiply the bulk volume by porosity to obtain the connected pore space:

$$V_{pore} = A \cdot h \cdot \phi$$

This represents the total interconnected void space capable of storing fluids.

**Step 3 — Hydrocarbon Pore Volume.** Of the pore volume, a fraction $(1 - S_{w})$ is occupied by hydrocarbons (gas + condensate). Water saturation $S_{w}$ occupies the remainder:

$$V_{HC} = A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{w})$$

**Step 4 — Convert Reservoir Volume to Surface Volume.** Reservoir conditions (high P, high T) cause gas to occupy a smaller volume at surface. The formation volume factor $B_{g}$ (in res bbl/scf) does this conversion. Therefore:

$$OGIP = \dfrac{A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{w})}{B_{g}}$$

**Step 5 — Practical Units Conversion.** For field use, the areal term is often expressed in acres and the result is converted to BCF using the constant $43,560$ ft²/acre. Substituting:

$$OGIP \; [BCF] = \dfrac{43{,}560 \cdot A \; [acres] \cdot h \; [ft] \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{w})}{B_{g} \; [res\,bbl/scf] \times 10^{9}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $B_{g}$ in the denominator?** Because reservoir barrels (at high P, T) compress to *fewer* stock-tank barrels/scf at surface. Dividing by $B_{g}$ *expands* the reservoir volume to its surface-condition equivalent — exactly what we report as OGIP.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Technically Recoverable Resource (TRR) from Decline Curve

**Problem Setup.** A horizontal shale well in the Marcellus produces at initial rate $q_{i} = 12{,}000$ Mcf/day with first-year exponential decline $D = 0.0045$ /day. The operator plans to plug and abandon the well when the rate drops to $q_{a} = 100$ Mcf/day. Derive the **Estimated Ultimate Recovery (EUR)**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Decline Rate Converted to Annual Basis.**

$$D_{annual} = D_{daily} \times 365 = 0.0045 \times 365 = 1.6425 \, /yr$$

**Step 2 — Time to Reach Abandonment Rate.** From the exponential decline law:

$$\begin{aligned}
q(t) &= q_{i} \cdot e^{-Dt} \\
100 &= 12{,}000 \cdot e^{-0.0045 \, t} \\
e^{-0.0045 \, t} &= \dfrac{100}{12{,}000} = 0.00833 \\
-0.0045 \, t &= \ln(0.00833) = -4.787 \\
t_{a} &= \dfrac{4.787}{0.0045} = 1{,}063.8 \; \text{days} \approx 2.91 \; \text{years}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Cumulative Production (EUR) Under Exponential Decline.** For exponential decline, the cumulative production from $t = 0$ to $t_{a}$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
EUR &= \int_{0}^{t_{a}} q(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{t_{a}} q_{i} \cdot e^{-Dt} \, dt \\
&= q_{i} \left[ \dfrac{-e^{-Dt}}{D} \right]_{0}^{t_{a}} = \dfrac{q_{i}}{D} \left( 1 - e^{-D t_{a}} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
EUR &= \dfrac{12{,}000 \, \text{Mcf/day}}{0.0045 \, /\text{day}} \times \left( 1 - 0.00833 \right) \\
&= 2{,}666{,}667 \times 0.99167 \\
&= 2{,}644{,}440 \; \text{Mcf} \\
&\approx 2.64 \; \text{BCF}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.** The well produces **2.64 BCF over 2.91 years** before reaching the 100 Mcf/day economic limit. At a wellhead gas price of **$3.50/MMBtu**, gross revenue ≈ **$9.25 million** — sufficient to justify the typical Marcellus CapEx of $6–8 million per well.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always quote the abandonment rate assumption, the decline model (exponential vs. hyperbolic), and the time horizon explicitly. Examiners reward **clarity of assumptions** as much as final numbers.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Breakeven Price Sensitivity Analysis

**Problem Setup.** A shale project requires CapEx = **$8 million/well** and lifetime OpEx = **$2 million/well**. EUR = **2.5 BCF**. Royalty + Tax = **20% of revenue**. Compute the breakeven Henry Hub price.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Total Cost per Well.**

$$C_{total} = CapEx + OpEx_{life} = 8 + 2 = 10 \; \text{million USD}$$

**Step 2 — Net Revenue Required After Royalty.** Since 20% goes to government/landowner:

$$R_{required} = \dfrac{10}{1 - 0.20} = \dfrac{10}{0.80} = 12.5 \; \text{million USD}$$

**Step 3 — Volume in MMBtu.** Using $1 \, BCF = 10^{6} \, MMBtu$:

$$V = 2.5 \times 10^{6} \; \text{MMBtu}$$

**Step 4 — Breakeven Price.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{be} &= \dfrac{12.5 \times 10^{6} \; \text{USD}}{2.5 \times 10^{6} \; \text{MMBtu}} \\
&= 5.00 \; \text{USD/MMBtu}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Sensitivity to EUR.** If EUR improves to 5 BCF (due to longer laterals, better completions):

$$P_{be} = \dfrac{12.5 \times 10^{6}}{5 \times 10^{6}} = 2.50 \; \text{USD/MMBtu}$$

This demonstrates why operators aggressively pursue **EUR enhancement technologies** (super-long laterals, simul-frac, re-fracs).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Top 10 Global Shale Gas Plays

| Rank | Play (Country) | TRR (TCF) | Avg. EUR (BCF/well) | Breakeven ($/MMBtu) | Primary Operator |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Marcellus (USA) | **1,484** | 3.0 – 7.0 | 1.50 – 2.50 | EQT, Range Resources |
| 2 | Haynesville (USA) | **304** | 4.0 – 8.0 | 2.00 – 3.00 | Chesapeake, ExxonMobil |
| 3 | Vaca Muerta (Argentina) | **802** | 2.5 – 5.0 | 2.50 – 4.00 | YPF, Shell |
| 4 | Sichuan Basin (China) | **1,275** | 1.0 – 3.0 | 4.00 – 7.00 | PetroChina, Sinopec |
| 5 | Eagle Ford (USA) | **335** | 2.0 – 4.0 | 2.00 – 3.50 | EOG, ConocoPhillips |
| 6 | Permian Wolfcamp (USA) | **419** | 2.5 – 5.0 | 1.50 – 2.50 | Pioneer, ExxonMobil |
| 7 | Western Canada (Canada) | **573** | 1.5 – 4.0 | 2.50 – 4.50 | Tourmaline, ARC |
| 8 | Cambay Basin (India) | **22** | 0.5 – 2.0 | 5.00 – 8.00 | ONGC |
| 9 | Damodar Valley (India) | **15** | 0.5 – 1.5 | 6.00 – 9.00 | ONGC, RIL |
| 10 | Karoo Basin (South Africa) | **390** | Frontier | >8.00 | Falcon (planned) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Source of TRR figures:** U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA), *Technically Recoverable Shale Oil and Shale Gas Resources*, 2013 — the most cited global reference dataset. Updated estimates have been published by EIA in 2024 for select basins.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Future Prospects — Five-Pillar Decision Framework

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **interconnected decision framework** used by operators, governments, and analysts to evaluate the future prospects of any shale play.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shale Gas Play Identification] --> B{Geological Endowment?}
    B -- Yes --> C{TOC > 2% AND Ro 1-2%?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Technically Recoverable Resource TRR]
    C -- No --> E[Play Rejected - Sub-economic Source Rock]
    D --> F{Production Decline Test}
    F --> G[Estimated Ultimate Recovery EUR]
    G --> H{EUR > 1.5 BCF per well?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Breakeven Price Calculation]
    H -- No --> J[Unconventional Economics - High Risk]
    I --> K{Breakeven less than 5 USD per MMBtu?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Economic Reserves Booking]
    K -- No --> M[Watchlist - Technology Dependent]
    L --> N{Environmental Impact Assessment}
    N --> O{Methane Leakage less than 3% AND Water Use Acceptable?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Commercial Development Approved]
    O -- No --> Q[Mitigation Required - Re-engineering]
    P --> R[Energy Security and Emission Reduction Benefits]
    Q --> S[Social License to Operate Negotiated]
    R --> T[Future Prospect: VIABLE TRANSITION FUEL]
    S --> T
    M --> U[Future Prospect: CONDITIONAL]
    J --> U
    E --> V[Future Prospect: NOT VIABLE]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Shale Gas Value Chain

The following diagram maps the **end-to-end value chain** that determines the future prospects of shale gas, from subsurface reservoir to end-use markets.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UP[Upstream Domain]
```

## 4.3 Trajectory of Global Shale Gas Production (Forecast Scenario)

The following diagram compares the **historical** (2010–2023) and **projected** (2024–2050) shale gas production under the IEA's *Stated Policies* vs. *Net Zero* scenarios.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[2010: 5 TCF - USA Dominant] --> P2[2015: 15 TCF - USA Boom]
    P2 --> P3[2020: 27 TCF - COVID Dip]
    P3 --> P4[2023: 33 TCF - Recovery and Argentina Ramp]
    P4 --> S1{Scenario Fork}
    S1 -- Stated Policies --> S2[2030: 42 TCF - China Argentina Pickup]
    S2 --> S3[2040: 50 TCF - Plateau Phase]
    S3 --> S4[2050: 55 TCF - Mature Industry]

    S1 -- Net Zero 2050 --> N1[2030: 30 TCF - Methane Regulations]
    N1 --> N2[2040: 20 TCF - Substitution by Renewables]
    N2 --> N3[2050: 12 TCF - Residual Use Only]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** When asked about "future prospects," always present **two scenarios** — a *business-as-usual* projection and a *climate-constrained* projection. This dual framing demonstrates **CO3 (engineering thinking)** and **CO5 (engineering sustainability)** competencies under the KTU 2024 OBE framework.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q.** Define "Technically Recoverable Resource (TRR)" of shale gas. How is it distinguished from Original Gas In Place (OGIP)?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **OGIP** (Original Gas In Place) is the **total volume of natural gas** estimated to be contained within a shale formation at initial reservoir conditions, expressed in standard cubic feet (scf) or cubic meters. It includes gas present in pore spaces (free gas) **and** gas adsorbed onto kerogen/clay surfaces. OGIP is calculated using the volumetric equation:
>
> $$OGIP = \dfrac{A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{w})}{B_{g}}$$
>
> **TRR** (Technically Recoverable Resource) is the **fraction of OGIP** that can be extracted using **currently available technology** (e.g., horizontal drilling + multi-stage hydraulic fracturing), regardless of economic viability. The two are related by the recovery factor $R_{F}$:
>
> $$TRR = OGIP \times R_{F}$$
>
> For shale gas, $R_{F}$ typically ranges from **5% to 30%**, much lower than conventional gas (60–80%) because of ultra-low matrix permeability.
>
> **Key Distinction:** OGIP is a *geological* estimate (what is *in the rock*), whereas TRR is an *engineering* estimate (what we can *physically extract* given today's technology). **(3 Marks)**

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q.** List any **four key parameters** that must be evaluated when assessing the future prospects of a shale gas basin.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The four essential screening parameters are:

1. **Total Organic Carbon (TOC)** — must be ≥ 2 wt% to indicate sufficient hydrocarbon source potential. **(0.75 Marks)**
2. **Thermal Maturity (Ro)** — vitrinite reflectance between 1.0% and 2.0% indicates the kerogen is in the gas window. **(0.75 Marks)**
3. **Burial Depth & Thickness** — optimal depth 1,500–4,000 m; thickness ≥ 30 m for economic hydraulic fracturing. **(0.75 Marks)**
4. **Brittleness & Fracability** — high quartz/carbonate content (> 40%) and low clay content (< 30%) enable complex fracture networks. **(0.75 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**Q.** *"Shale gas is projected to be the most significant unconventional gas resource globally over the next three decades."* Critically evaluate this statement with reference to **(a)** global technically recoverable resources, **(b)** the top producing shale plays, and **(c)** the major challenges and opportunities facing shale gas development.

#### Part (a) — Global Technically Recoverable Resources (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the Global TRR Dataset.** According to the U.S. EIA (2013) and subsequent updates, the world holds an estimated **7,299 TCF of technically recoverable shale gas resources**, distributed across 41 countries. Updated 2024 estimates from Rystad Energy place this at approximately **14,000 TCF of risked prospective resources**.

**Step 2 — Top 10 Countries by TRR (EIA Data).** Ranked shale gas resources:

| Rank | Country | TRR (TCF) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | China | 1,275 |
| 2 | Argentina | 802 |
| 3 | Algeria | 707 |
| 4 | USA | 622 |
| 5 | Canada | 573 |
| 6 | Mexico | 545 |
| 7 | Australia | 437 |
| 8 | South Africa | 390 |
| 9 | Russia | 285 |
| 10 | Brazil | 245 |

**(2 Marks for the data table)**

**Step 3 — Regional Trends.** North America holds ~25% of the global TRR but accounts for **>90% of current production**, demonstrating that geology alone does not determine production — *technology, infrastructure, land access, and water availability* are equally critical. China, despite having the largest resource, has struggled with complex tectonics, deep targets (> 3,500 m), and mountainous terrain. Argentina's Vaca Muerta is rapidly emerging as the next great play outside North America. **(2 Marks)**

**Step 4 — Growth Projections.** The IEA's *World Energy Outlook 2023* projects global shale gas production to grow from **33 TCF (2023)** to **55 TCF (2050)** in the Stated Policies scenario, accounting for **~30% of total natural gas supply** by mid-century. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 5 — India's Position.** India's assessed shale gas resource (Cambay, KG, Cauvery, Damodar Valley, Upper Assam) totals **96 TCF** in the most optimistic 2013 EIA estimate, though ONGC pilot data suggests commercial viability remains unproven. **(1 Mark)**

**Synthesis (1 Mark):** The combination of vast global TRR, advanced extraction technology, and rising LNG demand supports the projection that shale gas will remain a dominant unconventional gas resource for the next 30 years, though its peak may arrive sooner than oil & gas due to substitution by renewables.

#### Part (b) — Top Producing Shale Plays (4 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The **"Big Five" producing shale gas plays** are:

1. **Marcellus Shale (USA, Appalachian Basin):** TRR 1,484 TCF, ~5,000 horizontal wells drilled per year, breakeven $1.50–$2.50/MMBtu. The most prolific and economic gas play globally.
2. **Haynesville Shale (USA, Louisiana–East Texas):** TRR 304 TCF, deeper and hotter than Marcellus, EUR 4–8 BCF/well, breakeven $2.00–$3.00/MMBtu.
3. **Eagle Ford Shale (USA, South Texas):** TRR 335 TCF, condensate-rich window makes it economically attractive.
4. **Vaca Muerta (Argentina, Neuquén Basin):** TRR 802 TCF, second-most-prolific play outside USA, breakeven $2.50–$4.00/MMBtu, government policy supportive.
5. **Sichuan Basin (China):** TRR 1,275 TCF, technically challenging due to deep, tectonically deformed formations; breakeven $4.00–$7.00/MMBtu.

**(3 Marks for identification + key metrics; 1 Mark for comparison)**

#### Part (c) — Challenges and Opportunities (3 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Category | Challenges | Opportunities |
|---|---|---|
| **Geological** | Deep, overpressured, faulted formations | TOC-rich shales in 40+ countries |
| **Economic** | High decline rates; price volatility | Breakeven <$2/MMBtu in core US plays |
| **Environmental** | Methane leakage; water use; induced seismicity | 30–50% lower CO₂ than coal |
| **Social** | Local opposition; truck traffic | Rural job creation; energy security |
| **Geopolitical** | Russia–Ukraine reshapes European gas market | LNG export boom; import substitution for India/China |

**(3 Marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q.** Estimate the **Estimated Ultimate Recovery (EUR)** of a horizontal shale gas well and compute its **breakeven price** using the following data:
- Initial production rate $q_{i} = 10{,}000$ Mcf/day
- Exponential decline rate $D = 0.005$ per day
- Abandonment rate $q_{a} = 200$ Mcf/day
- CapEx = $7.5 million per well
- Lifetime OpEx = $1.5 million per well
- Royalty + Tax = 22% of gross revenue
- Wellhead gas price = $3.80 per MMBtu
- Heating value: 1 Mcf = 1 MMBtu

#### Part (a) — Compute EUR and Abandonment Time (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Time to Reach Abandonment.** Applying exponential decline:

$$\begin{aligned}
q(t) &= q_{i} \cdot e^{-Dt} \\
200 &= 10{,}000 \cdot e^{-0.005 t} \\
e^{-0.005 t} &= 0.02 \\
-0.005 t &= \ln(0.02) = -3.912 \\
t_{a} &= \dfrac{3.912}{0.005} = 782.4 \; \text{days} \approx 2.14 \; \text{years}
\end{aligned}$$

**(2 Marks)**

**Step 2 — Compute EUR (Cumulative Production).**

$$\begin{aligned}
EUR &= \dfrac{q_{i}}{D} \left(1 - e^{-D t_{a}}\right) \\
&= \dfrac{10{,}000}{0.005} \times (1 - 0.02) \\
&= 2{,}000{,}000 \times 0.98 \\
&= 1{,}960{,}000 \; \text{Mcf} \\
&= 1.96 \; \text{BCF}
\end{aligned}$$

**(3 Marks)**

**Step 3 — Validate Result Using Direct Integration.**

$$\begin{aligned}
EUR &= \int_{0}^{782.4} 10{,}000 \cdot e^{-0.005 t} \, dt \\
&= 10{,}000 \left[ \dfrac{-e^{-0.005 t}}{0.005} \right]_{0}^{782.4} \\
&= 10{,}000 \times 200 \times \left(1 - e^{-3.912}\right) \\
&= 2{,}000{,}000 \times 0.98 = 1.96 \; \text{BCF} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**(2 Marks)**

#### Part (b) — Compute Breakeven Price and Net Profit Margin (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Cost.**

$$C_{total} = 7.5 + 1.5 = 9.0 \; \text{million USD}$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 2 — Net Revenue Required After Royalty/Tax.** Since 22% goes to government/landowner:

$$R_{required} = \dfrac{9.0}{1 - 0.22} = \dfrac{9.0}{0.78} = 11.538 \; \text{million USD}$$

**(2 Marks)**

**Step 3 — Breakeven Price.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{be} &= \dfrac{11.538 \times 10^{6} \; \text{USD}}{1.96 \times 10^{6} \; \text{MMBtu}} \\
&= 5.89 \; \text{USD/MMBtu}
\end{aligned}$$

**(2 Marks)**

**Step 4 — Compare with Market Price and Compute Profit.** Market price = $3.80/MMBtu, which is **below breakeven ($5.89/MMBtu)**.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Gross Revenue} &= 3.80 \times 1.96 \times 10^{6} = 7.448 \; \text{million USD} \\
\text{Net Revenue (after 22%)} &= 7.448 \times 0.78 = 5.809 \; \text{million USD} \\
\text{Net Loss} &= 5.809 - 9.0 = -3.191 \; \text{million USD}
\end{aligned}$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 5 — Engineering Recommendation (1 Mark):** At a market price of $3.80/MMBtu, the well is **uneconomic**. To make it viable, the operator must either: (i) increase EUR to ≥ 3.04 BCF (via longer laterals, simul-frac, re-frac), (ii) reduce CapEx to ≤ $4.85 million, or (iii) wait for gas prices to rise above $5.89/MMBtu.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistakes** — Always convert $q_i$ from Mcf/day to scf/year when computing cumulative production over multi-year horizons. (Common 2-mark loss)
> 2. **Forgetting Royalty/Tax in Breakeven** — Many students compute breakeven on gross revenue instead of net revenue, underestimating true cost. (Common 3-mark loss)
> 3. **Confusing OGIP with TRR** — OGIP is *all the gas in the rock*; TRR is only what can be *technically* extracted. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring the $b$-factor in Hyperbolic Decline** — Modern shale wells follow *hyperbolic* (not pure exponential) decline with $b \approx 0.5–1.0$. A 2-mark deduction if not mentioned.
> 5. **Forgetting to State Assumptions** — Always declare: "assuming exponential decline, constant $B_g$, abandonment rate, and 30-year horizon."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Future Prospects of Shale Gas"**

- **Definition:** Shale gas is natural gas trapped in fine-grained, organic-rich shale formations, requiring horizontal drilling + multi-stage hydraulic fracturing.
- **Global TRR:** ~7,299 TCF (EIA 2013) to ~14,000 TCF (Rystad 2024, risked prospective).
- **Top 5 Countries by TRR:** China, Argentina, Algeria, USA, Canada.
- **"Big Five" Producing Plays:** Marcellus, Haynesville, Eagle Ford, Vaca Muerta, Sichuan Basin.
- **India's Shale Gas Resources:** ~96 TCF across Cambay, KG, Cauvery, Damodar Valley, Upper Assam (EIA 2013); ONGC pilot results below expectations.
- **OGIP Equation:** $OGIP = \dfrac{A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_g}$ — total gas in place.
- **TRR Equation:** $TRR = OGIP \times R_F$, where $R_F = 5\%–30\%$ for shale.
- **Decline Law:** $q(t) = q_i \cdot e^{-Dt}$ (exponential) or hyperbolic with $b \approx 0.5–1.0$.
- **EUR Formula:** $EUR = \dfrac{q_i}{D}(1 - e^{-D t_a})$ for exponential decline.
- **Breakeven Price Formula:** $P_{be} = \dfrac{CapEx + OpEx_{life}}{EUR \cdot (1 - \text{Tax/Royalty})}$.
- **US Marcellus Breakeven:** $1.50–$2.50/MMBtu (lowest globally).
- **Methane Leakage Threshold:** Must be < 3% to maintain climate advantage over coal.
- **CO₂ Emission Factor:** ~50 g CO₂eq/MJ for shale gas vs. ~95 g for coal.
- **Energy Return on Investment (EROI):** Modern shale gas ≈ 20–30:1.
- **Key Risk Factors:** Induced seismicity, groundwater contamination, high water use (10,000–30,000 m³ per pad), truck traffic, local opposition.
- **Global Production Outlook:** IEA projects 33 TCF (2023) → 55 TCF (2050) under Stated Policies.
- **Future Role:** *Bridge fuel* in the energy transition; competing with renewables + LNG + hydrogen.
- **Strategic Importance for India:** Potential to reduce LNG import bill (currently > $10 billion/year) and enhance energy security, provided techno-economic challenges are resolved.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Formation and Properties of Coal Bed Methane. Thermodynamics of Coal Bed Methane. Exploration and Evaluation of CBM. Hydrofracturing of Coal Seam. Production Installation and Surface Facilities. Well Operations and Production Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Coal Bed Methane (CBM) — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coal Bed Methane (CBM)**, also referred to as **Coal Seam Gas (CSG)** or **Virgin Coalmine Methane (VCM)** when extracted prior to mining, is a form of **unconventional natural gas** stored primarily by **adsorption** onto the internal microporous surface area of coal matrices (micropores of 0.5–1.0 nm diameter), with secondary storage as **free gas** within coal cleats (natural fractures) and minor amounts as **dissolved gas** in formation water.

The gas is generated during the **coalification** process — the progressive thermal and biochemical transformation of plant debris (peat → lignite → sub-bituminous → bituminous → anthracite) — yielding predominantly **methane (CH₄)** with smaller fractions of **CO₂, N₂, and heavier hydrocarbons (C₂+)**.

**Key Standard Metrics (KTU-emphasized):**
- **Gas Content (G_c):** 1–25 m³/ton (typically), measured in **scf/ton** (standard cubic feet per short ton)
- **Coal Rank Indicator:** Vitrinite Reflectance **R_o (%)**: 0.8–6.0% (for CBM generation)
- **Critical Desorption Pressure (P_cd):** Reservoir pressure at which gas begins to desorb
- **Adsorption Capacity:** Typically **5–30 cm³/g** of coal (DArcy, 0.5 nm pore basis)
- **Sorbed Gas Fraction:** **>95%** of total gas in-place (uniquely high vs. conventional gas)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Microscopic Sponge with Cracks"**

Imagine a **charcoal briquette** you use for a barbecue. The coal is essentially a **rigid, dual-porosity sponge**:
- The **matrix** (the bulk of the briquette) is riddled with **microscopic pores** (the "sponge holes"). Methane molecules cling to the inner walls of these pores the way **dust sticks to a sticky surface** — this is **adsorption**.
- The **cleats** are the **natural cracks** running through the briquette, like the fractures in a dry lakebed. These hold **free gas** and provide pathways for water and gas to flow toward a wellbore.

When you drill into this "briquette underground" and pump out the water that initially saturates the cleats, the reservoir pressure drops. The methane "dust" on the pore walls then **unsticks (desorbs)**, diffuses through the matrix, migrates to the cleats, and flows to the well. CBM production is therefore a **depressurization-driven** process — *the opposite* of conventional gas, where you push gas out by maintaining pressure. Here, you **pull the pressure down** to liberate the gas.

## 1.3 Why CBM is a Strategic Unconventional Resource

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Strategic Relevance**

CBM is classified as an **unconventional gas** under India's **New Exploration and Licensing Policy (NELP)** and **Open Acreage Licensing Policy (OALP)**. Notable Indian basins include:
- **Jharia, Raniganj, Bokaro** (Damodar Valley, Jharkhand/West Bengal)
- **South Karanpura, North Karanpura, Sohagpur** (Gondwana basins)
- **Giridih, Rajmahal** (Bihar/Jharkhand)

Globally, the **San Juan Basin (USA)**, **Bowen Basin (Australia)**, and **Qinshui Basin (China)** are benchmark CBM plays.

---

## 1.4 Formation of Coal Bed Methane — The Coalification Process

The generation of CBM is intrinsically linked to the **rank maturation of coal**. Three primary mechanisms generate methane:

| Stage | Mechanism | Depth | R_o (%) | Dominant Process |
|-------|-----------|-------|---------|------------------|
| 1. **Bio-diagenesis** | Microbial (bacterial) action on peat | 0–1 km | <0.3 | Biogenic CO₂ → CH₄ |
| 2. **Catagenesis** | Thermal cracking of kerogen | 1–4 km | 0.3–2.0 | Thermogenic CH₄ generation |
| 3. **Metagenesis** | High-temperature cracking | 4–8 km | >2.0 | Dry gas (CH₄), anthracite |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Most CBM is generated when **vitrinite reflectance R_o** lies between **0.8% and 1.6%** (high-volatile to medium-volatile bituminous coal). At very high ranks, the coal loses sorption capacity due to **thermal maturation of micropores** — counterintuitively reducing gas content.

## 1.5 Physical Properties of Coal Relevant to CBM

1. **Dual Porosity System:**
   - **Matrix porosity (φ_m):** Microporous (0.5–1.0 nm); holds sorbed gas
   - **Cleat porosity (φ_c):** Macroporous; holds free gas and water
   - **Total porosity:** φ = φ_m + φ_c

2. **Permeability:** Typically **0.1–50 mD** (often <5 mD). Highly **stress-sensitive** — permeability decreases sharply with effective confining stress.

3. **Langmuir Volume (V_L) and Langmuir Pressure (P_L):** Empirical sorption parameters (discussed in Section 2).

4. **Ash and Moisture Content:** High ash (>40%) and moisture reduce sorption capacity.

5. **Gas Composition:** Predominantly **CH₄ (90–98%)**, with **CO₂, N₂, C₂H₆, and trace H₂S**.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Section 1 — Conceptual Foundation Established**
> You now understand: (1) CBM is *stored mostly by adsorption*, not compression; (2) formation requires coal maturation; and (3) production is *depressurization-driven*. Sections 2–5 build the quantitative, operational, and examination-level mastery.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm — The Heart of CBM Thermodynamics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Concept — Langmuir Isotherm**
> The amount of gas adsorbed per unit mass of coal is described by the **Langmuir isotherm** (1918), which assumes **monolayer adsorption** on energetically homogeneous surfaces. This is the single most important equation in CBM engineering.

$$V = \frac{V_L \cdot P}{P_L + P}$$

Where:
- $V$ = gas volume adsorbed at pressure $P$ (cm³/g or scf/ton)
- $V_L$ = **Langmuir Volume** = maximum adsorption capacity (cm³/g)
- $P_L$ = **Langmuir Pressure** = pressure at which $V = V_L / 2$ (kPa or psia)
- $P$ = reservoir pressure (kPa or psia)

### Asymptotic Behavior (Boundary Conditions for KTU Problems)

| Condition | Resulting V | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| $P \to 0$ | $V \to 0$ | No gas adsorbed at zero pressure |
| $P = P_L$ | $V = V_L/2$ | Half of capacity filled |
| $P \to \infty$ | $V \to V_L$ | Saturation — all micropores filled |
| $P = P_L/3$ | $V = V_L/4$ | Useful for isotherm construction |

## 2.2 Critical Desorption Pressure (P_cd) — The CBM "Bubble Point"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Meaning:** The **Critical Desorption Pressure** is the reservoir pressure at which the in-situ sorbed gas first begins to desorb as pressure declines. It is the CBM analogue of the **bubble-point pressure** in oil systems.

Mathematical definition (from material balance):

$$P_{cd} = \frac{P_L \cdot (G_c / V_L)}{1 - (G_c / V_L)}$$

Where $G_c$ is the **in-situ gas content** (cm³/g). The parameter $G_c / V_L$ is called the **sorbed gas saturation fraction** — the fraction of ultimate sorption capacity occupied in the reservoir.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of CBM Production Stages

1. **Stage 1 — Single-Phase Water Production:** Initially, cleats are 100% water-saturated. The well produces water only; reservoir pressure declines.
2. **Stage 2 — Two-Phase Production (Water + Gas):** Once $P_{reservoir} = P_{cd}$, gas begins to desorb. Two phases flow simultaneously.
3. **Stage 3 — Peak Gas Production:** The desorption front migrates into the coal; gas rate peaks. Water rate continues to decline.
4. **Stage 4 — Decline Phase:** As mobile water is depleted, gas rate declines. CO₂/N₂ (if any) come out last.
5. **Stage 5 — Abandonment:** When gas rate falls below economic limit (often 30–50 Mcf/day), the well is plugged.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why P_cd Matters for KTU Examinations:**
> A well where $P_{cd} \approx P_{reservoir}$ (initial reservoir pressure) will produce gas almost immediately. A well with $P_{cd}$ well below $P_{reservoir}$ requires extensive **dewatering** before gas production begins — an economic risk.

## 2.4 The CBM Material Balance Equation

For a volumetric CBM reservoir (no water influx), the gas material balance is:

$$G_p = G \cdot \left[ 1 - \frac{P}{P_i} \cdot \frac{Z_i}{Z} \right] + V_L \cdot \rho_c \cdot \left[ \frac{P_i}{P_L + P_i} - \frac{P}{P_L + P} \right]$$

Where:
- $G_p$ = cumulative gas produced (scf)
- $G$ = total original gas-in-place (scf)
- $P_i$ = initial reservoir pressure
- $Z, Z_i$ = gas compressibility factors
- $\rho_c$ = coal density (g/cm³)

## 2.5 Coal Seam Permeability and Stress Sensitivity

Coal permeability ($k$) is **fracture (cleat) permeability**, given by:

$$k = k_{o} \cdot e^{-3 C_f (P_i - P)}$$

Where:
- $k_o$ = permeability at initial reservoir pressure
- $C_f$ = cleat compressibility (typically $1 \times 10^{-3}$ to $1 \times 10^{-4}$ psi⁻¹)
- $(P_i - P)$ = pressure drawdown

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Coal *shrinks* when gas desorbs and *swells* when gas adsorbs. This is **matrix shrinkage/swelling** — a critical CBM phenomenon. Permeability can *increase* by 2–10× during late-life depletion as the matrix shrinks and cleats open. This is opposite to conventional gas behavior.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Key Variable Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|-------------------|------------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Langmuir Isotherm | $V = V_L P / (P_L + P)$ | $V_L, P_L$ | $V$: cm³/g; $P$: kPa |
| 2 | Critical Desorption Pressure | $P_{cd} = P_L (G_c/V_L) / [1 - (G_c/V_L)]$ | $G_c$: gas content | kPa or psia |
| 3 | Cleat Porosity | $\phi_c = b_f / (b_f + s_f)$ | $b_f$ = fracture aperture, $s_f$ = spacing | dimensionless |
| 4 | Permeability (Cleat) | $k = b_f^3 / (12 \cdot s_f)$ | From cubic law | m² or mD |
| 5 | Stress-Dependent Permeability | $k = k_o \cdot e^{-3 C_f \Delta P}$ | $C_f$: cleat compressibility | mD, psi⁻¹ |
| 6 | Sorbed Gas Density | $\rho_{sorbed} = V_{sc} \cdot \rho_{stp}$ | $\rho_{stp}$ = gas density at STP | g/cm³ |
| 7 | Gas Material Balance | $G_p = G [1 - (P Z_i)/(P_i Z)] + V_L \rho_c [P_i/(P_L+P_i) - P/(P_L+P)]$ | Full MBE | scf |
| 8 | Diffusion Coefficient (Matrix) | $D_m \sim 10^{-8}$ to $10^{-10}$ | Pseudo-steady state diffusion | cm²/s |
| 9 | Vitrinite Reflectance (Rank) | R_o (%) | 0.8–1.6% for peak CBM | dimensionless |
| 10 | Water Specific Gravity (CBM) | 1.00–1.05 (fresh) | Often high TDS | dimensionless |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Langmuir framework is used in:
- **Reserves estimation** (GIP calculations)
- **Production forecasting** (declining gas rate curves)
- **CO₂-ECBM** (Enhanced CBM via CO₂ injection) — uses competitive adsorption of CO₂ over CH₄ on coal surfaces, sequestering CO₂ while liberating CH₄.
- **Gas-in-place calculations** in field development planning.
- **Hydraulic fracturing design** — frac fluid volumes are tuned to cleat orientation and natural fracture patterns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Section 2 — Theoretical & Quantitative Foundation Complete**
> You now have the equations to handle: (a) gas content from isotherms, (b) desorption pressure, (c) permeability decline, and (d) material balances.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Desorption Pressure from Langmuir Isotherm

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Premise:** The **in-situ gas content** $G_c$ (volume of gas per unit mass of coal at initial reservoir pressure $P_i$) must equal the Langmuir isotherm value at $P_i$. As pressure drops to $P_{cd}$, the isotherm value equals $G_c$ (all gas remains sorbed, no desorption yet). At $P < P_{cd}$, gas desorbs.

**Step 1: Express the in-situ gas content using the Langmuir equation at $P_i$:**

$$G_c = \frac{V_L \cdot P_i}{P_L + P_i}$$

**Step 2: At $P = P_{cd}$, the sorbed volume still equals $G_c$ (boundary condition for desorption onset):**

$$G_c = \frac{V_L \cdot P_{cd}}{P_L + P_{cd}}$$

**Step 3: Equate the two expressions for $G_c$:**

$$\frac{V_L \cdot P_i}{P_L + P_i} = \frac{V_L \cdot P_{cd}}{P_L + P_{cd}}$$

**Step 4: Cancel $V_L$ from both sides (it is a constant for a given coal):**

$$\frac{P_i}{P_L + P_i} = \frac{P_{cd}}{P_L + P_{cd}}$$

**Step 5: Cross-multiply:**

$$P_i \cdot (P_L + P_{cd}) = P_{cd} \cdot (P_L + P_i)$$

**Step 6: Expand both sides:**

$$P_i \cdot P_L + P_i \cdot P_{cd} = P_{cd} \cdot P_L + P_{cd} \cdot P_i$$

**Step 7: Subtract $P_i \cdot P_{cd}$ from both sides (telescopes):**

$$P_i \cdot P_L = P_{cd} \cdot P_L$$

**Step 8: This gives the trivial $P_i = P_{cd}$ when $G_c$ equals the isotherm value at $P_i$ — a tautology.** The **non-trivial form** comes from defining the **sorbed gas saturation fraction** as $S_{gs} = G_c / V_L$, allowing us to write:

$$G_c = \frac{V_L \cdot P_{cd}}{P_L + P_{cd}} \implies \frac{G_c}{V_L} = \frac{P_{cd}}{P_L + P_{cd}} \implies S_{gs} = \frac{P_{cd}}{P_L + P_{cd}}$$

**Step 9: Solve for $P_{cd}$:**

$$P_{cd} = \frac{P_L \cdot S_{gs}}{1 - S_{gs}} = \frac{P_L \cdot (G_c / V_L)}{1 - (G_c / V_L)}$$

**Result (boxed KTU-formula):**

$$\boxed{P_{cd} = \frac{P_L \cdot (G_c / V_L)}{1 - (G_c / V_L)}}$$

**Numerical Verification Example (KTU Practice):**
A coal sample has $V_L = 25$ cm³/g, $P_L = 1500$ kPa, and measured gas content $G_c = 12$ cm³/g. Compute $P_{cd}$.

$$S_{gs} = \frac{12}{25} = 0.48$$

$$P_{cd} = \frac{1500 \times 0.48}{1 - 0.48} = \frac{720}{0.52} = 1384.6 \text{ kPa}$$

**Interpretation:** If reservoir pressure $P_i > 1384.6$ kPa, the coal is **undersaturated** — extensive dewatering will be required before gas production begins. If $P_i \approx 1384.6$ kPa, gas will desorb almost immediately.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gas-In-Place (GIP) for a CBM Reservoir

The volumetric gas-in-place for a coal seam of volume $V_{coal}$ and density $\rho_c$:

**Step 1: Total sorbed gas (dominant term):**

$$G_{sorbed} = V_{coal} \cdot \rho_c \cdot \frac{V_L \cdot P_i}{P_L + P_i}$$

**Step 2: Free gas in cleats (minor term):**

$$G_{free} = V_{coal} \cdot \phi_c \cdot S_{gi} \cdot \frac{1}{B_{gi}}$$

**Step 3: Total GIP:**

$$G_{total} = G_{sorbed} + G_{free}$$

For typical CBM, $G_{free} < 5\%$ of $G_{sorbed}$, so the simplified form is:

$$\boxed{G_{total} \approx V_{coal} \cdot \rho_c \cdot \frac{V_L \cdot P_i}{P_L + P_i}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Matrix-to-Cleat Diffusion (Fick's Law)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Concept:** After desorption, CH₄ molecules must **diffuse** through the coal matrix to reach the cleat network. This is described by **Fick's second law** in spherical coordinates for a coal matrix "chunk" of radius $r$:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D_m \left( \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r} \frac{\partial C}{\partial r} \right)$$

**Pseudo-Steady-State (PSS) Approximation Solution:**

$$\frac{dq}{dt} = \frac{2 \pi D_m \cdot (C_m - C_s)}{r}$$

Where:
- $C_m$ = average matrix concentration of CH₄
- $C_s$ = surface (cleat) concentration
- $D_m$ = molecular diffusion coefficient (cm²/s)
- $r$ = characteristic matrix block radius (cm)

**Quantitative Time-Scale:**

The characteristic diffusion time is:

$$t_d = \frac{r^2}{D_m} = \frac{(0.5 \text{ cm})^2}{10^{-9} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}} = 2.5 \times 10^8 \text{ s} \approx 7.9 \text{ years}$$

This explains the **multi-year** production profiles of CBM wells.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — CBM Property Ranges by Coal Rank

| Property | Lignite | Sub-Bituminous | High-Vol Bituminous | Low-Vol Bituminous | Anthracite |
|----------|---------|----------------|---------------------|---------------------|------------|
| $V_L$ (cm³/g) | 2–5 | 5–15 | 15–30 | 20–40 | 10–25 |
| $P_L$ (kPa) | 2000–3000 | 1500–2500 | 1000–2000 | 800–1500 | 500–1000 |
| $G_c$ (cm³/g) | 1–3 | 3–8 | 8–18 | 10–25 | 5–15 |
| Permeability (mD) | 50–500 | 10–100 | 1–30 | 0.1–10 | <1 |
| $R_o$ (%) | <0.4 | 0.4–0.8 | 0.8–1.4 | 1.4–2.0 | >2.0 |
| Best for CBM? | No | Marginal | **Yes (peak)** | **Yes (peak)** | Marginal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** **High-volatile and low-volatile bituminous coals (R_o = 0.8–2.0%)** are the **sweet spot** for CBM — high $V_L$ AND adequate permeability.

---

## 3.5 Derivative Check — Verifying Langmuir Behavior at Limit Points

**Check 1:** $P \to 0$

$$\lim_{P \to 0} \frac{V_L P}{P_L + P} = \frac{V_L \cdot 0}{P_L + 0} = 0 \checkmark$$

**Check 2:** $P \to \infty$

$$\lim_{P \to \infty} \frac{V_L P}{P_L + P} = \lim_{P \to \infty} \frac{V_L P}{P(1 + P_L/P)} = \lim_{P \to \infty} \frac{V_L}{1 + 0} = V_L \checkmark$$

**Check 3:** $P = P_L$

$$V = \frac{V_L \cdot P_L}{P_L + P_L} = \frac{V_L \cdot P_L}{2 P_L} = \frac{V_L}{2} \checkmark$$

All asymptotic behaviors are physically consistent.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Section 3 — Derivations Complete**
> You can now derive $P_{cd}$, GIP, and diffusion time-scales from first principles.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    F --> G
    G --> H

    F --> G
    G --> H

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The right-side flow shows **biogenic methane generation** (early stage) and **thermogenic methane generation** (mid-to-late stages). CBM reservoirs are formed when gas is adsorbed in coal seams during burial and rank progression.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CBM Frac is Special (KTU Note):**
> Unlike conventional sandstone frac, **coal seams have low Young's modulus and high Poisson's ratio**, leading to **complex T-shaped or dendritic fractures** that follow natural cleats. Proppant must be **lightweight (e.g., 100-mesh sand)** and pumped at **moderate rates** to avoid screen-out due to fracture closure.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Coal Bed Methane. List its three modes of storage in a coal seam.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | 3 Marks | CO1 | Remember Level]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Coal Bed Methane (CBM)** is an unconventional natural gas stored predominantly by **adsorption** onto the microporous internal surfaces of coal seams, generated during the **coalification** of organic matter.

**Three Modes of Storage:**
1. **Adsorbed Gas** (sorbed onto micropore surfaces) — *dominant, >95% of total*
2. **Free Gas** (in cleat/macrofracture pore space) — *minor, ~5%*
3. **Dissolved Gas** (in formation water within cleats) — *trace amounts*

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** A common error is listing *only two* modes or stating the percentages incorrectly. Memorize **>95% adsorbed, ~5% free**.

---

### Question 2: What is the Langmuir Pressure (P_L)? State its physical significance.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | 3 Marks | CO2 | Understand Level]**

**Model Answer:**

**Langmuir Pressure ($P_L$)** is the pressure at which the **adsorbed gas volume equals one-half of the Langmuir Volume** ($V = V_L / 2$) on the Langmuir adsorption isotherm.

**Physical Significance:**
- $P_L$ is **inversely related** to the **affinity of coal for methane**. A **low $P_L$** indicates the coal has a **strong affinity** for CH₄ (gas adsorbs readily at low pressure).
- A **high $P_L$** indicates **weak affinity** (gas requires high pressure to be retained).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always quote both the *mathematical* (V = V_L/2) and *physical* (affinity for CH₄) definitions to score full marks.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU 2024 Scheme — End Semester Examination (ESE) Pattern]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive CBM Reservoir Analysis

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 | 14 Marks | CO2, CO3 | Apply / Analyze Level]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Derive the Critical Desorption Pressure (P_cd) formula from the Langmuir isotherm. A coal sample has $V_L = 20$ cm³/g, $P_L = 2000$ kPa, and gas content $G_c = 10$ cm³/g. Compute the critical desorption pressure. Comment on whether the coal is saturated or undersaturated if the initial reservoir pressure is 3000 kPa.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** At initial reservoir pressure $P_i$, the in-situ gas content is:

$$G_c = \frac{V_L \cdot P_i}{P_L + P_i}$$

**Step 2:** At critical desorption pressure $P_{cd}$, the isotherm value equals $G_c$:

$$G_c = \frac{V_L \cdot P_{cd}}{P_L + P_{cd}}$$

**Step 3:** Eliminate $G_c$ and solve for $P_{cd}$:

$$P_{cd} = \frac{P_L \cdot (G_c / V_L)}{1 - (G_c / V_L)}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute numerical values:

$$S_{gs} = \frac{G_c}{V_L} = \frac{10}{20} = 0.5$$

$$P_{cd} = \frac{2000 \times 0.5}{1 - 0.5} = \frac{1000}{0.5} = 2000 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 5:** Comparison with $P_i = 3000$ kPa:

Since $P_i = 3000$ kPa > $P_{cd} = 2000$ kPa, the coal is **undersaturated** — gas will not desorb immediately. The well must undergo significant **dewatering** until reservoir pressure drops to 2000 kPa.

**Step 6:** Pressure drop required for desorption onset:

$$\Delta P = P_i - P_{cd} = 3000 - 2000 = 1000 \text{ kPa}$$

**Conclusion:** The well is **undersaturated**; substantial dewatering is required.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Describe the three stages of methane generation during coalification. List five key differences between CBM and conventional natural gas reservoirs.

**Model Answer:**

**Three Stages of Methane Generation:**

| Stage | Mechanism | R_o Range | Coal Type |
|-------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| **1. Bio-diagenesis** | Bacterial action on peat; CO₂ reduction to CH₄ | <0.3% | Peat / Lignite |
| **2. Catagenesis** | Thermal cracking of kerogen | 0.3–2.0% | Sub-bituminous to Bituminous |
| **3. Metagenesis** | High-temperature cracking of residual kerogen | >2.0% | Anthracite |

**Five Differences between CBM and Conventional Gas:**

| # | Property | CBM | Conventional Gas |
|---|----------|-----|------------------|
| 1 | Storage mechanism | Adsorbed (95%) | Compression in pores |
| 2 | Reservoir rock | Coal (organic) | Sandstone/Carbonate (inorganic) |
| 3 | Drive mechanism | Pressure depletion via dewatering | Pressure depletion / aquifer drive |
| 4 | Production profile | Long plateau, multi-year | Initial high, fast decline |
| 5 | Well spacing | Tight (80–160 acres) | Larger (640 acres typical) |
| 6 | Water production | High (dewatering needed) | Low (except aquifer drive) |
| 7 | Permeability | Stress-sensitive, cleat-controlled | Porosity-controlled |

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Hydrofracturing and Surface Facilities for CBM

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Alternative | 14 Marks | CO3, CO4 | Apply / Analyze Level]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the design considerations for hydrofracturing a coal seam. Compare it with conventional sandstone fracturing.

**Model Answer:**

**Coal Seam Hydrofracturing — Key Design Considerations:**

1. **Low Young's Modulus (E ~ 0.2–1.0 × 10⁶ psi):** Coal is **softer** than sandstone (E ~ 1–5 × 10⁶ psi), so fractures **close more readily** post-frac.
2. **High Poisson's Ratio (ν ~ 0.3–0.4):** Generates **complex T-shaped / dendritic fracture networks** that follow natural cleats.
3. **Proppant Selection:** Use **lightweight, fine-mesh sand (100-mesh or 20/40)** to enter narrow cleat apertures; avoid high concentrations that screen out.
4. **Fluid System:** **Slickwater** (low-viscosity, friction-reduced) is preferred over cross-linked gels to minimize formation damage.
5. **Pump Rate and Pressure:** Moderate rates (10–30 bbl/min) at moderate pressures (1500–2500 psi surface) to follow cleat orientation.
6. **Fluid Volume:** 50,000–200,000 gallons per stage; multiple stages in long horizontal wells.
7. **Coal Sensitivity:** Coal is **fracture-face sensitive** — oxidative fluids (e.g., high-pH gels) can plug micropores.

**Comparison with Conventional Sandstone Frac:**

| Parameter | Coal Seam Frac | Sandstone Frac |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| Young's Modulus | Low (soft) | High (stiff) |
| Fracture Geometry | Complex T-shape, cleat-following | Bi-wing planar |
| Proppant | Lightweight 100-mesh sand | Higher strength 20/40 or resin-coated |
| Fluid | Slickwater | Cross-linked gel or hybrid |
| Pump Rate | Lower (10–30 bbl/min) | Higher (40–80 bbl/min) |
| Closure Risk | High | Moderate |
| Damage Susceptibility | High (pore plugging) | Lower |

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Describe the major surface facilities required for a CBM production field. With a neat block diagram, show the flow from wellhead to sales gas pipeline.

**Model Answer:**

**Major Surface Facilities for CBM:**

1. **Wellhead / Christmas Tree:**
   - Houses the master valve, wing valve, and production choke
   - Routes gas + water mixture to the gathering line

2. **Water-Gas Separator (Two-Phase):**
   - Knocks out free water from the gas stream
   - Operates at low pressure (50–150 psig)

3. **Water Treatment and Disposal:**
   - CBM water is often **brackish, high-TDS** (Total Dissolved Solids)
   - Treatment options: settling ponds, RO, ion exchange
   - Disposal: injection wells or surface discharge (per regulation)

4. **Compressor Station (Low-Pressure Boost):**
   - CBM is produced at **low wellhead pressure (10–100 psig)**
   - Compressors boost it to 500–1000 psig for pipeline entry

5. **Dehydration Unit:**
   - Removes water vapor to prevent hydrate formation in pipelines
   - Uses **glycol contactor** (TEG) or **molecular sieve desiccant**

6. **Gas Metering and Custody Transfer:**
   - Turbine or orifice meters for volume measurement
   - BTU analyzers for gas quality

7. **Sales Gas Pipeline Tie-In:**
   - Connects to the regional gas grid

**Block Flow Diagram (Verbal Representation):**

```
Multiple CBM Wells → Wellhead → Water/Gas Separator → Compressor →
Dehydration Unit → Gas Metering → Sales Gas Pipeline

(Separator) → Water Tank → Water Treatment → Re-injection / Discharge
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Surface Facilities Differ for CBM:**
> Conventional gas wells typically need **separation, dehydration, and compression** only when pressure is low. CBM wells are *always* low-pressure at the wellhead because of the depressurization process, so **compression is mandatory** rather than optional.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Module 3 — CBM):**

1. **Conflating adsorbed gas with free gas:** The CBM storage is **>95% adsorbed**, not free. Examiners deduct heavily if students state "CBM is stored in pore space."

2. **Confusing P_cd with P_i:** Critical desorption pressure is **not** equal to initial reservoir pressure. The relationship $P_{cd} = P_i$ only holds for *saturated* coals (rare).

3. **Forgetting units in Langmuir isotherm:** $V_L$ is in **cm³/g or scf/ton**, NOT m³/kg. State units explicitly.

4. **Mistaking coal rank for coal grade:** **Rank** is metamorphic grade (R_o); **grade** refers to ash/sulfur content. They are independent.

5. **Skipping dewatering in production sequence:** CBM wells **must** be dewatered first. A production profile starting directly with gas flow is incorrect.

6. **Treating coal as homogeneous:** Coal is a **dual-porosity medium** (matrix + cleats). All flow equations must reflect this.

7. **Forgetting matrix shrinkage effect:** In the **late life** of a CBM well, permeability *increases* due to matrix shrinkage — opposite of conventional gas. Examiners love this trick question.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: CBM**

- [x] **CBM is stored >95% by adsorption** on micropore surfaces (Langmuir isotherm).
- [x] **Three storage modes:** adsorbed, free (cleat), dissolved (water).
- [x] **Formation:** Coalification — biogenic (R_o < 0.3%) + thermogenic (R_o = 0.3–2.0%).
- [x] **Sweet spot rank:** High-volatile to low-volatile bituminous coal (R_o = 0.8–2.0%).
- [x] **Langmuir isotherm:** $V = V_L P / (P_L + P)$ — the master equation.
- [x] **Critical desorption pressure:** $P_{cd} = P_L (G_c/V_L) / [1 - (G_c/V_L)]$.
- [x] **Material balance** must include **sorbed gas** (dominant) and free gas (minor).
- [x] **Coal is dual-porosity:** matrix (micropores) + cleats (macrofractures).
- [x] **Permeability is stress-sensitive** AND **matrix-shrinkage enhanced** late-life.
- [x] **Exploration workflow:** Seismic → drilling → coring → desorption tests (USBM) → isotherm → GIP.
- [x] **Gas content test (USBM method):** Lost gas + Desorbed gas + Residual gas = Total gas content.
- [x] **Hydrofracturing:** Slickwater + 100-mesh sand + low pump rates → complex T-shaped fractures along cleats.
- [x] **Surface facilities:** Separator → Compressor (mandatory) → Dehydration (TEG) → Metering → Sales line.
- [x] **Water disposal is critical** — CBM water is brackish, high-TDS; often re-injected.
- [x] **CO₂-ECBM:** Injecting CO₂ preferentially adsorbs on coal, liberating CH₄ and sequestering CO₂ simultaneously.
- [x] **Production profile:** Dewatering (1–3 years) → Peak gas → Long decline (10–20 years).
- [x] **Vitrinite Reflectance R_o** is the **key rank indicator** for CBM prospectivity.
- [x] **Indian CBM basins:** Jharia, Raniganj, Bokaro, Sohagpur, South Karanpura.
- [x] **Global benchmarks:** San Juan (USA), Bowen (Australia), Qinshui (China).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 Mastery Achieved.** You are now prepared for: (1) descriptive questions on CBM formation and properties, (2) numerical problems on Langmuir isotherm and P_cd, (3) comparative analysis of CBM vs conventional gas, and (4) design/operational questions on hydrofracturing and surface facilities.
