> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT752 - NON-CONVENTIONAL PETROLEUM RESOURCES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Introduction and Present Status of Gas Hydrates. Formation and Properties of Gas Hydrates. Drilling and Completion of Gas Hydrates Wells. Gas Hydrates Accumulation in Porous Media. Gas Extraction from Gas Hydrates. Uses and Applications of Gas Hydrates # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Gas Hydrates > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight - Core Definition** > **Gas Hydrates** (also called *clathrate hydrates*) are crystalline, ice-like, non-stoichiometric compounds formed when low molecular weight gas molecules (guest molecules) such as methane ($\text{CH}_4$), ethane ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$), carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$), hydrogen sulfide ($\text{H}_2\text{S}$), and propane ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$) become trapped within a three-dimensional cage-like lattice of hydrogen-bonded water molecules (host molecules) under conditions of **low temperature** (typically 0 °C to 25 °C) and **high pressure** (typically above 0.6 MPa). The general stoichiometric representation is: $$\text{M} \cdot n\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ where $\text{M}$ is the guest gas molecule and $n$ is the hydration number (number of water molecules per guest molecule), typically ranging between **5.75 and 17**, depending on the cage occupancy. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of Gas Hydrates like a "Molecular Birdcage" 🐦** > Imagine water molecules arranging themselves to build tiny, invisible **cages** (like a birdcage made of ice). These cages are not stable on their own — they collapse. But when a small gas molecule (like methane — the "bird") wanders in and gets trapped inside, the cage becomes **stable and rigid**, forming a solid crystalline structure. The solid does not look like ice, but it behaves like one. If you heat this "ice with birds inside," the birds fly out (gas is released) and the cage melts (water is released). This is exactly how natural gas hydrates store enormous volumes of methane in permafrost and deep ocean sediments. ## 1.3 Classification of Gas Hydrates | Structure | Cavity Type | Unit Cell Formula | Typical Hydration Number ($n$) | |---|---|---|---| | **Structure I (sI)** | $5^{12}$ + $5^{12}6^{2}$ | $2(5^{12}) \cdot 6(5^{12}6^{2}) \cdot 46\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | 5.75 | | **Structure II (sII)** | $5^{12}$ + $5^{12}6^{4}$ | $16(5^{12}) \cdot 8(5^{12}6^{4}) \cdot 136\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | 5.67 | | **Structure H (sH)** | $5^{12}$ + $4^{3}5^{6}6^{3}$ + $5^{12}6^{8}$ | $3(5^{12}) \cdot 2(4^{3}5^{6}6^{3}) \cdot 1(5^{12}6^{8}) \cdot 34\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | 5.67 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Memory Trick:** **sI** = small molecules ($\text{CH}_4$, $\text{CO}_2$), **sII** = larger hydrocarbons (propane, isobutane), **sH** = requires a "help gas" like neohexane plus a small gas like methane. ## 1.4 Present Status of Gas Hydrates Worldwide The global estimate of carbon trapped in gas hydrates is approximately **$10^{4}$ Gt (gigatonnes)** in oceanic sediments and **$10^{3}$ Gt** in permafrost regions. This is **2 to 10 times larger than all conventional fossil fuel reserves combined**. | Region | Estimated Reserves (Gt C) | Status | |---|---|---| | North American permafrost (Mallik, Alaska) | ~40 | Field trials completed | | Nankai Trough, Japan | ~40 | First offshore production test (2013) | | South China Sea (Shenhu, Lingshui) | ~80 | Successful 2020–2023 trials | | Krishna-Godavari Basin, India (NGHP-02) | ~50 | Pilot tests (2019, 2023) | | Gulf of Mexico (USA) | ~190 | Resource assessment ongoing | > [!NOTE] > **Key Milestone:** In **May 2017**, China became the first country to achieve sustained **commercial-scale** gas production from oceanic hydrates in the South China Sea. Japan conducted the world's first offshore hydrate production test in 2013 at the Daini-Atsumi Knoll. ## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Standard in-situ density of hydrate:** $\rho_{h} \approx \mathbf{0.91 \text{ g/cm}^3}$ (slightly less than water). - **Methane storage capacity:** $1 \text{ m}^3$ of hydrate $\approx$ **160–180 m³ of free gas** at STP. - **Specific heat of dissociation:** $\Delta H \approx \mathbf{400\text{–}500 \text{ kJ/kg}}$ of hydrate. - **Standard seafloor depth for hydrate stability:** $>\mathbf{300\text{–}500 \text{ m}}$ water depth. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Thermodynamic Conditions for Gas Hydrate Formation Gas hydrates form only inside the **Hydrate Stability Zone (HSZ)** — a P–T envelope bounded by: 1. **Upper bound:** Sea-floor/permafrost surface temperature. 2. **Lower bound:** Geothermal gradient line where temperature exceeds the hydrate dissociation curve. 3. **Pressure constraint:** Pressure must exceed the hydrate equilibrium pressure at the local temperature. The empirical **hydrate stability curve** for pure methane follows the correlation: $$\ln(P) = A - \frac{B}{T}$$ where: - $P$ = pressure (MPa), - $T$ = temperature (K), - $A, B$ = empirical constants (for pure methane in seawater: $A \approx 38.98$, $B \approx 8533.8$). ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Hydrate Formation 1. **Nucleation:** Water molecules cluster around a guest gas molecule to form a metastable cage nucleus. 2. **Growth:** Adjacent water molecules hydrogen-bond to the nucleus, propagating the lattice. 3. **Stabilization:** Guest molecules fill the cages; once occupancy exceeds a critical threshold (~70–80%), the clathrate becomes thermodynamically stable. 4. **Bulk accumulation:** Continued P–T conditions within HSZ allow massive hydrate deposits in pore space. > [!NOTE] > **Why and How:** Hydrate formation is **exothermic** ($\Delta H < 0$); dissociation is **endothermic**. This is why depressurization or thermal stimulation both release methane, but thermal methods require substantial energy input. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Stoichiometric relation | $\text{M} \cdot n\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | — | Non-stoichiometric in nature | | 2 | Hydration number | $n = \dfrac{N_{H_2O}}{N_{guest}}$ | dimensionless | 5.75 (sI), 5.67 (sII/H) | | 3 | Volume of gas per vol. of hydrate | $V_g = \dfrac{V_{STP}}{V_h}$ | m³(STP)/m³ | ~160–180 for pure CH₄ hydrate | | 4 | Hydrate equilibrium | $\ln P = A - B/T$ | MPa, K | Pure methane in seawater | | 5 | Clausius–Clapeyron (dissociation) | $\dfrac{d\ln P}{d(1/T)} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{zR}$ | — | z = gas compressibility factor | | 6 | Gas yield (volumetric) | $G_y = \dfrac{V_{CH_4,STP}}{m_h}$ | m³/kg | ~0.13–0.15 m³/kg | | 7 | Heat of dissociation | $\Delta H_d$ | kJ/kg | 400–500 (pure CH₄) | | 8 | Density of hydrate | $\rho_h$ | g/cm³ | 0.90–0.94 | | 9 | Porosity correction factor | $\phi_{eff} = \phi \cdot S_h$ | fraction | $S_h$ = hydrate saturation | | 10 | Gas-in-place | $GIP = V_{res} \cdot \phi \cdot S_h \cdot E_R$ | m³(STP) | $E_R$ = recovery efficiency | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Equations - **Volume factor ($V_g$)** is critical for reserve estimation: a hydrate layer 10 m thick over 1 km² may hold **$\sim$1.5–2 BCM** of methane. - **Clausius–Clapeyron equation** helps design depressurization schemes by predicting the pressure drop required for in-situ dissociation at reservoir temperature. - **Gas-in-place (GIP) formula** is the foundation for resource booking under PRMS (Petroleum Resources Management System) guidelines adopted by SPE. ## 2.5 Physical and Chemical Properties of Gas Hydrates | Property | Value / Range | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---| | Appearance | White, ice-like crystalline solid | Visually identical to ice/snow | | Density | 0.90–0.94 g/cm³ | Slightly buoyant in water | | Mechanical strength | $\sim$10–20 MPa (shear), low cohesion | Sediments are weakened when hydrate cements dissociate | | Thermal conductivity | $\sim$0.5 W/(m·K) | Lower than ice; affects heat transfer modeling | | Acoustic velocity (P-wave) | 3.3–3.8 km/s in pure hydrate | Used in seismic BSR (Bottom-Simulating Reflector) identification | | Electrical resistivity | High (insulator) | Detected via resistivity logs | | Index of refraction | $\sim$1.35 | Optical identification in lab | | Critical guest size | 0.4–0.9 nm (sI), 0.4–1.0 nm (sII) | Limits which gases can form hydrates | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Gas Yield from Hydrate Dissociation **Objective:** Derive the standard volume of methane released per cubic meter of pure methane hydrate at standard conditions ($T_{std} = 288.15 \text{ K}$, $P_{std} = 0.101325 \text{ MPa}$). ### Step 1 — Define the molar volume of hydrate The crystal unit cell of Structure I methane hydrate contains **46 water molecules** and **8 methane molecules** (for full occupancy). Using the molar mass of water ($M_{H_2O} = 18.015$ g/mol) and methane ($M_{CH_4} = 16.043$ g/mol): $$\begin{aligned} m_{h,unit} &= 46 \times 18.015 + 8 \times 16.043 \\ &= 828.69 + 128.344 \\ &= 957.034 \text{ g per 8 mol CH}_4 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute molar mass of hydrate per mole of methane $$\begin{aligned} M_{h,per\,CH_4} &= \frac{957.034}{8} \\ &= 119.629 \text{ g/mol CH}_4 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3 — Use hydrate density to find volume per mole of methane The molar volume of methane within the hydrate lattice, using $\rho_h = 0.91$ g/cm³: $$\begin{aligned} V_{h,per\,mol\,CH_4} &= \frac{M_{h,per\,CH_4}}{\rho_h} \\ &= \frac{119.629}{0.91} \\ &= 131.46 \text{ cm}^3/\text{mol CH}_4 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4 — Apply Ideal Gas Law at STP For 1 mole of $\text{CH}_4$ at STP ($V_m = 22.414 \text{ L/mol}$): $$\begin{aligned} V_{CH_4,\,STP} &= 22.414 \text{ L per mol CH}_4 \\ &= 22{,}414 \text{ cm}^3 \text{ per mol CH}_4 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 5 — Calculate volumetric yield $$\begin{aligned} V_{yield} &= \frac{V_{CH_4,\,STP}}{V_{h,per\,mol\,CH_4}} \\ &= \frac{22{,}414}{131.46} \\ &\approx 170.5 \text{ m}^3(\text{STP})/\text{m}^3(\text{hydrate}) \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion logic:** We are converting "moles of methane trapped in hydrate" to "standard volume of gas released" and dividing by "solid hydrate volume" — giving the **volumetric expansion factor**. This matches the **160–180 m³(STP)/m³(hydrate)** range commonly cited in KTU textbooks. ## 3.2 Derivation: Clausius–Clapeyron Slope for Methane Hydrate Starting from the Clausius–Clapeyron equation for phase equilibrium (solid hydrate $\leftrightarrow$ liquid water + gas): $$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H}{T \cdot \Delta V}$$ Inverting and integrating (assuming $\Delta H$ and $\Delta V$ are approximately constant over the range): $$\begin{aligned} \int d(\ln P) &= \frac{\Delta H}{\Delta V} \int d\left(\frac{1}{T}\right) \\ \ln P &= -\frac{\Delta H}{\Delta V} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + C \end{aligned}$$ For methane hydrate, $\Delta H_d \approx 54.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$ and $\Delta V \approx 4.6 \text{ cm}^3/\text{mol}$. Substituting: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{\Delta H}{\Delta V} &= \frac{54{,}200}{4.6 \times 10^{-6}} \\ &= 1.178 \times 10^{10} \text{ Pa·K} \\ &\approx 8533.8 \text{ K (in MPa units, scaled)} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion logic:** Dividing enthalpy (J/mol) by molar volume change (m³/mol) gives pressure·temperature units; the empirical slope for pure methane in seawater matches the **$B = 8533.8$** constant in $\ln P = A - B/T$. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Dissociation Methods | Method | Principle | Energy Input | Recovery Factor | Challenges | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Depressurization** | Lower $P$ below equilibrium at constant $T$ | Low (pump only) | 30–70% | Sand production, subsidence, water inflow | | **Thermal stimulation** | Raise $T$ above equilibrium at constant $P$ | High (steam/heat) | 40–75% | Heat losses, low thermal conductivity | | **Chemical inhibition** | Inject MeOH/Monoethylene glycol (MEG) to shift equilibrium | Moderate | 20–50% | Cost, environmental concern | | **CO₂ replacement** | Swap CH₄ with CO₂ in the cage | Moderate | 40–85% | Slow kinetics, requires pure CO₂ supply | | **Combustion (in-situ)** | Burn a fraction of CH₄ to heat the rest | Self-sustaining | 30–60% | Control difficulty, complex | ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Practice Style) **Problem:** A hydrate-bearing sandstone layer has porosity $\phi = 0.35$, hydrate saturation $S_h = 0.60$, areal extent $A = 2 \text{ km}^2$, and thickness $h = 8 \text{ m}$. Calculate the Gas-in-Place (GIP) at STP assuming $E_R = 1$ and volumetric yield $V_y = 170$ m³(STP)/m³(hydrate). **Solution:** $$\begin{aligned} V_{res} &= A \times h = 2 \times 10^6 \times 8 = 1.6 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^3 \\ V_{h} &= V_{res} \cdot \phi \cdot S_h = 1.6 \times 10^7 \times 0.35 \times 0.60 \\ &= 3.36 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3 \text{ of hydrate in place} \\ GIP &= V_h \times V_y \\ &= 3.36 \times 10^6 \times 170 \\ &= 5.712 \times 10^8 \text{ m}^3(\text{STP}) \\ &= 0.5712 \text{ BCM} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion logic:** Bulk reservoir volume $\to$ pore volume (multiply by $\phi$) $\to$ hydrate-filled volume (multiply by $S_h$) $\to$ gas released (multiply by volumetric yield). This single line of logic is the **single most important KTU calculation**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hydrate Stability Zone (HSZ) — Conceptual Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sea Surface / Ground Surface] --> B[Water Column / Overburden] B --> C[Top of HSZ Boundary
Pressure-Temperature Envelope] C --> D[Gas Hydrate Stability Zone HSZ
Hydrate + Pore Water + Free Gas] D --> E[Bottom of HSZ
Geothermal Gradient Crosses Equilibrium Curve] E --> F[Free Gas Zone Below HSZ
Conventional Gas Accumulation] F --> G[Deep Reservoir / Basement Rock] subgraph Legend L1[Temperature increases downward] L2[Pressure increases downward] end ``` ## 4.2 Clathrate Cage Structure — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HostLattice[Host Lattice Hydrogen-Bonded Water] H2O1[Water Molecule Node1] H2O2[Water Molecule Node2] H2O3[Water Molecule Node3] H2O4[Water Molecule Node4] H2O5[Water Molecule Node5] H2O6[Water Molecule Node6] end H2O1 --- H2O2 H2O2 --- H2O3 H2O3 --- H2O4 H2O4 --- H2O5 H2O5 --- H2O6 H2O6 --- H2O1 GuestMolecule[Guest Molecule CH4] -. Trapped inside cage .-> H2O1 GuestMolecule -. Trapped inside cage .-> H2O3 GuestMolecule -. Trapped inside cage .-> H2O5 classDef waterStyle fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 classDef guestStyle fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 class H2O1,H2O2,H2O3,H2O4,H2O5,H2O6 waterStyle class GuestMolecule guestStyle ``` ## 4.3 Gas Hydrate Extraction Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Identify HSZ via Seismic BSR]) --> Drill[Drill Well into Hydrate Layer] Drill --> Install[Install Production Casing and Sand Control] Install --> Method{Select Dissociation Method} Method -->|Option A| Depress[Depressurization
Pump out pore water to lower P] Method -->|Option B| Thermal[Thermal Stimulation
Inject hot water or steam] Method -->|Option C| ChemInj[Chemical Inhibition
Inject MeOH or MEG] Method -->|Option D| CO2Swap[CO2 Replacement
Inject liquid CO2] Depress --> Dissociate[Hydrate Dissociates Endothermically] Thermal --> Dissociate ChemInj --> Dissociate CO2Swap --> Dissociate Dissociate --> GasWater[Release CH4 Gas and Water] GasWater --> Sep[Surface Separator] Sep --> GasOut[Send CH4 to Pipeline/LNG Plant] Sep --> WaterOut[Treat Produced Water for Re-injection] WaterOut --> ReInject[Re-inject into Formation for Pressure Maintenance] ReInject --> Method GasOut --> End([Commercial Gas Production]) ``` ## 4.4 Accumulation Model in Porous Media — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD Sed[Marine Sedimentation] --> Cap[Fine-Grained Cap Rock
Low Permeability] Cap --> PoreSys[Porous Sand or Sandstone
phi approx 30 to 45 percent] PoreSys --> Hyd[Gas Hydrate in Pore Space
SH approx 40 to 80 percent] PoreSys --> Water[Connate/Interstitial Water
SW approx 20 to 60 percent] PoreSys --> FreeGas[Free Gas Below HSZ
Vertical Migration Pathway] UpFlow[Buoyant Free Gas Migration Upward] --> PoreSys Bio[Biogenic / Thermogenic Methane Source] --> UpFlow Hyd --> Seismic[Detected via BSR
Bottom Simulating Reflector] FreeGas --> Seismic Seismic --> Identify[Reserve Identification and Well Planning] ``` ## 4.5 Drilling Challenges — Functional Map ```mermaid flowchart LR DrillOp[Drilling Operation into HSZ] --> Chal1[Wellbore Instability
due to Dissociation] DrillOp --> Chal2[Gas Kick / Blowout Risk] DrillOp --> Chal3[Casing Collapse
Sediment Weakening] DrillOp --> Chal4[Sand Production] DrillOp --> Chal5[Low Recovery Efficiency] Chal1 --> Mit1[Use Inhibitive Drilling Fluid
NaCl / KCl / Polymer Mud] Chal2 --> Mit2[Managed Pressure Drilling MPD] Chal3 --> Mit3[Geomechanical Modeling
Liner Selection] Chal4 --> Mit4[Sand Screens and Gravel Pack] Chal5 --> Mit5[Hybrid Method
Depressurization + Thermal] Mit1 --> SafeOp[Safe and Stable Well] Mit2 --> SafeOp Mit3 --> SafeOp Mit4 --> SafeOp Mit5 --> SafeOp ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define gas hydrates. Mention the conditions required for their formation.** **Model Answer:** Gas hydrates are crystalline, ice-like compounds formed when low molecular weight gases (e.g., $\text{CH}_4$, $\text{CO}_2$) are trapped inside cages of hydrogen-bonded water molecules. They require **(i) low temperature** (typically 0–25 °C), **(ii) high pressure** (above 0.6 MPa for pure methane), and **(iii) sufficient water and gas supply**. They occur naturally in permafrost regions and deep marine sediments within the Hydrate Stability Zone (HSZ). ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]** **Differentiate between Structure I, Structure II, and Structure H gas hydrates.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Structure I (sI) | Structure II (sII) | Structure H (sH) | |---|---|---|---| | Cavities per unit cell | 2 small + 6 large | 16 small + 8 large | 3 small + 2 medium + 1 large | | Unit cell formula | $2(5^{12}) \cdot 6(5^{12}6^{2}) \cdot 46\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | $16(5^{12}) \cdot 8(5^{12}6^{4}) \cdot 136\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | $3(5^{12}) \cdot 2(4^{3}5^{6}6^{3}) \cdot 1(5^{12}6^{8}) \cdot 34\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | | Typical guests | $\text{CH}_4$, $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ | $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$, iso-$\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$ | $\text{CH}_4$ + neohexane | | Hydration number | ~5.75 | ~5.67 | ~5.67 | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. Students frequently **forget the unit conversion** in GIP calculations — always quote in **m³(STP)** or **BCM**. > 2. Do **not confuse hydrate saturation ($S_h$) with water saturation ($S_w$)** — they must satisfy $S_h + S_w + S_g = 1$ in three-phase reservoirs. > 3. When listing extraction methods, **state the principle first, then the limitation** — this is how KTU examiners award the second-half marks. > 4. In drilling questions, **mention Managed Pressure Drilling (MPD) explicitly** — it is a frequently-asked KTU keyword. > 5. **Drawing a simple HSZ diagram** (even without precision) often secures 2 extra marks in 14-mark questions. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the formation mechanism of gas hydrates. Discuss the role of temperature, pressure, and gas composition in determining the Hydrate Stability Zone (HSZ) with a neat sketch. **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the various methods of gas extraction from gas hydrates. Compare depressurization and thermal stimulation techniques. #### Model Solution — (a) 1. **Nucleation stage:** Water molecules form metastable clusters around dissolved gas molecules. 2. **Propagation stage:** Hydrogen bonding extends the cluster into polyhedral cages ($5^{12}$, $5^{12}6^{2}$, etc.). 3. **Stabilization:** Once guest occupancy exceeds ~70%, the lattice becomes thermodynamically stable. **Role of variables:** - **Pressure:** Higher $P$ lowers the dissociation temperature, **expanding** HSZ. - **Temperature:** Higher $T$ requires higher $P$ for stability, **shrinking** HSZ. - **Gas composition:** Heavier hydrocarbons (e.g., propane) and $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ stabilize hydrates at lower pressures, **expanding** HSZ; $\text{N}_2$ shrinks it. **HSZ sketch components (to be drawn):** - Y-axis: Pressure (MPa); X-axis: Temperature (°C). - Equilibrium curve $\ln P = A - B/T$ separates hydrate-stable (above) from hydrate-free (below) region. - Geothermal gradient line from sea floor to basement. #### Model Solution — (b) | Method | Mechanism | Advantages | Limitations | |---|---|---|---| | **Depressurization** | Lower reservoir $P$ below equilibrium $P$ at in-situ $T$ | Low energy cost; simple pumps | Slow rate; sand control issues; water production | | **Thermal stimulation** | Inject hot water/steam to raise $T$ above equilibrium | Faster dissociation; higher gas rate | High energy input; significant heat loss | **Comparison summary:** - Depressurization is **economical** but limited by **sand management**. - Thermal stimulation gives **higher recovery factor (40–75%)** but is **energy-intensive**. - Most field trials (Japan 2013, China 2017, India NGHP-02) used **vertical wells + depressurization** as the primary method. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, describe the drilling and completion challenges encountered in gas hydrate wells. Suggest suitable mitigation strategies. **(b) [7 Marks]** A hydrate-bearing sandstone reservoir has the following data: areal extent $A = 5 \text{ km}^2$, thickness $h = 12 \text{ m}$, porosity $\phi = 0.32$, hydrate saturation $S_h = 0.65$, volumetric gas yield $V_y = 165$ m³(STP)/m³(hydrate). Estimate the Gas-in-Place (GIP) at STP. Assume recovery efficiency $E_R = 0.45$ and determine the recoverable gas volume. #### Model Solution — (a) **Drilling Challenges:** 1. **Wellbore instability** – As the drill bit enters the HSZ, local heating and depressurization dissociate hydrates, weakening the cementing matrix and causing borehole collapse. 2. **Gas kick / blowout risk** – Released methane can rapidly fill the annulus if circulation is inadequate. 3. **Casing collapse** – Hydrate-bearing sediments lose mechanical strength upon dissociation. 4. **Sand production** – Sand-sized grains detach as the hydrate binder dissociates. **Mitigation Strategies:** 1. **Inhibitive drilling fluids** – Use NaCl/KCl/polymer-based muds to shift equilibrium and prevent dissociation. 2. **Managed Pressure Drilling (MPD)** – Maintains bottomhole pressure within ±0.5 MPa of formation pressure. 3. **Geomechanical modeling** – Pre-drill coupled THM (Thermo-Hydro-Mechanical) simulations; use expandable tubulars or premium liners. 4. **Sand control** – Install standalone screens, gravel packs, or frac-pack completions. 5. **Real-time monitoring** – Use PWD (Pressure-While-Drilling) and gas chromatography to detect kick indicators early. **Completion strategy:** Typically a **vertical or horizontal well with sand control and submersible pump** (for depressurization); in some cases a **dual-string completion** separates depressurization and gas lift. #### Model Solution — (b) **Step 1 — Reservoir bulk volume:** $$V_{res} = A \times h = 5 \times 10^6 \times 12 = 6.0 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 2 — Hydrate volume in place:** $$V_h = V_{res} \cdot \phi \cdot S_h = 6.0 \times 10^7 \times 0.32 \times 0.65$$ $$V_h = 1.248 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^3 \text{ of hydrate}$$ **Step 3 — Gas-in-Place (GIP):** $$GIP = V_h \times V_y = 1.248 \times 10^7 \times 165$$ $$GIP = 2.0592 \times 10^9 \text{ m}^3(\text{STP})$$ $$\boxed{GIP \approx 2.06 \text{ BCM}}$$ **Step 4 — Recoverable gas volume:** $$G_{R} = GIP \times E_R = 2.0592 \times 10^9 \times 0.45$$ $$\boxed{G_R \approx 0.927 \text{ BCM}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion logic check:** $5 \text{ km}^2 = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$. Multiplying by thickness gives bulk volume. Multiplying by porosity gives pore volume. Multiplying by hydrate saturation gives the fraction of pore space occupied by hydrate. Multiplying by volumetric yield gives STP gas volume. The final step applies recovery efficiency — a critical parameter rarely mentioned in textbooks but always tested. --- ## 5.3 Uses and Applications of Gas Hydrates | Application | Description | |---|---| | **Future energy resource** | Massive untapped methane reserve — 2× to 10× all conventional fossil fuels | | **Gas storage and transportation** | 1 m³ hydrate stores ~170 m³ STP gas — compact, safer than LNG for short distances | | **CO₂ sequestration** | Replacing $\text{CH}_4$ with $\text{CO}_2$ in the hydrate structure permanently stores carbon | | **Desalination** | Hydrate-based desalination (HBD) — salts are excluded during hydrate formation | | **Gas separation** | Fractionation of gas mixtures (e.g., $\text{CO}_2$ from flue gas) | | **Cold storage / refrigeration** | Latent heat of dissociation used in refrigeration cycles | | **Flow assurance concern** | In conventional oil/gas pipelines, hydrates form plugs — must be prevented using inhibitors | ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 4 Rapid Revision** - **Definition:** Gas hydrates = ice-like crystalline compounds of water (host) + light gases (guest). - **General formula:** $\text{M} \cdot n\text{H}_2\text{O}$; hydration number $n \in [5.67, 17]$. - **Three crystal structures:** sI, sII, sH — memorize cavity types and guest molecule sizes. - **HSZ envelope** bounded by seafloor temperature (top) and geothermal gradient (bottom). - **Equilibrium equation:** $\ln P = A - B/T$; for pure methane in seawater, $B \approx 8533.8$ K. - **Volumetric yield:** $\sim$160–180 m³(STP) CH₄ per m³ hydrate. - **Density:** $\rho_h \approx 0.91$ g/cm³ (buoyant in water). - **Global reserves:** $\sim$$10^4$ Gt C in oceans; 2–10× all fossil fuels combined. - **Field milestones:** Japan 2013 (first offshore test), China 2017 (first sustained production), India NGHP-02 (2019, 2023). - **Four extraction methods:** Depressurization, Thermal stimulation, Chemical inhibition, $\text{CO}_2$ replacement. - **Depressurization = most economical; Thermal = highest recovery but energy-intensive.** - **Drilling hazards:** Wellbore instability, gas kick, casing collapse, sand production. - **Mitigation:** Inhibitive mud (NaCl/KCl), Managed Pressure Drilling (MPD), sand screens. - **GIP formula:** $GIP = V_{res} \cdot \phi \cdot S_h \cdot V_y$ — always use this exact order. - **Recovery factor** for hydrates: typically 0.30–0.70 (much lower than conventional gas). - **Applications:** Energy, gas storage, $\text{CO}_2$ sequestration, desalination, refrigeration, flow assurance. - **Bottom-Simulating Reflector (BSR):** A key seismic indicator of hydrate presence — always mention in identification questions. - **Most-tested KTU keywords:** HSZ, BSR, MPD, sI/sII/sH, depressurization, GIP formula, Clausius–Clapeyron slope. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Tip:** Always draw a HSZ diagram in any 14-mark question related to hydrates — even a basic one. KTU examiners routinely allocate **2–3 marks** for proper sketches with labels.

topics.title: Coal and Gas Conversion to Oil: Introduction, Classification and Principles, Pyrolysis, Theoretical Aspect of Processes Involved in Conversion. Environmental and Economic Considerations of Non-Conventional Oil and Gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT752 - NON-CONVENTIONAL PETROLEUM RESOURCES > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Coal and Gas Conversion to Oil: Introduction, Classification and Principles, Pyrolysis, Theoretical Aspect of Processes Involved in Conversion. Environmental and Economic Considerations of Non-Conventional Oil and Gas # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Coal-to-Liquid (CTL)** and **Gas-to-Liquid (GTL)** are synthetic fuel (XTL) processes that convert solid coal or gaseous hydrocarbons (natural gas, syngas) into liquid hydrocarbon fuels—predominantly naphtha, diesel, kerosene, and jet fuel—via thermochemical routes. They are non-conventional petroleum routes because they bypass the conventional crude-oil reservoir pathway entirely. **Core Process Families:** - **Direct Liquefaction (DCL):** Coal is reacted with hydrogen at high pressure and temperature to yield liquid products (e.g., Bergius process, Solvent Refined Coal). - **Indirect Liquefaction (ICL):** Coal/natural gas is first gasified/reformed to synthesis gas (CO + H₂), which is then catalytically upgraded (e.g., Fischer–Tropsch synthesis, Methanol-to-Gasoline). - **Pyrolysis (Carbonization):** Thermal decomposition of coal in the absence of air, producing char, tar (pyrolysis oil), and gases. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Kitchen of Synthetic Fuels"** > Think of the process like making coffee from beans. A whole coal "bean" is too big, dirty, and solid to burn cleanly in a modern engine. So, like a barista, we **grind** (gasify) the bean into syngas (the "espresso shot"), then **brew/catalyze** (Fischer–Tropsch) it into a smooth, liquid "latte" (synthetic diesel). The chemistry kitchen uses **H₂ and CO** as universal ingredients to "rebuild" liquid hydrocarbons atom by atom, much like Lego bricks recombined into a new toy. ## 1.3 Why These Resources Matter — Strategic Metrics - **World coal reserves:** ~1,070 billion tonnes (BP Statistical Review, **2022**), enough for **>150 years** at current rates. - **H/C atomic ratio of coal:** ~0.5–1.0 (vs. petroleum: 1.5–2.0) — explains why coal needs **hydrogen addition** to become oil. - **CO₂ emissions factor:** CTL emits **~2× more CO₂ per barrel** than conventional crude refining without Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Strategic Insight:** The product of CTL/GTL is *not* an imitation—it is **chemically identical** to petroleum-derived diesel (same paraffinic isomer profile, near-zero sulfur, high cetane number). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Coal-to-Oil Processes A clear taxonomic framework expected in KTU 14-mark answers: | S.No. | Class | Sub-Process | Operating Principle | T (°C) | P (bar) | |-------|-------|-------------|--------------------|---------|---------| | 1 | **Direct Liquefaction** | Bergius / H-Coal / SRC-I & II | H-donor solvent + molecular H₂ | 400–470 | 100–700 | | 2 | **Indirect Liquefaction** | Fischer–Tropsch (FT) | Syngas → paraffins | 200–350 | 10–40 | | 3 | **Indirect Liquefaction** | Methanol-to-Gasoline (MTG) | MeOH → gasoline over ZSM-5 | 350–400 | 15–25 | | 4 | **Pyrolysis** | Slow / Flash / Hydropyrolysis | Thermal depolymerization | 500–1000 | 1–30 | | 5 | **Co-processing** | Coal-Oil Coprocessing | Coal + vacuum residue | 400–450 | 70–200 | ## 2.2 Principles of Conversion — Step-Wise Logic ### A. Direct Liquefaction (DCL) — "The Hydrogenation Highway" 1. Coal is ground to <100 µm. 2. Slurried in a **hydrogen-donor solvent** (tetralin, hydrogenated recycle oil). 3. Heated with **H₂ gas** under high P, T. 4. Free-radical fragments from coal are immediately **capped by H• radicals** (from donor solvent dehydrogenation) → liquid products. 5. **Why H-donor?** Prevents retrogressive recombination to char; the donor acts as a "radical sponge." > **Free-Radical Stabilization Reaction:** > $$\text{Coal} \xrightarrow{\Delta} R^{\bullet} + \text{donor (H-D)} \rightarrow R\text{–H} + D^{\bullet}$$ ### B. Indirect Liquefaction (Fischer–Tropsch) — "The Lego-Brick Reactor" **Three sequential stages:** 1. **Gasification (Steam reforming / partial oxidation):** $$\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad (\text{endothermic})$$ $$\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} \quad (\text{exothermic})$$ 2. **Water-Gas Shift (WGS) for H₂:CO ratio adjustment:** $$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ 3. **FT Polymerization over Co or Fe catalyst:** $$n\text{CO} + (2n+1)\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ ### C. Pyrolysis — "The Thermal Decomposition Furnace" Coal is heated **without O₂**. Bond scission produces: - **Char (solid):** ~50–70 wt% - **Tar (liquid):** ~10–25 wt% (the "primary oil") - **Gas:** ~10–30 wt% (CO, CO₂, CH₄, H₂, light HC) ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > This table contains all derivable expressions the examiner may test. | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---------|---------|-------| | 1 | $n\text{CO} + (2n+1)\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | FT paraffin synthesis (general) | mol | | 2 | $\dfrac{W_n}{n} = (1-\alpha)^2\,\alpha^{n-1}$ | Anderson–Schulz–Flory (ASF) product distribution | — | | 3 | $\alpha = \dfrac{r_p}{r_p + r_t}$ | Chain growth probability (p = propagation, t = termination) | — | | 4 | $k = A\,e^{-E_a/RT}$ | First-order pyrolysis kinetic constant (Arrhenius) | s⁻¹ | | 5 | $\text{HHV}_{\text{coal}} = 0.3536\,C + 1.5477\,H - 0.1424\,O + 0.0243\,S$ | Dulong's higher heating value (ultimate analysis, % daf) | MJ/kg | | 6 | $\dfrac{\text{H}}{\text{C}}_{\text{coal}} \approx 0.5-1.0$ | Hydrogen deficiency ratio | — | | 7 | $S_{CO_2}^{\text{CTL}} = 0.18-0.24 \,\text{t CO}_2/\text{bbl}$ | Specific CO₂ emission (no CCS) | t/bbl | | 8 | $\text{Syngas module}\,M = \dfrac{\text{H}_2}{\text{CO}} = 2.0-2.15$ | Required H₂:CO ratio for FT (Co catalyst) | — | > [!NOTE] > **Note on Symbols:** $\vert x \vert$ in a table cell would break Markdown — represented here as $\vert x \vert$ only outside the table; inside the table, magnitudes are omitted in favor of literal values. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Sasol (South Africa):** Largest commercial CTL → ~150,000 bbl/day since 1955. - **Shell Pearl GTL (Qatar):** 140,000 bbl/day from natural gas. - **Defense Fuel:** US Navy / Air Force use FT-SPK (Synthetic Paraffinic Kerosene) for jet propulsion — 50/50 blend approved under **ASTM D7566**. - **Strategic Autonomy:** India, China, Germany, and Indonesia use CTL/GTL to monetize domestic coal/gas and reduce crude-import bills. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Anderson–Schulz–Flory (ASF) Distribution The ASF equation is the **probability backbone of FT kinetics**—Kerala examiners frequently test its derivation and application. ### 3.1.1 Setting up the Probability Model Let: - $r_p$ = rate of chain propagation (monomer addition) - $r_t$ = rate of chain termination - $\alpha$ = probability of chain growth = $\dfrac{r_p}{r_p + r_t}$ Each carbon unit (C₁) added has a probability $\alpha$ of continuing and $(1-\alpha)$ of terminating. ### 3.1.2 Probability of an n-carbon chain forming For a chain of length $n$ to form, it must: - **Grow** $(n-1)$ times in succession: $\alpha^{n-1}$ - **Terminate** at the $n^{th}$ step: $(1-\alpha)$ $$P_n = (1-\alpha)\,\alpha^{n-1}$$ ### 3.1.3 Converting probability to mass fraction Mass of an n-carbon paraffin = $n \times$ (CH₂ mass unit). The weight fraction $W_n$ is: $$\frac{W_n}{n} \propto P_n \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{W_n}{n} = (1-\alpha)^2\,\alpha^{n-1}$$ The square of $(1-\alpha)$ arises because **two chain ends are potential termination points** (initiation and termination rates both contribute to total termination). $$\boxed{\,\frac{W_n}{n} = (1-\alpha)^2\,\alpha^{n-1}\,}$$ ### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Example > **Problem:** A FT reactor produces syngas with $\alpha = 0.85$. Calculate the mass fraction of gasoline-range hydrocarbons ($C_5$–$C_{11}$, lump at $n=8$). **Solution:** $$\frac{W_8}{8} = (1-0.85)^2 (0.85)^{8-1} = (0.15)^2 (0.3202) = 0.0225 \times 0.3202 = 0.00720$$ Total moles $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} P_n = 1$ implies $\sum W_n/n = 1 - \alpha$ is not dimensionless; the **mass fraction** is obtained by: $$W_n = \frac{n(1-\alpha)^2 \alpha^{n-1}}{\sum_{k} k(1-\alpha)^2\alpha^{k-1}} = \frac{n\alpha^{n-1}}{\sum_k k \alpha^{k-1}}$$ $$\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k\alpha^{k-1} = \frac{1}{(1-\alpha)^2}$$ Therefore: $$W_n = n(1-\alpha)^2 \alpha^{n-1}$$ $$W_8 = 8 \times (0.15)^2 \times (0.85)^7 = 8 \times 0.0225 \times 0.3202 = 0.0577 \approx 5.77\%$$ > **Interpretation:** Only ~5.77% mass lies at $C_8$ — confirming that FT inherently yields a **broad product slate** (wax → diesel → gasoline → gas), mandating downstream hydrocracking/isomerization. ### 3.2 Derivation: First-Order Pyrolysis Kinetics Coal devolatilization is modeled as a **first-order decay** of the residual volatile matter $V^*$: $$\frac{dV^*}{dt} = -k\,V^* \quad \text{where} \quad k = A\,e^{-E_a/RT}$$ Integrating from $V^*_0$ to $V^*$: $$\int_{V^*_0}^{V^*}\frac{dV^*}{V^*} = -\int_0^t k\,dt$$ $$\ln\frac{V^*}{V^*_0} = -k\,t$$ $$\boxed{\,V^*(t) = V^*_0\, e^{-A\,e^{-E_a/RT}\,t}\,}$$ This is the basis of the **distributed activation energy model (DAEM)** used in ASPEN/ChemCAD simulations. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operating Windows | Parameter | Pyrolysis (Slow) | Pyrolysis (Flash) | Direct Liq. (Bergius) | Indirect (FT) | |-----------|------------------|-------------------|------------------------|---------------| | **T (°C)** | 500–700 | 700–1000 | 400–470 | 200–350 | | **P (bar)** | 1 | 1–2 | 100–700 | 10–40 | | **Catalyst** | None | None | Fe / Ni-Mo (heterogeneous) | Co, Fe, Ru | | **Liquid yield (% daf)** | 10–25 | 35–45 | 40–65 (oil) | 70–85 (FT wax + diesel) | | **H₂ consumption (kg/kg oil)** | 0 | 0 | 0.05–0.08 | 0.20–0.30 | | **CO₂ emitted (kg/kg oil)** | 0.3 | 0.2 | 0.5 | 1.5–2.0 | | **TRL (maturity)** | 9 | 6 | 6 | 9 (proven) | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Flow Diagram — Indirect Coal-to-Liquid (CTL) Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Coal Feedstock
ROM Coal] --> B[Coal Preparation
Crushing Drying] B --> C[Gasifier
Lurgi Texaco Shell] C --> D[Air Separation Unit
ASU 99 percent O2] D --> C C --> E[Raw Syngas
CO H2 CO2 CH4] E --> F[Sour Shift Reactor
WGS Reaction] F --> G[Acid Gas Removal
Rectisol Selexol] G --> H[Syngas Module M 2.0 to 2.15
Adjusted Ratio] H --> I[FT Reactor
Co or Fe Catalyst] I --> J[FT Product Slate
Wax Diesel Naphtha LPG] J --> K[Hydrocracker
Wax to Diesel] K --> L[Distillation Column
Product Recovery] L --> M[Final Fuels
Diesel Jet Naphtha] C --> N[Steam Turbine
Power Generation] I --> O[Water Treatment
Recycle] O --> F ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The path from **A → C → I → M** is the canonical CTL backbone. ASU is integral to gasifier oxygen supply, and the recycle loop **I → O → F** is essential for thermal integration and water balance. ## 4.2 Block Flow — Gas-to-Liquid (GTL) Reforming Options ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Natural Gas
CH4 C2H6] --> B{Process Selection} B -->|Endothermic| C[Steam Methane Reformer
SMR 800 to 900 C] B -->|Exothermic| D[Autothermal Reformer
ATR 950 to 1050 C] B -->|Partial Oxidation| E[POX Reactor
1300 to 1500 C] C --> F[Syngas H2 CO ratio 3 to 4] D --> F E --> F F --> G[Co Catalyst FT Reactor
Low Temperature FT] F --> H[Fe Catalyst FT Reactor
High Temperature FT] G --> I[Wax Hydrocracking
300 to 350 C] H --> J[Distillation to Gasoline
Diesel Cut] I --> K[Premium Diesel
ULSD Cetane gt 70] J --> L[Gasoline Naphtha
RON 90 plus] ``` ## 4.3 Pyrolysis Reaction Pathway — Free-Radical Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Coal Macromolecule
Aromatic Clusters] -->|Heat 400 to 500 C| B[Bridge Scission
CH2 O S bridges break] B --> C[Free Radical Fragments
R1 R2 R3] C --> D{Stabilization Pathway} D -->|H abstraction from donor| E1[Stabilized Tar
Primary Oil] D -->|Radical recombination| E2[Char / Coke
Retrogressive] D -->|Beta scission + H2 loss| E3[Light Gases
CH4 C2H4 CO] C --> F[Volatile Recombination
Secondary Reactions] F --> E1 E1 --> G[Condensation
Cooling to 25 C] G --> H[Pyrolysis Tar
Heavy Oil Fraction] ``` > **Engineering takeaway:** The **competition** between E1 (desired) and E2 (retrograde) determines yield. H-donor solvents, rapid heating rates (flash pyrolysis), and short residence times all **suppress E2**, favouring E1. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **Define the Fischer–Tropsch synthesis reaction and write the generalized equation for paraffin formation.** **Model Answer:** Fischer–Tropsch (FT) is the catalytic hydrogenation of carbon monoxide into liquid hydrocarbons, typically over Co, Fe, or Ru catalysts at 200–350 °C and 10–40 bar. $$\text{Generalized paraffin formation:}\quad n\text{CO} + (2n+1)\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Co/Fe, 200-350 °C}} \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ The reaction is **exothermic** ($\Delta H \approx -165\,\text{kJ/mol CO}$), necessitating efficient heat removal in slurry/reactors. *(3 marks: 1 — definition; 1 — equation; 1 — operating conditions)* ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **Differentiate between direct and indirect coal liquefaction with one example each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Direct Liquefaction (DCL) | Indirect Liquefaction (ICL) | |--------|---------------------------|----------------------------| | Intermediate | None — coal reacts directly with H₂ | Syngas (CO + H₂) is the intermediate | | Conditions | 400–470 °C, 100–700 bar | 200–350 °C, 10–40 bar | | H-donor solvent | Required (tetralin) | Not required | | Example | Bergius process, H-Coal | Fischer–Tropsch, MTG | | H₂ consumption | Lower (0.05–0.08 kg/kg oil) | Higher (0.2–0.3 kg/kg oil) | *(3 marks: 1 — distinction table; 1 — example; 1 — operating parameter contrast)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - Students **omit the syngas module** $M = H_2/CO$ and the role of the **Water-Gas Shift** — losing ~2 marks. > - Confusion between **ASF chain growth probability ($\alpha$) and selectivity** — memorize the *square* in $(1-\alpha)^2$. > - In environmental questions, students forget to discuss **CCS (Carbon Capture & Storage)** as a mitigation lever — Examiner explicitly tests this per NEP 2020 sustainability outcomes. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]` **a)** Explain the **classification, principles, and chemistry** of converting coal into liquid fuels via the **Fischer–Tropsch route**. Include the role of syngas, the ASF distribution, and operating conditions. **(7 marks)** **b)** Discuss the **environmental impacts** of CTL processes with quantitative CO₂ metrics, and explain how Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) can reduce the carbon footprint. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Three-Stage Classification:** FT-based CTL is classified as **Indirect Liquefaction (ICL)** because it uses a gaseous intermediate (syngas). The three stages are: 1. **Gasification** of coal to raw syngas. 2. **Conditioning** (WGS + AGR) to reach the optimal **syngas module** $M = H_2/CO = 2.0–2.15$. 3. **FT Polymerization** over Co or Fe catalyst. **Step 2 — Gasification Chemistry (Steam + O₂):** $$2C + O_2 + 4H_2O \rightarrow 2CO_2 + 4H_2 \quad \text{(overall)}$$ The more useful individual reactions: $$\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +131\,\text{kJ/mol}$$ $$\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} \quad \Delta H = -111\,\text{kJ/mol}$$ $$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41\,\text{kJ/mol}$$ **Step 3 — WGS for H₂:CO ratio adjustment:** To achieve $M = 2.0$, WGS is exploited. For Co-catalyzed FT, **M = 2.15** is optimal; for Fe, M = 1.0–1.5 with internal WGS activity. **Step 4 — FT Reaction:** $$n\text{CO} + (2n+1)\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ Operating conditions: **T = 200–250 °C (Co, low-T FT)** or **300–350 °C (Fe, high-T FT)**; **P = 10–40 bar**. **Step 5 — ASF Product Distribution:** $$\frac{W_n}{n} = (1-\alpha)^2\alpha^{n-1}$$ - $\alpha \approx 0.85–0.95$ (Co) → wax-dominant (high diesel yield after hydrocracking). - $\alpha \approx 0.60–0.70$ (Fe, HTFT) → gasoline-dominant. **Step 6 — Catalyst Selection:** - **Co:** Higher activity, longer life, no WGS activity → suited for wax → diesel. - **Fe:** Cheap, internal WGS, suits CO-rich syngas → gasoline. - **Ru:** Most active, but prohibitive cost; used in academic research. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Quantitative CO₂ Emissions:** Without CCS, CTL emits: $$S_{CO_2} \approx 0.18-0.24 \text{ t CO}_2/\text{bbl of liquid fuel}$$ This is **~2× higher** than conventional crude (0.10–0.12 t CO₂/bbl). For a 100,000 bbl/day plant, this equates to ~7.3–8.8 Mt CO₂/year — comparable to a medium-sized power plant. **Step 2 — Source-wise CO₂ Allocation:** | Source | % of Total CO₂ | |--------|----------------| | Gasification block (C → CO) | ~60% | | Power island (heat + electricity) | ~25% | | Auxiliary (H₂, utilities) | ~15% | **Step 3 — Environmental Impacts Beyond CO₂:** - **Water consumption:** 5–10 m³/bbl (3–5× conventional refining). - **Solid waste:** Slag, fly ash with trace Hg, As, Se. - **Liquid effluents:** Phenols, PAHs, NH₃ — require biotreatment. - **Air emissions:** SO₂, NOₓ, particulate matter; mitigated by AGR + SCR. **Step 4 — CCS Integration:** Post-combustion CCS (amine scrubbing) or pre-combustion (Shift + Rectisol + CO₂ compression to 80–100 bar) can capture **~85–95% of CO₂**. Storage options: - **EOR (Enhanced Oil Recovery):** Economic co-benefit. - **Saline aquifers:** Caprock-confined, >800 m depth. - **Depleted gas reservoirs.** **Step 5 — Footprint Reduction:** With CCS, CTL specific emissions drop to **0.03–0.05 t CO₂/bbl**, comparable to bio-derived fuels. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice:** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]` **a)** Describe in detail the **principles, mechanisms, and kinetics** of coal **pyrolysis**. Discuss slow, fast, and flash pyrolysis with product yields. **(7 marks)** **b)** Critically evaluate the **economic viability** of CTL/GTL processes versus conventional petroleum refining, including CAPEX, OPEX, and break-even crude price. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Definition:** Pyrolysis is the **thermal decomposition of organic matter in the absence of air/oxygen**. For coal, it produces three phases: **char (solid), tar (condensable liquid), and gas (non-condensable).** **Step 2 — Free-Radical Mechanism:** 1. **Initiation (400–500 °C):** Bridge bonds (–CH₂–, –O–, –S–) cleave. $$\text{Coal macromolecule} \xrightarrow{\Delta} R_1^{\bullet} + R_2^{\bullet}$$ 2. **Propagation:** Radicals abstract H or undergo β-scission. $$R^{\bullet} + \text{Coal} \rightarrow R\text{–H} + R'^{\bullet}$$ 3. **Termination:** Radical recombination, H-abstraction, or disproportionation. $$R_1^{\bullet} + R_2^{\bullet} \rightarrow R_1\text{–}R_2 \quad \text{(coke)}$$ **Step 3 — Kinetics (First-Order):** $$\frac{dV^*}{dt} = -A\,e^{-E_a/RT}\,V^* \implies V^*(t) = V^*_0\,e^{-k t}$$ Typical values: **Eₐ = 200–250 kJ/mol; A = 10¹³–10¹⁵ s⁻¹.** **Step 4 — Classification & Product Distribution:** | Process | Heating Rate (K/s) | T (°C) | Tar (wt%) | Char (wt%) | Gas (wt%) | |---------|--------------------|--------|-----------|------------|-----------| | **Slow** | 1–10 | 500–700 | 10–25 | 70–80 | 10–20 | | **Fast** | 100–1000 | 700–900 | 35–45 | 50–60 | 15–20 | | **Flash** | >1000 | 800–1000 | 40–55 | 35–50 | 15–25 | | **Hydropyrolysis** | 1000 (with H₂) | 700–900 | 50–65 | 30–40 | 10–15 | **Step 5 — Engineering Significance:** - **Secondary reactions** (in vapour phase) reduce tar yield; suppressed by **short residence times** (~1 s) and **rapid quench**. - **Hydropyrolysis** uses H₂ pressure (up to 30 bar) to cap radicals and prevent retrograde char. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — CAPEX Comparison (USD per bbl/day capacity):** | Process | CAPEX (US$/bbl/day) | |---------|---------------------| | Conventional refinery | 18,000 – 25,000 | | CTL (Sasol-class) | 80,000 – 120,000 | | GTL (Sasol / Shell) | 35,000 – 55,000 | CTL is **~4–5× more capital-intensive** than conventional refining. **Step 2 — OPEX Comparison (USD per barrel of product):** | Process | OPEX (US$/bbl) | |---------|----------------| | Conventional refining | 4 – 7 | | CTL | 35 – 50 | | GTL | 25 – 40 | CTL OPEX is dominated by **coal cost + oxygen/ASU + utilities**. **Step 3 — Break-Even Crude Price:** For a CTL plant at 12% IRR over 25 years: $$P_{\text{break-even}} \approx 1.4 - 1.8 \times P_{\text{crude, equivalent barrel}}$$ In practice, CTL is **economically viable** when: $$P_{\text{crude}} > \text{US\$ 80/bbl} \quad \text{(rule of thumb)}$$ **Step 4 — Sensitivity Analysis:** CTL economics are most sensitive to: - **Coal price** (low rank coal preferred: lignite). - **Plant scale** (≥50,000 bbl/day for economy of scale). - **Co-product credits:** Sulfur, ammonia, phenols, electricity (cogeneration). - **Carbon credits:** With US$50/t CO₂, GTL+CCS breaks even at lower crude prices. **Step 5 — Strategic Considerations:** - **Energy security:** Reduces import dependence (India, China, Germany). - **Carbon penalty:** Without CCS, CTL attracts a **carbon border tax** (e.g., EU CBAM). - **Technology risk:** Proven at scale in **South Africa (Sasol)** and **Qatar (Shell, ORYX)**; smaller deployments remain high-risk. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Points (must-memorize for KTU):** - ✅ **Three CTL routes:** Direct (Bergius), Indirect (FT/MTG), Pyrolysis. - ✅ **FT generalized reaction:** $n\text{CO} + (2n+1)\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\text{H}_2\text{O}$. - ✅ **ASF equation:** $W_n/n = (1-\alpha)^2\alpha^{n-1}$ — note the **square**. - ✅ **Syngas module:** $M = H_2/CO = 2.0–2.15$ for Co; ~1.0 for Fe. - ✅ **H/C atomic ratio of coal:** 0.5–1.0 (deficient vs. petroleum's 1.5–2.0). - ✅ **Pyrolysis product split:** Char (~50–70%), Tar (~10–25%), Gas (~10–30%). - ✅ **Pyrolysis kinetics:** First-order Arrhenius, $E_a \approx 200–250\,\text{kJ/mol}$. - ✅ **CTL CO₂ emissions:** 0.18–0.24 t/bbl (without CCS); ~0.04 t/bbl (with CCS). - ✅ **CTL CAPEX:** ~80,000–120,000 US$/bbl/day (4–5× conventional). - ✅ **GTL pioneers:** Sasol (South Africa), Shell Pearl (Qatar). - ✅ **Pyrolysis H-donor role:** Prevents retrogressive char; tetralin is the classical donor. - ✅ **Catalyst choice:** Co (wax/diesel), Fe (gasoline), Ru (research-grade). - ✅ **WGS Reaction:** $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$, $\Delta H = -41\,\text{kJ/mol}$. - ✅ **Bergius conditions:** 400–470 °C, 100–700 bar H₂, with H-donor solvent. - ✅ **FT operating windows:** Low-T FT (200–250 °C, Co, wax), High-T FT (300–350 °C, Fe, gasoline). - ✅ **Environmental triad:** CO₂, water consumption, trace metals (Hg, As) in slag/ash. > **Examiner's One-Liner Mnemonic:** *"Coal and gas are too rich in C and too poor in H. CTL/GTL uses H-donor solvent or syngas chemistry to **add hydrogen** and **rebuild** the liquid hydrocarbon chain — at a cost."*
